1) Where the UI shows masked values (e.g. card ****1234, SSN ***-**-6789), check the underlying API response - masking must happen server-side, not just in CSS/JS:
   Intercept in Burp: GET /api/account -> does the JSON contain the FULL card/SSN while the UI hides it?
2) Inspect exports (CSV/PDF), print views, and error/log messages for unmasked values.
3) Test role differences: does a support/agent view leak full PII that should be masked for that role?
4) Check that masked fields cannot be un-masked via an alternate endpoint or parameter (e.g. ?full=true).
items.how_to_fix: Mask sensitive data server-side before it leaves the backend (return only last-4 etc.) and gate full values behind explicit authorization + audit logging. Never rely on client-side masking. Apply masking consistently to APIs, exports, print views, and logs. VERIFY by intercepting the API responses (masked values only, unless authorized).

items.title: Sensitive Data in URLs
items.description: Check that sensitive data is not included in URLs or referrer headers.
items.how_to_find:
1) Look for secrets in query strings across the Burp proxy history:
   Search for token=, session=, api_key=, password=, reset=, email=, jwt in GET URLs.
   Example smell: GET /reset?token=abc123  or  GET /login?user=x&pass=y
   URLs leak into: server/proxy access logs, browser history, bookmarks, and the Referer header sent to third parties.
2) Confirm Referer leakage: on a page reached via such a URL, check outbound requests (analytics/CDN) for a Referer carrying the token.
3) Check password reset / magic-link / OAuth flows specifically for tokens placed in the URL.
items.how_to_fix: Keep sensitive data out of URLs - send tokens/credentials in the POST body or headers (Authorization). Use one-time, short-lived tokens for reset/magic links and invalidate on use. Set Referrer-Policy: no-referrer / strict-origin-when-cross-origin to limit Referer leakage. Scrub sensitive query params from logs. VERIFY by re-checking the proxy history and Referer headers (no secrets in URLs).

items.title: Secure Data Handling
items.description: Ensure secure handling of sensitive data throughout its lifecycle.
items.how_to_find:
Trace sensitive data across its full lifecycle:
1) COLLECTION: is only necessary data collected? Is it received over HTTPS?
2) PROCESSING: does it appear in logs, analytics, error messages, or third-party calls? Check server logs and outbound requests in Burp.
3) STORAGE: encrypted at rest? correct access controls? (see Encryption at Rest checks)
4) TRANSMISSION: encrypted to every downstream service/partner?
5) RETENTION/DELETION: does 'delete account' actually purge data (and backups/caches)? Test GDPR/deletion flows and check the record is gone via API/DB.
6) CACHING: are authenticated/sensitive responses cached by the browser/CDN (Cache-Control)?
items.how_to_fix: Apply data minimization (collect/retain only what's needed), encrypt in transit and at rest at every hop, restrict access by role with audit logging, and set Cache-Control: no-store on sensitive responses. Implement retention limits and real deletion (including backups/caches). Vet third-party processors. VERIFY by tracing a sample record end-to-end and confirming deletion purges it.

items.title: Encryption Key Management
items.description: Verify secure management and storage of encryption keys.
items.how_to_find:
Mostly code/config/infra review:
1) Hunt for hardcoded keys/secrets in code, repos, and client bundles:
   git log -p | grep -iE 'AKIA|BEGIN (RSA|EC|PRIVATE) KEY|secret|api[_-]?key'
   trufflehog git file://. ;  gitleaks detect --source .
   grep the JS bundle for keys served to the browser.
2) Check where keys live: in env vars/config files on disk (weak) vs a KMS/HSM/Vault (good).
3) Look for a single static key never rotated, keys shared across environments, or keys in backups/logs.
4) Check who can read the key (file perms, IAM policy) - least privilege?
items.how_to_fix: Store keys in a dedicated KMS/HSM or secrets manager (AWS KMS, HashiCorp Vault); never hardcode in source, images, or client code. Enforce least-privilege access, separate keys per environment, enable rotation, and audit key usage. Purge any leaked keys from git history and rotate them. VERIFY with trufflehog/gitleaks (no secrets) and confirm keys resolve from the vault at runtime.

items.title: Tokenization
items.description: Ensure that tokenization is used where applicable for sensitive data.
items.how_to_find:
For high-value data like payment cards (PCI DSS), check whether raw values are stored/handled or replaced by tokens:
1) Inspect DB/API for real PANs vs format-preserving tokens; a stored full PAN outside the payment processor is a red flag.
2) Confirm the app forwards card data directly to a tokenization/payment provider (e.g. Stripe Elements) rather than touching raw PANs on its own servers.
3) Check the token is not reversible client-side and that de-tokenization is restricted to the vault/provider.
4) Review logs/analytics for raw sensitive values that should have been tokenized.
items.how_to_fix: Tokenize high-value sensitive data (cards, SSNs) via a dedicated vault/provider so systems store non-reversible tokens instead of raw values; keep the real data only in the vault with strict access. Use provider-hosted fields to keep raw PANs off your servers (reduces PCI scope). Ensure de-tokenization is tightly access-controlled and audited. VERIFY that no raw sensitive values appear in your DB/logs.

items.title: CSRF Vulnerabilities
items.description: Test for CSRF vulnerabilities and ensure the use of anti-CSRF tokens.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find a state-changing request (change email, transfer, update profile) and check for an anti-CSRF token.
2) In Burp Repeater, remove the CSRF token param/header entirely and resend - if it still succeeds, no CSRF protection.
3) Token validation bypasses to try:
   - Use another user's / an old token (token not tied to the session).
   - Empty token value or delete the parameter.
   - Change method POST -> GET.
   - Swap Content-Type to text/plain or application/x-www-form-urlencoded to dodge a JSON-only check.
4) Build a PoC (Burp: right-click request -> Engagement tools -> Generate CSRF PoC) and load it from a different origin while logged in:
   <form action=https://target.tld/account/email method=POST><input name=email value=attacker@evil.tld></form><script>document.forms[0].submit()</script>
5) Confirm SameSite: if the session cookie is SameSite=Lax/Strict, classic cross-site POST CSRF is largely mitigated - test GET-based and top-level navigation vectors.
items.how_to_fix: Use per-session (or per-request) synchronizer CSRF tokens validated server-side on every state-changing route, AND set session cookies to SameSite=Lax/Strict as defense in depth. For APIs, require a custom header (e.g. X-Requested-With) or a token that simple cross-site forms cannot set. Reject cross-origin requests via Origin/Referer checks on sensitive actions. VERIFY: the generated CSRF PoC and token-removal/replay tests are all rejected.

items.title: State-Changing Operations
items.description: Validate that the application does not perform state-changing operations without proper authorization.
items.how_to_find: 1) Enumerate all state-changing actions (create/update/delete, transfer, role change, settings) - these are the CSRF-relevant targets. 2) Check that each uses a safe method (POST/PUT/DELETE) - a GET that changes state (GET /delete?id=5) is trivially CSRF-able and cacheable: 3) Confirm each requires proper authorization AND anti-CSRF protection, not just a logged-in session. 4) Test whether the action can be triggered cross-site (see the CSRF PoC checks).
items.how_to_fix: Never perform state changes on GET; use POST/PUT/DELETE with anti-CSRF tokens and SameSite cookies. Enforce authorization server-side on every state-changing endpoint. Make GET requests side-effect free (safe/idempotent). VERIFY by attempting each action cross-site and via GET (should fail).

items.title: SameSite Cookies
items.description: Ensure cookies are set with the SameSite attribute to prevent CSRF attacks.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inspect the session cookie's SameSite attribute:
   curl -sSI https://target.tld/login | grep -i set-cookie
   DevTools > Application > Cookies (SameSite column).
2) Interpret:
   - SameSite=Strict: strongest (cookie never sent cross-site) - can break some legit navigations.
   - SameSite=Lax: sent on top-level GET navigations only - blocks cross-site POST CSRF.
   - SameSite=None: MUST also be Secure; cookie sent cross-site (needs other CSRF defenses).
   - Attribute absent: browsers default to Lax, but older browsers = None (risky).
3) Confirm the flag is present on the real auth cookie (not just a tracking cookie) and that it isn't downgraded on some responses.
items.how_to_fix: Set SameSite=Lax (or Strict for the most sensitive apps) plus Secure and HttpOnly on session cookies. Only use SameSite=None with Secure for genuine cross-site needs, and pair it with synchronizer tokens. Treat SameSite as defense-in-depth, not the sole CSRF control. VERIFY with curl -I that the auth cookie carries SameSite + Secure + HttpOnly.

items.title: Referer Header
items.description: Verify the use of the Referer header to help prevent CSRF attacks.
items.how_to_find:
If the app relies on Origin/Referer validation for CSRF defense, test its robustness in Burp Repeater on a state-changing request:
1) Remove the Referer header entirely - does it still succeed? (many apps 'fail open' when Referer is absent).
2) Set Referer/Origin to an attacker value: Referer: https://evil.tld - accepted?
3) Try a prefix/substring bypass: Referer: https://target.tld.evil.tld or https://evil.tld/target.tld (naive 'contains' checks).
4) Try Origin: null (sandboxed iframe / data: URL) which some allow-lists permit.
If any bypass works, the Referer/Origin check is ineffective.
items.how_to_fix: Don't rely on Referer/Origin alone - use synchronizer tokens as the primary defense. If used as defense-in-depth, validate the Origin header with a strict allow-list (exact host match, not substring), and fail CLOSED when Origin/Referer is missing on sensitive actions. VERIFY by replaying the missing/forged Referer and null-origin tests (all rejected).

items.title: Double Submit Cookie
items.description: Check for the implementation of the double submit cookie pattern as an additional CSRF mitigation.
items.how_to_find:
Double-submit pattern = the CSRF token is sent both as a cookie and as a request param/header, and the server checks they match. Test its weaknesses:
1) Set both the cookie value and the request field to the SAME arbitrary value (e.g. 'csrf=aaaa' in both) - if it passes, the server only checks equality, not authenticity, so an attacker who can set a cookie (subdomain, HTTP injection) can forge requests.
2) Check if the CSRF cookie is HttpOnly (if the JS must read it, it can't be HttpOnly - acceptable, but confirm it's not the session cookie).
3) Test subdomain cookie-setting: a vulnerable/attacker-controlled subdomain can write the parent-domain CSRF cookie, defeating the pattern.
items.how_to_fix: Prefer the signed/HMAC double-submit variant (token bound to the session via HMAC) or synchronizer tokens over naive double-submit. Ensure the CSRF cookie can't be overwritten by subdomains (scope carefully, use __Host- prefix). Combine with SameSite. VERIFY that arbitrary matching values and subdomain-set cookies are rejected.

items.title: Custom Headers
items.description: Ensure that custom headers are required for state-changing requests to prevent CSRF.
items.how_to_find:
Many APIs rely on a custom header (e.g. X-Requested-With: XMLHttpRequest or X-CSRF-Token) that simple cross-site HTML forms cannot set (they'd trigger a CORS preflight). Test:
1) Remove the custom header in Burp Repeater on a state-changing request - still succeeds? Then the header isn't actually enforced.
2) Confirm the endpoint requires a non-simple content type (application/json) or the custom header, so <form> CSRF can't reach it.
3) Check CORS config isn't so permissive (Access-Control-Allow-Origin reflected + Allow-Headers *) that an attacker origin could send the header via fetch with credentials.
items.how_to_fix: Require a custom header (X-Requested-With or an anti-CSRF header) and/or JSON content type on state-changing API endpoints, and enforce it server-side (reject if absent). Keep CORS strict (no wildcard with credentials) so attacker origins can't add the header. Pair with tokens for browser-form endpoints. VERIFY by replaying without the header (rejected).

items.title: Form Token Validation
items.description: Verify that form tokens are used and validated for all state-changing requests.
items.how_to_find:
For each form/state-change, verify the token is truly validated:
1) Submit with the token removed / empty - accepted? (validation missing).
2) Submit with a random/garbage token - accepted? (token not checked).
3) Reuse a token from another user or a previous session - accepted? (not bound to session).
4) Reuse a token after it should have expired or after logout.
5) Check the token is present on ALL state-changing forms (attackers target the one that forgot it).
6) Confirm the token isn't leaked in the URL/Referer (would let an attacker read it).
items.how_to_fix: Generate CSRF tokens with a CSPRNG, bind them to the user session, validate on the server for every state-changing request, and reject missing/invalid/foreign tokens. Rotate on login/privilege change and don't place tokens in URLs. Apply centrally so no form is missed. VERIFY by replaying removal/garbage/foreign-token tests (all rejected).

items.title: Secure Token Storage
items.description: Ensure anti-CSRF tokens are stored securely and not exposed to attackers.
items.how_to_find:
1) Check where the CSRF token lives: in a hidden form field / meta tag (fine) vs leaked in the URL or logged.
2) Confirm the token is NOT exposed to unrelated origins - e.g. reflected in a JSONP/CORS response an attacker can read, or readable via an XSS (any XSS defeats CSRF tokens - test XSS too).
3) If stored in a cookie for double-submit, verify scope/prefix (__Host-) so it can't be read/overwritten cross-subdomain.
4) Ensure the token isn't cached in a shared cache/CDN and served to other users.
items.how_to_fix: Deliver CSRF tokens in the response body/hidden field or a non-HttpOnly cookie scoped with __Host-; never in URLs, logs, or CORS/JSONP responses readable cross-origin. Set Cache-Control: no-store on pages carrying tokens. Fix any XSS (it nullifies CSRF defenses). VERIFY the token isn't retrievable cross-origin or from logs/cache.

items.title: Unrestricted File Upload and Malware Injection
items.description: Assess file upload functionality for vulnerabilities like unrestricted file upload and malware injection.
items.how_to_find:
Goal: upload an executable file (web shell) and get the server to run it.
1) Upload a simple PHP shell and note the returned path:
   echo '<?php system($_GET[c]); ?>' > shell.php
   then browse /uploads/shell.php?c=id
2) If .php is blocked, try extension bypasses:
   - Alternate extensions: .phtml .php3 .php5 .phar .pht (PHP), .asp .aspx .cshtml, .jsp .jspx
   - Double extension: shell.php.jpg / shell.jpg.php
   - Case: shell.PhP ; trailing chars: shell.php%00.jpg (null byte), shell.php.  / shell.php:: (Windows)
   - .htaccess upload to make the server treat images as PHP: 'AddType application/x-httpd-php .jpg'
3) Content-Type / magic-byte bypass: set Content-Type: image/png and prepend GIF89a; to the shell so it passes MIME/signature checks.
4) SVG/HTML uploads for stored XSS: <svg onload=alert(document.domain)>.
5) Polyglot: a valid image that also contains PHP (see fuzzdb/Upload payloads). Confirm exec by hitting the file URL.
items.how_to_fix: Validate uploads with an allow-list of extensions AND real content type (magic bytes), re-encode/normalize images, and generate a random server-side filename with the correct extension. Store uploads OUTSIDE the web root (or in object storage) and serve via a handler that never executes them; disable script execution in the upload dir (nginx: don't pass to PHP-FPM; Apache: php_admin_flag engine off / RemoveHandler). Set Content-Disposition: attachment and X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff. Scan for malware. VERIFY the uploaded shell/SVG cannot be executed or rendered.

items.title: File Type Validation and Storage
items.description: Check for proper file type validation and storage.
items.how_to_find:
1) Determine how type is validated: client-side JS only (bypass by sending the request in Burp), Content-Type header, file extension, or magic bytes.
2) Client-side only: intercept and change the filename/type after the JS 'passes' it.
3) Extension allow-list vs block-list: block-lists miss variants (.phtml, .phar) - try them.
4) Magic-byte check only: append your payload after valid magic bytes (GIF89a; + PHP).
5) Extension/content mismatch: upload shell.php with Content-Type: image/jpeg, or image.jpg containing HTML/JS.
6) Check where it's stored and whether the stored name/path/extension is attacker-controlled.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce a strict allow-list of both extension and verified content type (magic bytes) server-side; reject mismatches. Re-encode images and assign server-generated names. Don't trust the client Content-Type or original filename. Store outside the web root with execution disabled. VERIFY by replaying extension/MIME/magic-byte bypasses (all rejected).

items.title: File Execution
items.description: Ensure that uploaded files cannot be executed on the server.
items.how_to_find:
Even if only 'images' upload, confirm the storage location can't execute code:
1) Upload a benign server-side script named as an image variant (shell.php.jpg, .phtml) and request it directly - does the interpreter run it?
2) Upload .htaccess / web.config to re-enable handlers in the upload directory, then upload the shell.
3) If files go to a path you can browse (/uploads/), test each dangerous extension for execution and check the response (code output vs raw source).
4) Test path confusion: does requesting /uploads/shell.php%00.jpg or /uploads/shell.php/.jpg execute the PHP?
Confirm RCE only with a benign command (id / whoami) or an OOB callback.
items.how_to_fix: Store uploads outside the web root or in object storage and serve through a download handler that sets Content-Disposition: attachment and never invokes an interpreter. Disable script execution in the upload directory at the web-server level and forbid uploading .htaccess/web.config. Assign random names + safe extensions. VERIFY that no uploaded file can be executed.

items.title: Content-Type Verification
items.description: Verify that the content type of uploaded files matches the expected type.
items.how_to_find:
1) In Burp, capture the multipart upload and edit the part's Content-Type header (e.g. change application/x-php or text/html to image/png) while keeping malicious content - if accepted, the server trusts the client-declared type.
2) Conversely, keep Content-Type: image/png but put script content in the body.
3) Confirm the server verifies the ACTUAL content (magic bytes / file command) and not just the header:
   file shell.php.png   (should reveal PHP, not PNG)
4) Check that the response Content-Type when the file is later served can't be sniffed into executing (missing X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff).
items.how_to_fix: Verify the true file type server-side via magic bytes/content inspection, not the client Content-Type header; reject when declared vs actual type mismatch. When serving files, set the correct Content-Type plus X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff. VERIFY by uploading a mismatched Content-Type file (rejected).

items.title: File Size Limits
items.description: Ensure file size limits are enforced to prevent denial of service attacks.
items.how_to_find:
1) Upload a very large file and see if it's accepted / processed (DoS via disk/memory exhaustion):
   head -c 500M /dev/urandom > big.bin  then upload big.bin
2) Test many concurrent large uploads (fill disk / exhaust workers).
3) Test decompression bombs where archives/images are processed (a small zip/PNG that expands to gigabytes).
4) Confirm the limit is enforced server-side, not just by client JS (send oversized in Burp) - expect 413 Payload Too Large.
5) Check limits on both the web server (nginx client_max_body_size) and app layer.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce a maximum upload size at BOTH the web server (e.g. nginx client_max_body_size, PHP upload_max_filesize/post_max_size) and the application, rejecting oversized uploads early with 413. Guard against decompression bombs (limit expanded size), limit concurrent uploads per user, and store on a quota'd volume. VERIFY by sending oversized/concurrent uploads (rejected without exhausting resources).

items.title: Virus Scanning
items.description: Verify that uploaded files are scanned for viruses and malware.
items.how_to_find:
1) Upload the harmless EICAR antivirus test string as a file and see if it's accepted and stored/served:
   printf 'X5O!P%%@AP[4\\PZX54(P^)7CC)7}$EICAR-STANDARD-ANTIVIRUS-TEST-FILE!$H+H*' > eicar.com
   If it passes and can be downloaded by others, there is no AV scanning.
2) Upload a known-malicious-format doc (macro-enabled) to a system that shares files with users.
3) Check whether scanning happens before the file becomes accessible (race: is it served during the scan window?).
items.how_to_fix: Scan all uploads with an up-to-date AV/malware engine (e.g. ClamAV) and quarantine until the scan passes - don't serve files during scanning. For documents, consider content disarm & reconstruction (CDR) and macro stripping. Keep signatures current. VERIFY the EICAR upload is detected/blocked.

items.title: Temporary Storage
items.description: Check the security of temporary storage locations for uploaded files.
items.how_to_find:
1) Determine where files land before processing (temp dir) and whether that dir is web-accessible or predictable (e.g. /tmp/uploads/, /var/www/tmp/).
2) Test a race window: upload a shell, then immediately request its temp path repeatedly before validation/move/delete completes - if reachable and executed, it's exploitable (classic 'upload race').
3) Check temp files are cleaned up and not left world-readable, and that temp names aren't guessable.
4) Look for temp/processing artifacts left in the web root.
items.how_to_fix: Use OS-secure temp files (random names, 600 perms, non-web-accessible dir), process/validate atomically, and delete temp files promptly. Never place temp uploads under the web root. Avoid TOCTOU by validating the moved final file, not the temp one. VERIFY the temp path is not reachable/executable during the upload window.

items.title: File Path Manipulation
items.description: Ensure that file path manipulation is not possible through file uploads.
items.how_to_find:
If the upload lets you influence the destination path/filename, test path traversal to write outside the intended dir:
1) Set the multipart filename to traversal sequences:
   filename="../../../../var/www/html/shell.php"
   filename="..%2f..%2f..%2fshell.php"  (URL-encoded)
   Windows: filename="..\\..\\inetpub\\wwwroot\\shell.aspx"
2) Test any 'folder'/'path'/'name' parameter the client sends alongside the file.
3) Zip Slip: upload an archive whose entries contain ../ so extraction writes outside the target (overwrite config or drop a shell).
Confirm by locating the file where it shouldn't be (e.g. web root) or overwriting a known file.
items.how_to_fix: Ignore the client-supplied filename/path entirely - generate the storage path and name server-side. Canonicalize and confine any path to a fixed base directory (reject ../ after decoding). Validate archive entry paths before extraction (defeat Zip Slip). VERIFY traversal filenames land only in the intended directory.

items.title: Storage Location
items.description: Ensure uploaded files are stored in secure locations with appropriate access controls.
items.how_to_find:
1) Determine where uploads are served from and whether files are directly, publicly reachable by URL without authorization (IDOR on files):
   Upload a private file as user A, then request its URL as user B / anonymously.
2) Check for predictable/sequential file URLs (/uploads/1001.pdf) enabling enumeration.
3) If stored in cloud (S3/GCS), test for public bucket ACLs / listable buckets.
4) Confirm sensitive uploads (IDs, contracts) require authorization to download, not just a hard-to-guess URL.
items.how_to_fix: Store uploads with access control: serve private files through an authenticated handler that checks ownership/permission (not a direct public path), use random unguessable identifiers, and lock down cloud buckets (block public access, per-object auth/signed URLs with short expiry). VERIFY cross-user/anonymous access to private files is denied.

items.title: File Integrity
items.description: Verify that file integrity checks are performed on uploaded files.
items.how_to_find:
1) If the client sends a hash/checksum with the upload, tamper the file while keeping the old hash (or recompute) to see if integrity is actually verified server-side.
2) Check whether uploaded files can be modified in place after validation (validate-then-store race, or writable storage).
3) For updates/patches/firmware uploads, test unsigned or wrongly-signed artifacts are accepted (supply-chain risk).
4) Confirm the served file matches what was validated (no post-validation swap).
items.how_to_fix: Compute and verify integrity (hash, and signatures for update/firmware artifacts) server-side; reject mismatches. Store files immutably or re-verify on serve, and require signed artifacts for anything executed/installed. VERIFY by submitting tampered/unsigned files (rejected).

items.title: Sanitization of File Names
items.description: Ensure that uploaded file names are sanitized to prevent directory traversal attacks.
items.how_to_find: Set the multipart filename to hostile values and see how it's stored/reflected: 1) Traversal: ../../../etc/passwd, ..\\..\\file (see File Path Manipulation). 2) Special chars/spaces/null: 'my file;.php', 'a\u0000.jpg', very long names (255+), reserved Windows names (CON, NUL, PRN). 3) XSS via filename: name the file '.jpg' and check if the filename is rendered unescaped in a listing/admin panel (stored XSS). 4) Overwrite: name it the same as an existing file to test clobbering. 5) Double extension / RTL override (\u202E) to disguise the real extension.
items.how_to_fix: Don't use the client filename for storage - generate a safe random name server-side and store the original name only as escaped metadata. If you must keep it, strip path separators/control chars, enforce a length cap, normalize unicode, and HTML-encode it wherever displayed. VERIFY hostile filenames are neutralized in storage and UI.

items.title: Content Security Policy (CSP)
items.description: Verify the implementation of Content Security Policy (CSP).
items.how_to_find:
1) Read the policy:
   curl -sSI https://target.tld/ | grep -i content-security-policy
2) Paste it into Google CSP Evaluator (csp-evaluator.withgoogle.com) to grade it.
3) Common weaknesses that make CSP useless against XSS:
   - 'unsafe-inline' in script-src (inline scripts allowed).
   - 'unsafe-eval'.
   - Wildcards (script-src *) or overly broad hosts (https:).
   - Allow-listed CDNs that host known JSONP/bypass gadgets.
   - Missing object-src 'none' and base-uri 'none'.
   - No nonce/hash for inline scripts.
4) Confirm it's actually enforced (not Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only).
items.how_to_fix: Deploy a strict CSP: default-src 'self'; object-src 'none'; base-uri 'none'; frame-ancestors 'self'; and script-src using per-response nonces or hashes (drop 'unsafe-inline'/'unsafe-eval' and wildcards). Prefer 'strict-dynamic' for modern apps. Roll out with report-only + report-uri first, then enforce. VERIFY with CSP Evaluator (no high-severity findings) - note CSP is defense-in-depth, still fix XSS at the source.

items.title: JavaScript Code and Third-Party Libraries
items.description: Test for vulnerabilities in JavaScript code and third-party libraries.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inventory client-side libraries and versions, then check for known CVEs:
   retire.js --outputformat json --js  (or the browser extension)
   npm audit / npx retire on the source.
2) Review custom JS for DOM XSS sinks fed by user input (innerHTML, eval, document.write, setTimeout(string), location) - use LinkFinder to map endpoints and grep the bundle.
3) Check for Subresource Integrity (SRI) on third-party <script>/<link> (integrity= attribute) - missing SRI means a compromised CDN can inject code.
4) Look for secrets/API keys and dead/debug code left in bundles.
5) Check supply-chain risk: are scripts loaded from many third-party origins?
items.how_to_fix: Keep libraries patched (SCA in CI: retire.js, npm audit, Snyk), remove unused/vulnerable ones, and add Subresource Integrity + a strict CSP for third-party scripts. Fix DOM sinks (use safe APIs/textContent, sanitize with DOMPurify). Strip secrets/debug code from prod bundles. VERIFY by re-running retire.js (no known-vulnerable libs).

items.title: Secure Use of Cookies
items.description: Ensure secure use of cookies (HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite attributes).
items.how_to_find:
1) List all cookies and their flags:
   curl -sSI https://target.tld/ | grep -i set-cookie
   DevTools > Application > Cookies (check HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite, Domain, Path, Expiry columns).
2) Flag: session/auth cookies missing HttpOnly (stealable via XSS), missing Secure (leak over HTTP), missing/weak SameSite (CSRF), overly broad Domain (.example.com sent to all subdomains), or non-expiring sensitive cookies.
3) Check for sensitive data stored directly in cookie values (base64-decode them).
4) Prefer the __Host-/__Secure- cookie name prefixes for session cookies.
items.how_to_fix: Set HttpOnly + Secure + SameSite=Lax/Strict on session/auth cookies, scope Domain/Path narrowly, and use the __Host- prefix. Don't store sensitive data in cookie values. Rotate on privilege change and invalidate server-side on logout. VERIFY flags via curl -I / DevTools.

items.title: Local Storage and Session Storage
items.description: Check for sensitive data stored insecurely in local storage or session storage.
items.how_to_find:
1) Open DevTools > Application > Local Storage / Session Storage and inspect stored keys.
2) Red flags: JWTs / access tokens, session identifiers, passwords, PII, or API keys stored there - localStorage/sessionStorage are readable by ANY JavaScript on the page, so a single XSS = full token theft (and localStorage persists).
3) In console run: JSON.stringify(localStorage) to dump it quickly.
4) Check whether tokens in storage survive logout (should be cleared) and whether they're used as bearer tokens (XSS-exfiltratable).
items.how_to_fix: Store session/auth tokens in HttpOnly+Secure+SameSite cookies rather than local/session storage so JavaScript (and XSS) cannot read them. Keep only non-sensitive UI state in web storage, clear it on logout, and fix XSS. VERIFY that no tokens/PII remain in web storage.

items.title: JavaScript Obfuscation
items.description: Ensure that sensitive business logic is not exposed in client-side JavaScript.
items.how_to_find:
1) Beautify the JS (DevTools 'Pretty print {}', or js-beautify) and read it - obfuscation/minification is NOT a security control.
2) Look for sensitive logic that shouldn't be client-side: hardcoded credentials/API keys, pricing/discount logic, feature-flag/authorization checks, hidden admin endpoints, encryption keys.
   grep -iE 'api[_-]?key|secret|password|admin|isAdmin|price|discount|token' app.js
3) Check source maps (.js.map) that reveal original source.
4) Confirm any 'security' enforced only in JS (e.g. client-side auth checks) can be bypassed by calling the API directly.
items.how_to_fix: Never place secrets or trust-sensitive logic (authorization, pricing, limits) in client-side JS - enforce it server-side. Remove source maps and secrets from production bundles. Treat obfuscation as anti-tamper friction only, not protection. VERIFY no secrets/authz logic are recoverable from the client.

items.title: Clickjacking
items.description: Test for clickjacking vulnerabilities and ensure the use of X-Frame-Options or CSP frame-ancestors.
items.how_to_find:
1) Check anti-framing headers:
   curl -sSI https://target.tld/ | grep -iE 'x-frame-options|content-security-policy'
   Missing X-Frame-Options AND no CSP frame-ancestors = framable.
2) Build a PoC page and load the target in an iframe:
   <style>iframe{opacity:0.3;position:absolute}</style><iframe src='https://target.tld/account/settings'></iframe>
   If it renders (not blank/refused), overlay decoy buttons over sensitive actions to demonstrate UI redress.
3) Focus on sensitive state-changing pages (change email, delete, transfer, admin toggles).
items.how_to_fix: Send Content-Security-Policy: frame-ancestors 'self' (or 'none') as the primary defense, plus X-Frame-Options: DENY/SAMEORIGIN for legacy browsers, on all pages (especially sensitive ones). Add re-authentication/confirmation for critical actions. VERIFY the iframe PoC no longer renders the page.

items.title: DOM Manipulation
items.description: Check for insecure DOM manipulation practices that could lead to vulnerabilities.
items.how_to_find: Hunt for DOM-based XSS (client-side sink fed by attacker-controlled source): 1) SOURCES: location.href/.hash/.search, document.URL, document.referrer, window.name, postMessage data. 2) SINKS: element.innerHTML/outerHTML, document.write, eval, setTimeout/setInterval(string), Function(), jQuery $(...).html(), location assignment. 3) Grep the JS: grep -nE 'innerHTML|document.write|eval\(|location\.|\.html\(' app.js 4) Test payloads via the fragment (not sent to server): https://target.tld/# 5) Use the DOM Invader tool in Burp's embedded browser to auto-trace sources to sinks.
items.how_to_fix: Avoid dangerous sinks - use textContent / safe DOM APIs and framework binding instead of innerHTML/document.write; sanitize any HTML with DOMPurify; never pass untrusted data to eval/Function/setTimeout(string). Enable Trusted Types (CSP require-trusted-types-for 'script'). VERIFY the fragment/postMessage payloads no longer execute.

items.title: HTML Injection
items.description: Test for HTML injection vulnerabilities that could compromise the application's integrity.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inject benign HTML into every reflected/stored input and check if it renders as markup rather than text:

test

, test , click , 2) If HTML renders but
items.how_to_fix: HTML-encode all user input on output (< > " ') via framework auto-escaping; if rich HTML is required, sanitize server-side with an allow-list library (OWASP Java HTML Sanitizer, DOMPurify). Add a strict CSP as defense-in-depth. VERIFY injected HTML renders as inert text.

items.title: Secure Event Handling
items.description: Ensure that event handlers are securely implemented to prevent exploitation.
items.how_to_find:
1) Review how event handlers are wired: inline handlers built from user data (onclick=userdata) are XSS-prone.
2) postMessage: search JS for addEventListener('message', ...) and check the handler validates event.origin AND event.data - a missing origin check lets any site send messages (postMessage XSS / data theft). Test by sending window.postMessage from an attacker page.
3) Check for clobbering / prototype-pollution feeding event logic.
4) Look for eval/Function inside handlers processing untrusted data.
items.how_to_fix: Attach handlers via addEventListener (not inline HTML built from input). In postMessage listeners, strictly validate event.origin against an allow-list and validate the message schema before acting. Avoid eval in handlers. VERIFY cross-origin postMessages and injected handler data are rejected/inert.

items.title: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS)
items.description: Verify that CORS policies are correctly implemented to prevent unauthorized data access.
items.how_to_find:
Test the CORS policy on an authenticated API endpoint by sending different Origin headers:
1) Reflected origin + credentials (critical):
   curl -sSI https://target.tld/api/me -H 'Origin: https://evil.tld'
   If the response has Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.tld AND Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true, any site can read the victim's authenticated data.
2) Wildcard: Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * on sensitive endpoints.
3) Weak allow-list logic: Origin: https://target.tld.evil.tld or https://eviltarget.tld (substring/suffix match), and Origin: null (some allow it).
4) Pre-flight: check Access-Control-Allow-Headers/Methods are not overly permissive.
items.how_to_fix: Allow only explicit trusted origins (exact string match against an allow-list); never reflect an arbitrary Origin, never use '*' with Allow-Credentials, and never trust Origin: null. Keep Allow-Methods/Headers minimal. VERIFY by replaying evil/null/substring origins (ACAO must not reflect them).

items.title: JavaScript Sandbox
items.description: Ensure that any potentially unsafe JavaScript execution is contained within a sandbox environment.
items.how_to_find:
Where the app runs untrusted/third-party code or embeds external content:
1) Check iframes embedding third-party/user content have the sandbox attribute and it isn't neutered (sandbox='allow-scripts allow-same-origin' together effectively removes sandboxing).
2) If user-supplied scripts/templates run, confirm they execute in an isolated context (Web Worker, sandboxed iframe with a distinct origin) not the main app origin.
3) Test whether embedded content can access the parent DOM/cookies (breakout).
items.how_to_fix: Isolate untrusted content in a sandboxed iframe on a separate origin with a minimal sandbox allow-list (avoid combining allow-scripts + allow-same-origin), or run in a Web Worker. Apply CSP to the frame. VERIFY embedded content cannot reach the parent origin's DOM/cookies.

items.title: Secure Frameworks
items.description: Check for secure usage of client-side frameworks and libraries (e.g., React, Angular).
items.how_to_find:
1) Identify the framework/version (React/Angular/Vue) and check for known CVEs (retire.js).
2) Look for the framework's XSS escape hatches used with untrusted data:
   React: dangerouslySetInnerHTML
   Vue: v-html
   Angular: bypassSecurityTrust* / [innerHTML]
   grep -nE 'dangerouslySetInnerHTML|v-html|bypassSecurityTrust|innerHTML' src/
3) Check for client-side template injection (AngularJS {{}} sandbox escapes on old versions).
4) Verify SSR frameworks escape data and that auto-escaping isn't disabled.
items.how_to_fix: Rely on the framework's built-in contextual auto-escaping and avoid the escape-hatch APIs with untrusted data (sanitize with DOMPurify if unavoidable). Keep the framework patched, avoid EOL versions (e.g. AngularJS), and enable CSP + Trusted Types. VERIFY escape-hatch sinks are absent or sanitized.

items.title: Client-Side Caching
items.description: Validate that sensitive data is not inadvertently cached on the client-side.
items.how_to_find:
1) Check Cache-Control on authenticated/sensitive responses:
   curl -sSI https://target.tld/account -H 'Cookie: session=...' | grep -iE 'cache-control|pragma|expires'
   Missing no-store means the browser/back-button/shared proxy may retain the sensitive page.
2) Log in on a shared machine, log out, then press Back / reopen from history - is sensitive data still shown?
3) Inspect the browser disk cache for cached sensitive pages/API responses.
4) Check autocomplete on sensitive fields.
items.how_to_fix: Set Cache-Control: no-store (and no-cache, must-revalidate) plus Pragma: no-cache on authenticated/sensitive responses so they aren't cached by the browser or intermediaries. Disable autocomplete on sensitive fields. VERIFY the back-button/history no longer exposes data after logout.

items.title: Client-Side Encryption
items.description: Ensure any sensitive data processed on the client-side is encrypted appropriately.
items.how_to_find:
1) If the app does crypto in JS (E2E messaging, client-side field encryption), review the implementation - client-side crypto is only meaningful with proper key handling and does NOT replace TLS.
2) Red flags: home-grown crypto instead of Web Crypto API (SubtleCrypto), hardcoded keys/IVs in JS, ECB mode, static IV/nonce reuse, keys derived from weak/predictable values, or 'encryption' that's just base64/XOR.
   grep -iE 'CryptoJS|AES|createCipher|btoa|xor|key\s*=' app.js
3) Confirm the server still validates/enforces (client crypto can be bypassed by an attacker controlling the client).
items.how_to_fix: Use TLS for transport (don't rely on client-side crypto for confidentiality in transit). Where client-side/E2E crypto is genuinely needed, use the Web Crypto API (SubtleCrypto) with authenticated modes (AES-GCM), proper random IVs, and secure key exchange/derivation - never hardcode keys in JS. Keep authoritative validation server-side. VERIFY no weak/home-grown crypto or embedded keys.

items.title: Presence and Configuration
items.description: Verify the presence and correct configuration of security headers (e.g., X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Strict-Transport-Security).
items.how_to_find:
1) Dump all response headers and grade them:
   curl -sSI https://target.tld/
   Or use securityheaders.com / Mozilla Observatory for a scored report.
2) Check the core set is present and correct:
   - Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS)
   - Content-Security-Policy
   - X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff
   - X-Frame-Options / CSP frame-ancestors
   - Referrer-Policy
   - Permissions-Policy
3) Also confirm INFO-LEAK headers are removed: Server, X-Powered-By, X-AspNet-Version.
4) Verify headers are set on ALL responses (API, error pages, redirects), not just the homepage.
items.how_to_fix: Set the full security-header baseline centrally at the reverse proxy or app middleware so every response (incl. errors/APIs) gets them, and remove version-disclosing headers. Track with securityheaders.com in CI. VERIFY with curl -I across several endpoints (A grade).

items.title: Referrer Policy
items.description: Ensure that the Referrer-Policy header is configured correctly to minimize information leakage.
items.how_to_find:
1) Check the header:
   curl -sSI https://target.tld/ | grep -i referrer-policy
2) Risk: with no policy (or unsafe-url), the full URL - including any tokens/IDs in the path/query - is sent as the Referer to third parties (analytics, CDNs, external links), leaking sensitive data.
3) Confirm by visiting a page that has sensitive URL params and watching outbound requests' Referer header in DevTools/Burp.
4) Flag weak values: unsafe-url, or missing on pages that carry sensitive URLs.
items.how_to_fix: Set Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin (good default) or no-referrer for the most sensitive apps, so full URLs aren't leaked cross-origin. Also keep secrets out of URLs. VERIFY the Referer sent to third parties no longer contains sensitive data.

items.title: Permissions Policy
items.description: Check the Permissions-Policy header to control which features and APIs can be used in the browser.
items.how_to_find:
1) Check the header:
   curl -sSI https://target.tld/ | grep -i permissions-policy
2) Permissions-Policy (successor to Feature-Policy) controls access to powerful browser features (camera, microphone, geolocation, payment, usb, fullscreen) for the page and its iframes.
3) Flag: header missing (all features allowed by default) or overly permissive (camera=*, geolocation=*), especially if the app embeds third-party iframes that could then abuse features.
items.how_to_fix: Send a restrictive Permissions-Policy that disables unused features, e.g. Permissions-Policy: camera=(), microphone=(), geolocation=(), payment=(), usb=(). Only allow-list origins that genuinely need a feature. VERIFY with curl -I and by testing feature access from an embedded frame (should be blocked).

items.title: Feature Policy
items.description: Verify the implementation of Feature Policy to control the use of web features and APIs.
items.how_to_find:
1) Feature-Policy is the DEPRECATED predecessor of Permissions-Policy - modern browsers use Permissions-Policy.
   curl -sSI https://target.tld/ | grep -iE 'feature-policy|permissions-policy'
2) If the app still only sends Feature-Policy, it won't be honored by current browsers - features go unrestricted.
3) Check that the migration to Permissions-Policy has happened and covers the same feature set.
items.how_to_fix: Migrate from the deprecated Feature-Policy to Permissions-Policy (see the Permissions Policy control) and restrict unused features. You can send both during a transition for old-browser support. VERIFY Permissions-Policy is present and restrictive.

items.title: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS)
items.description: Ensure that CORS headers are properly configured to prevent unauthorized cross-origin requests.
items.how_to_find:
Probe the ACAO/ACAC headers with a spoofed Origin on an authenticated endpoint:
   curl -sSI https://target.tld/api/me -H 'Origin: https://evil.tld'
Critical if the response reflects Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.tld together with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true (any site can read the victim's data). Also test:
- ACAO: * on sensitive endpoints
- Suffix/substring bypass: Origin: https://target.tld.evil.tld
- Origin: null (sandboxed iframe / data: URI)
- Overly broad Access-Control-Allow-Headers / -Methods on the preflight (OPTIONS) response.
items.how_to_fix: Reflect only exact, allow-listed origins (no wildcard-with-credentials, no arbitrary reflection, no null), and keep Allow-Methods/Headers minimal. Validate Origin against a strict list server-side. VERIFY by replaying evil/null/substring origins (not reflected).

items.title: Content-Security-Policy (CSP)
items.description: Verify that CSP is properly configured to prevent XSS and data injection attacks.
items.how_to_find:
1) Read and grade the policy:
   curl -sSI https://target.tld/ | grep -i content-security-policy
   Paste into Google CSP Evaluator (csp-evaluator.withgoogle.com).
2) XSS-bypassable weaknesses to flag: 'unsafe-inline'/'unsafe-eval' in script-src, wildcards (* or https:), missing object-src 'none' and base-uri 'none', allow-listed JSONP-capable CDNs, and no nonce/hash.
3) Confirm it's enforced (header is Content-Security-Policy, not -Report-Only) and present on all HTML responses.
items.how_to_fix: Deploy a strict, nonce/hash-based CSP: default-src 'self'; object-src 'none'; base-uri 'none'; frame-ancestors 'self'; script-src 'nonce-<random>' 'strict-dynamic'. Remove unsafe-inline/unsafe-eval and wildcards. Roll out report-only first. VERIFY with CSP Evaluator (no high findings). CSP is defense-in-depth - still fix XSS at source.

items.title: X-Content-Type-Options
items.description: Ensure that the X-Content-Type-Options header is set to prevent MIME-type sniffing.
items.how_to_find:
1) Check the header:
   curl -sSI https://target.tld/ | grep -i x-content-type-options
   Expected: X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff
2) Without nosniff, browsers may MIME-sniff a response and execute it as a different type - e.g. a user-uploaded 'text' or 'image' file interpreted as HTML/JS (drive-by XSS), or a JSON response treated as script.
3) Especially important on user-content/download endpoints and API responses.
items.how_to_fix: Send X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff on all responses and always set an accurate Content-Type. Serve user downloads with Content-Disposition: attachment. VERIFY with curl -I and by attempting MIME-sniff on an upload/download endpoint (blocked).

items.title: X-XSS-Protection
items.description: Verify that X-XSS-Protection is configured to prevent reflected XSS attacks.
items.how_to_find:
1) Check whether the app relies on X-XSS-Protection:
   curl -sSI https://target.tld/ | grep -i x-xss-protection
2) IMPORTANT: X-XSS-Protection is DEPRECATED - modern browsers (Chrome, Edge, Firefox) removed the XSS Auditor because it caused bugs and info-leak side channels. X-XSS-Protection: 1; mode=block gives little/no protection today and 1 (without block) could even be abused.
3) The real test is for actual XSS (see the XSS control) and CSP quality - don't rely on this header.
items.how_to_fix: Do NOT depend on X-XSS-Protection. Recommended: set X-XSS-Protection: 0 to disable the legacy auditor's quirks, and instead prevent XSS via contextual output encoding + a strict CSP. VERIFY XSS is mitigated by the CSP/encoding, not this header.

items.title: X-Frame-Options
items.description: Ensure that X-Frame-Options is set to prevent clickjacking.
items.how_to_find:
1) Check the header:
   curl -sSI https://target.tld/ | grep -iE 'x-frame-options|content-security-policy'
   Values: DENY (no framing) or SAMEORIGIN (same-origin only). Missing = framable -> clickjacking.
2) Note ALLOW-FROM is obsolete/unsupported; CSP frame-ancestors is the modern replacement.
3) PoC: load the page in an iframe from another origin (see the Clickjacking control) and confirm it renders.
4) Check sensitive/state-changing pages specifically.
items.how_to_fix: Set Content-Security-Policy: frame-ancestors 'self' (or 'none') as the primary control, plus X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN/DENY for legacy browsers, on all pages. VERIFY the cross-origin iframe PoC no longer renders.

items.title: Public-Key-Pins (HPKP)
items.description: Check for the use of HTTP Public-Key-Pinning to mitigate man-in-the-middle attacks.
items.how_to_find:
1) HPKP (Public-Key-Pins) is DEPRECATED and removed from modern browsers - it caused widespread outages and 'HPKP suicide'/ransom risks, so it is NOT recommended.
   curl -sSI https://target.tld/ | grep -i public-key-pins
2) If the app still sends Public-Key-Pins, it's ignored by current browsers and can be a maintenance foot-gun. Its presence is a sign of outdated hardening guidance.
3) The modern equivalent goals are met by HSTS + Certificate Transparency monitoring (and CAA DNS records).
items.how_to_fix: Remove the deprecated Public-Key-Pins header. Instead enforce HSTS, monitor Certificate Transparency logs for mis-issued certs, and set a CAA DNS record to restrict which CAs may issue for your domain. For app-to-server pinning (mobile/native), pin at the client instead. VERIFY HPKP is gone and HSTS/CT/CAA are in place.

items.title: Rate Limiting and Throttling
items.description: Test for API rate limiting and throttling.
items.how_to_find:
(OWASP API4: Unrestricted Resource Consumption)
1) Fire a burst at an endpoint and watch for 429 / Retry-After:
   for i in $(seq 1 200); do curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}\n' https://target.tld/api/login -d '{"u":"a"}' -H 'Content-Type: application/json'; done | sort | uniq -c
   or: ffuf -w /dev/null -u https://target.tld/api/otp -X POST -rate 0 (max speed) and observe.
2) Focus on expensive/abusable endpoints: login, OTP verify, password reset, search, export, file generation, and any that fan out to email/SMS (cost abuse).
3) Test rate-limit bypasses: rotate X-Forwarded-For / X-Real-IP, change casing/trailing slash on the path, switch API version (/v1 vs /v2), or use different valid API keys.
4) Check for missing pagination limits (?limit=1000000) causing resource exhaustion.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce server-side rate limiting/throttling per user, per API key, and per IP (token-bucket) with 429 + Retry-After, and stricter limits on auth/OTP/reset. Cap pagination page size and query complexity, and determine client identity from trusted signals (not spoofable headers). Add quotas and alerting on spikes. VERIFY by re-running the burst (throttled) and header-spoof bypasses (still throttled).

items.title: Authentication and Authorization
items.description: Assess for improper API authentication and authorization.
items.how_to_find:
(OWASP API1 BOLA, API2 Broken Auth, API5 BFLA)
1) AuthN: remove/blank the Authorization header or cookie and replay - does the endpoint still return data? (missing auth). Test expired/forged tokens.
   curl -i https://target.tld/api/v1/orders   (no token)
2) BOLA (object-level): with user A's token, swap an object id to user B's:
   curl -i https://target.tld/api/v1/orders/1002 -H 'Authorization: Bearer A_TOKEN'
3) BFLA (function-level): call admin-only methods/endpoints as a normal user (POST /api/v1/users, DELETE /api/v1/users/5, or change GET->PUT/DELETE).
4) Automate authz matrix with the Burp Autorize extension (browse as admin, replay with low-priv token).
5) Test alternate auth: does a valid-but-wrong-audience JWT or another tenant's key work?
items.how_to_fix: Require authentication on every non-public endpoint and enforce object-level AND function-level authorization server-side (verify the caller owns/may act on the resource; deny by default). Centralize checks, derive identity from the verified token, and add automated authz tests. VERIFY by replaying no-token, cross-object, and low-priv admin-call tests (all denied).

items.title: Data Exposure
items.description: Check for data exposure through API responses.
items.how_to_find:
(OWASP API3: Broken Object Property Level Authorization / excessive data exposure)
1) Compare the API response to what the UI actually shows - APIs often return the whole object and rely on the client to hide fields:
   curl -s https://target.tld/api/v1/users/me -H 'Authorization: Bearer TOKEN' | jq
   Look for password hashes, tokens, internal flags (is_admin), other users' PII, full card/SSN.
2) Check list/search endpoints returning more per-record data than needed.
3) Test debug/verbose params (?fields=*, ?expand=all, ?include=secret).
4) GraphQL: introspect and over-fetch nested/sensitive fields.
items.how_to_fix: Return only the fields required per use case using explicit response schemas/DTOs (allow-list, not the raw model); never rely on the client to hide data. Apply per-property authorization for sensitive fields. For GraphQL, restrict field-level access. VERIFY responses contain no fields beyond the allow-list.

items.title: Input Validation
items.description: Ensure proper input validation and sanitization in API endpoints.
items.how_to_find:
1) Enumerate the schema (Swagger/OpenAPI /swagger.json, /openapi.json, GraphQL introspection) and fuzz each parameter with type/format/range violations and injection payloads:
   - Wrong types (string where int expected), oversized arrays/strings, negative numbers, null, extra fields.
   - Injection: SQLi ('), NoSQLi ({"$ne":null}), command (;id), SSTI ({{7*7}}) in JSON values.
   Automate with Burp Intruder, or an API fuzzer (e.g. schemathesis run openapi.json --checks all).
2) Test content-type confusion (send XML/urlencoded to a JSON endpoint) and JSON depth/array bombs.
3) Confirm validation is server-side (bypass any client checks via direct requests).
items.how_to_fix: Validate every request against a strict server-side schema (JSON Schema / OpenAPI request validation): type, format, length, range, enum, required, and reject unknown fields. Use parameterized queries and context-safe handling for values that reach sinks. Enforce content-type and size limits. VERIFY by re-fuzzing (malformed/injection inputs return 400).

items.title: CORS Configuration
items.description: Verify that Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) is properly configured to prevent unauthorized access.
items.how_to_find:
Send a spoofed Origin to an authenticated API endpoint and inspect the ACAO/ACAC headers:
   curl -sSI https://target.tld/api/v1/me -H 'Origin: https://evil.tld'
CRITICAL if it reflects Access-Control-Allow-Origin: https://evil.tld with Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true (any site reads the victim's data). Also test:
- ACAO: * on authenticated endpoints
- Substring/suffix allow-list bypass: Origin: https://target.tld.evil.tld
- Origin: null
- Preflight (OPTIONS) returning permissive Allow-Methods/Headers.
Build a fetch() PoC with credentials:'include' to prove data theft.
items.how_to_fix: Allow only exact, allow-listed origins (server-side validation), never reflect arbitrary Origin, never combine '*' with credentials, and reject Origin: null. Keep Allow-Methods/Headers minimal. VERIFY by replaying evil/null/substring origins (not reflected) and the fetch PoC (blocked).

items.title: API Key Management
items.description: Check for secure management and storage of API keys.
items.how_to_find:
1) Hunt for leaked API keys in client code, repos, and history:
   grep -riE 'api[_-]?key|x-api-key|sk_live|AKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}' ./
   trufflehog / gitleaks on the repo; check the JS bundle and mobile app for embedded keys.
2) Check key handling: sent in the URL (?api_key=, logged/cached) vs header; long-lived and never rotated; single shared key for all clients; no scope/least-privilege.
3) Test whether a leaked/low-priv key can access more than intended, and whether keys are validated server-side on every call.
4) Confirm keys can be revoked and are rate-limited per key.
items.how_to_fix: Treat API keys as secrets: send in headers (not URLs), store server-side/in a secret manager, scope least-privilege, and support rotation + revocation + per-key rate limits. Never embed secret keys in client/mobile code (proxy calls server-side). Rotate any leaked keys. VERIFY no keys in code/URLs/logs and that revocation works.

items.title: Versioning
items.description: Ensure that API versioning is implemented to manage changes and deprecations securely.
items.how_to_find:
(OWASP API9: Improper Inventory Management)
1) Enumerate API versions and forgotten/beta endpoints - old versions often lack current security fixes:
   ffuf -w versions.txt -u https://target.tld/api/FUZZ/users   (v1,v2,v3,beta,internal,old,test)
   Try /api/v1 while the app uses /api/v3; check host/subdomain variants (api-dev, staging).
2) Compare an action across versions - a fix/authz check present in v3 may be missing in v1.
3) Look for undocumented endpoints in JS, mobile traffic, and the OpenAPI spec's deprecated paths.
items.how_to_fix: Maintain an accurate API inventory, deprecate and DECOMMISSION old versions (don't just hide them), and apply the same security controls to every live version. Gate non-prod/internal APIs behind network controls. VERIFY old versions are removed or equally hardened.

items.title: Error Handling
items.description: Verify that API error messages do not reveal sensitive information.
items.how_to_find:
1) Force errors and read the JSON/body: send malformed JSON, wrong types, missing fields, unsupported methods, and hit non-existent IDs:
   curl -i https://target.tld/api/v1/users/@@@ -H 'Authorization: Bearer TOKEN'
2) Look for stack traces, exception class names, SQL fragments, ORM details, internal file paths, hostnames, and framework/version info in error responses.
3) Check that error detail doesn't differ in a way that enables enumeration (404 vs 403 vs 200 leaking existence).
4) Confirm 5xx errors don't dump debug info in prod.
items.how_to_fix: Return generic, structured error responses (code + safe message + correlation id) and log full details server-side only. Map exceptions to sanitized responses, disable debug/verbose errors in prod, and keep status codes consistent to avoid enumeration. VERIFY error responses contain no stack traces/internal detail.

items.title: Parameter Tampering
items.description: Test for vulnerabilities where API parameters can be tampered with to achieve unintended effects.
items.how_to_find:
In Burp Repeater, tamper server-owned parameters in the JSON body / query and observe:
1) Identity/authorization: user_id, account_id, role, is_admin, tenant_id -> set to another user's / a higher value.
2) Business values: price, amount, quantity (negative/zero), discount, currency, status.
3) Control flags: verified=true, approved=true, skip_payment=true.
4) IDs for IDOR (see BOLA). Also try type juggling ("1" vs 1, arrays) and JSON parameter pollution (duplicate keys).
Example: {"productId":123,"price":0.01,"role":"admin"}
items.how_to_fix: Never trust client-supplied values for identity, authorization, pricing, or state - derive them server-side (session/DB) and ignore/deny client attempts to set them. Bind actions to the authenticated principal and recompute sensitive values. Validate types strictly. VERIFY tampered identity/price/flag values are ignored or rejected.

items.title: Mass Assignment
items.description: Ensure that APIs do not accept and process unexpected parameters that could lead to mass assignment vulnerabilities.
items.how_to_find:
(OWASP API6 / Mass Assignment)
1) Take a normal update/create request and ADD extra properties that map to internal model fields:
   PATCH /api/v1/users/me  {"name":"x","role":"admin","isAdmin":true,"emailVerified":true,"balance":999999,"account_id":5}
2) Discover field names from GET responses, the OpenAPI/GraphQL schema, or by guessing (is_admin, verified, credits, owner_id).
3) Re-read the object (GET) to confirm the injected field stuck (privilege escalation / data tampering).
4) Test nested objects and arrays too.
items.how_to_fix: Bind only an explicit allow-list of client-editable fields (DTO / strong parameters / schema with additionalProperties:false); never bind request bodies straight to ORM models. Set sensitive fields server-side only. VERIFY that injecting extra fields (role/isAdmin/balance) has no effect.

items.title: JSON Web Token (JWT)
items.description: Verify the secure implementation and storage of JWTs, including proper signing and expiration.
items.how_to_find:
Decode the token (jwt.io, or: echo TOKEN | cut -d. -f2 | base64 -d) and attack the signature validation. Use the Burp 'JWT Editor' extension or jwt_tool:
1) alg:none - set header {"alg":"none"}, strip the signature: jwt_tool TOKEN -X a
2) alg confusion RS256->HS256 - sign with the public key as the HMAC secret: jwt_tool TOKEN -X k -pk public.pem
3) Weak HMAC secret - crack it: hashcat -m 16500 token.txt rockyou.txt (or jwt_tool -C -d rockyou.txt)
4) Claim tampering - change sub/role/admin and re-sign if signature is broken.
5) kid injection (path traversal/SQLi in kid), jku/x5u pointing to attacker JWKS.
6) No exp / not-checked expiry, and token reuse after logout.
items.how_to_fix: Pin the expected algorithm (reject 'none' and unexpected algs), verify signatures with a strong secret/proper keys (never accept public key as HMAC secret), and use a long random HMAC secret. Validate exp/nbf/iat, iss, aud; support revocation (short-lived tokens + denylist). Sanitize kid and don't fetch keys from client-controlled jku/x5u. VERIFY jwt_tool attacks all fail.

items.title: Input Whitelisting
items.description: Ensure APIs use input whitelisting to accept only known and expected inputs.
items.how_to_find:
1) Test whether inputs are allow-listed (accept only known-good) vs block-listed (reject known-bad, easily bypassed):
   - Send unexpected enum/status values, extra fields, and encoded/obfuscated payloads.
   - If a field expects e.g. status in {open,closed}, try status=deleted / status=admin.
2) Probe format fields (email, phone, uuid, date) with malformed values to see if only a strict pattern passes.
3) Confirm unknown JSON properties are rejected (ties to mass assignment).
items.how_to_fix: Use allow-list validation everywhere: strict enums, regex/format validators, numeric ranges, and reject unknown fields (additionalProperties:false). Prefer allow-lists over blacklists for anything reaching a sink. VERIFY unexpected values/fields are rejected with 400.

items.title: Data Filtering
items.description: Verify that sensitive data is properly filtered out of API responses.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inspect responses for sensitive fields that should be filtered out:
   curl -s https://target.tld/api/v1/users | jq '.[0] | keys'
   Look for password/hash, tokens, ssn, internal notes, is_admin, other-tenant data.
2) Test filter-bypass params: ?fields=, ?select=*, ?expand=, GraphQL nested selection, and admin-only fields returned to normal users.
3) Check error and list endpoints (often leak more than detail endpoints), and exports/CSV.
4) Confirm filtering is server-side, not just hidden by the UI.
items.how_to_fix: Filter/serialize responses server-side with explicit output schemas (allow-list fields) and apply per-role/per-property authorization; strip sensitive/internal fields before sending. For GraphQL, enforce field-level authorization. VERIFY no sensitive fields appear for unauthorized roles or via filter params.

items.title: API Gateway Security
items.description: Check the security configurations of API gateways and their role in protecting backend services.
items.how_to_find:
1) Try to reach backend services directly, bypassing the gateway (which enforces auth/rate limits/WAF):
   - Find backend hostnames/IPs (DNS, TLS SANs, error leaks, SSRF) and hit them directly.
   - Test internal ports and the origin behind a CDN (e.g. via historical DNS / Shodan).
2) Check gateway trust: does the backend trust gateway-set headers (X-User, X-Authenticated, X-Forwarded-For) that you can spoof if you reach it directly?
3) Look for path/route confusion where gateway auth applies to /api but not /internal or a differently-cased path.
4) Confirm the gateway actually terminates TLS, enforces authn/z, rate limits, and strips client-supplied trust headers.
items.how_to_fix: Ensure backends are not directly reachable (private network / mTLS / IP allow-list) and never trust gateway-injected identity headers unless the connection is authenticated (mTLS) and inbound copies are stripped. Enforce authn/z, rate limiting, and schema validation at the gateway AND defense-in-depth in services. VERIFY the backend rejects direct/spoofed-header requests.

items.title: OAuth/OpenID Connect
items.description: Verify secure implementation of OAuth and OpenID Connect for authentication and authorization.
items.how_to_find:
Map the flow (authorization_code, implicit, PKCE) and test classic OAuth/OIDC issues:
1) redirect_uri validation: change it to an attacker domain, or use path/subdomain/open-redirect tricks (redirect_uri=https://attacker.tld, https://target.tld.attacker.tld, //attacker.tld) to steal the code/token.
2) state parameter missing/not validated -> CSRF on the callback (account linking takeover).
3) PKCE missing on public clients (code interception).
4) Token leakage via Referer (implicit flow token in URL fragment) or logs.
5) id_token validation: check signature, iss, aud, exp, nonce; try alg:none / key confusion (see JWT).
6) Scope/consent: request extra scopes; test whether access tokens are accepted by the wrong audience/resource.
7) Authorization code reuse / not single-use.
items.how_to_fix: Strictly allow-list exact redirect_uris (no wildcards/substring), require and validate state (CSRF) and nonce (replay), enforce PKCE for all clients, and keep tokens out of URLs (use authorization_code, not implicit). Validate id_token signature/iss/aud/exp, make auth codes single-use and short-lived, and scope tokens to the correct audience. VERIFY redirect/state/PKCE/token-audience tests all fail for the attacker.

items.title: Log Sensitive Actions
items.description: Ensure that sensitive actions (e.g., login attempts, data modifications) are logged.
items.how_to_find:
(OWASP A09: Security Logging & Monitoring Failures - often assessed by review + a controlled attack)
1) Perform security-relevant actions and confirm they are logged with who/what/when/where: failed & successful logins, password/email change, MFA changes, privilege changes, data export/delete, admin actions, payment.
2) Black-box signal: run an attack (brute force, IDOR sweep, SQLi probes) and ask the blue team whether it generated alerts - silence = insufficient logging.
3) Check that logs capture source IP, user id, timestamp (UTC), action, and result - and that they DON'T store secrets/passwords/tokens/full PAN (log injection risk too: try newline/ANSI in a username to forge log lines).
4) Review that logs exist for auth, access-control failures, and input-validation failures.
items.how_to_fix: Log all security-significant events with consistent, structured fields (timestamp UTC, actor, source IP, action, outcome) - never log credentials/tokens/PII/PAN. Neutralize log injection (encode CR/LF/control chars). Ensure access-control and validation failures are logged and fed to alerting. VERIFY by performing each sensitive action and confirming a corresponding, sanitized log entry.

items.title: Log Integrity
items.description: Verify that logs are protected from tampering and unauthorized access.
items.how_to_find:
1) Determine whether logs can be tampered with or read by attackers: are log files in the web root / world-readable (try fetching /logs/, /app.log)? Can an app/DB compromise also edit the logs (same host, same creds)?
2) Check whether the app account can delete/modify logs (it shouldn't) and whether logs are shipped off-host quickly (attackers wipe local logs).
3) Look for log injection/forgery: inject CR/LF or fake entries via user-controlled fields that get logged.
4) Confirm integrity controls: append-only/WORM storage, hashing/signing, or a central store the app can only write to.
items.how_to_fix: Ship logs to a separate, append-only/WORM central store that the application/host cannot modify or delete; restrict read access (least privilege) and consider signing/hash-chaining for tamper-evidence. Keep logs out of the web root and sanitize inputs to prevent forgery. VERIFY logs can't be read/altered from an app-level compromise.

items.title: Centralized Logging
items.description: Check for the implementation of centralized logging for comprehensive monitoring.
items.how_to_find:
1) Check whether logs from all tiers (web, app, DB, WAF, auth, cloud/CloudTrail) are aggregated into one system (ELK/Splunk/Loki/SIEM) or scattered per-host (hard to correlate an attack).
2) Ask: can an incident be reconstructed across services from a single place with a shared correlation/request id?
3) Verify time synchronization (NTP) and consistent UTC timestamps across sources - skewed clocks break correlation.
4) Confirm log shipping is reliable and secured (TLS) and that a single host compromise doesn't lose the evidence.
items.how_to_fix: Aggregate all tiers' logs into a centralized, access-controlled platform over TLS, with a shared correlation id and NTP-synced UTC timestamps for cross-service tracing. Ensure durable, near-real-time shipping. VERIFY you can reconstruct a test attack end-to-end from the central store.

items.title: Real-Time Monitoring
items.description: Ensure that real-time monitoring and alerting are in place for security incidents.
items.how_to_find:
1) Generate noisy attack traffic (credential stuffing, forced-browsing 403 storm, SQLi/XSS probes, sudden data export) and measure whether it triggers real-time alerts and how fast.
2) Check for alerting on key signals: spikes in auth failures, 401/403/5xx rates, new-country/impossible-travel logins, privilege changes, WAF blocks.
3) Confirm alerts route to a monitored channel (on-call/SIEM) with sane thresholds (not so noisy they're ignored, not so lax they never fire).
4) Verify dashboards/health checks exist for availability and security KPIs.
items.how_to_fix: Implement real-time detection with tuned alert rules on auth failures, access-control violations, anomalous rates, and sensitive actions, routed to an on-call/SIEM workflow with defined response SLAs. Reduce noise to avoid alert fatigue. VERIFY by replaying the attack traffic and confirming timely alerts.

items.title: Log Retention Policies
items.description: Verify that log retention policies comply with regulatory and business requirements.
items.how_to_fix: Define and enforce retention aligned to compliance (e.g. PCI-DSS: retain audit logs 1 year with 3 months readily available; GDPR: don't keep PII in logs longer than necessary). Automate archival and secure deletion, protect archives (encryption/WORM), and document the policy. VERIFY retention and deletion actually run as configured.
items.how_to_find:
1) Review the documented retention period vs what's actually stored - too short loses forensic evidence after a breach (attackers often dwell for months); too long (esp. with PII) creates privacy/compliance risk.
2) Check regulatory drivers: PCI-DSS (>=1 year for audit trails), GDPR (data minimization/retention limits on PII in logs).
3) Confirm old logs are securely archived and then securely deleted (not just left around), and that archives are protected.
4) Verify logs survive long enough to investigate slow-burn incidents.

items.title: Anomaly Detection
items.description: Implement and verify anomaly detection to identify suspicious activities.
items.how_to_find:
1) Test whether abnormal behavior is detected: impossible-travel logins, sudden bulk data access/export, access at unusual hours, a normal user hitting admin endpoints, or a spike in unique object IDs (IDOR sweep).
2) Simulate account takeover patterns (new device + new geo + password change) and see if it's flagged.
3) Check for baseline/behavioral analytics (UEBA) vs only static thresholds.
4) Confirm anomalies create actionable alerts, not just dashboards nobody watches.
items.how_to_fix: Deploy anomaly/behavioral detection (rate baselines + UEBA) for authentication, data access, and privilege use, with alerts on deviations (impossible travel, bulk export, enumeration). Tune to the app's normal patterns. VERIFY simulated anomalous behavior is detected and alerted.

items.title: Security Event Management
items.description: Ensure integration with Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) systems.
items.how_to_find:
1) Confirm logs/events are integrated into a SIEM (Splunk, Sentinel, Elastic SIEM, Chronicle) with correlation rules, not just stored raw.
2) Check coverage: are app, auth, WAF, cloud, and endpoint sources all feeding the SIEM? Gaps = blind spots.
3) Verify correlation/use-cases exist (e.g. brute force followed by success = takeover) and map to detection frameworks (MITRE ATT&CK).
4) Confirm SIEM alerts drive an incident-response workflow with ownership and SLAs.
items.how_to_fix: Integrate all relevant sources into a SIEM with correlation rules mapped to real attack scenarios (MITRE ATT&CK), and connect alerts to an IR runbook with clear ownership/SLAs. Regularly review coverage and tune rules. VERIFY a multi-step test attack raises a correlated SIEM alert.

items.title: Compliance Logging
items.description: Validate that logging complies with industry standards and regulations (e.g., PCI-DSS, GDPR).
items.how_to_find:
1) Map required audit events to the applicable standard: PCI-DSS Req.10 (log all access to cardholder data, admin actions, auth events, with specific fields + time sync + 1yr retention); GDPR/HIPAA (audit access to personal/health data).
2) Verify the mandated fields are captured (user id, event type, date/time, success/failure, origin, affected data/resource).
3) Confirm logs themselves don't violate compliance (no full PAN, no plaintext secrets/PII beyond what's allowed).
4) Check that audit logs are protected, retained for the required period, and reviewable for audits.
items.how_to_fix: Implement logging that meets the specific standard's audit requirements (event coverage, mandated fields, time sync, protection, and retention), while ensuring logs don't store prohibited data (mask PAN/secrets/PII). Keep an audit-ready, access-controlled trail. VERIFY against the standard's checklist (e.g. PCI-DSS Req.10).

items.title: Strong Encryption Algorithms
items.description: Ensure the use of strong, industry-standard encryption algorithms (e.g., AES-256).
items.how_to_find:
(OWASP A02: Cryptographic Failures)
1) TRANSPORT: enumerate negotiated ciphers/protocols:
   testssl.sh https://target.tld ; nmap --script ssl-enum-ciphers -p 443 target.tld
   Flag weak/broken: DES/3DES, RC4, EXPORT, NULL, MD5/SHA1 signatures, <2048-bit RSA/DH.
2) AT REST / APP (code review or observed data): look for weak algorithms and modes:
   grep -riE 'DES|RC4|MD5|SHA1|ECB|createCipher\(|Random\(' src/
   Red flags: MD5/SHA1 for passwords, AES-ECB (identical plaintext blocks -> identical ciphertext), home-grown/XOR 'encryption', hardcoded IVs.
3) Passwords specifically: are they hashed with bcrypt/scrypt/Argon2 (good) or fast/broken hashes (MD5/SHA1/unsalted)? (see Password Storage).
4) Confirm sensitive fields use authenticated encryption (AES-GCM/ChaCha20-Poly1305).
items.how_to_fix: Use modern, standard algorithms: AES-256-GCM or ChaCha20-Poly1305 for symmetric encryption (authenticated modes, random IV/nonce per message), RSA-2048+/ECDSA P-256 or better for asymmetric, SHA-256+ for hashing, and Argon2id/bcrypt/scrypt for passwords. Remove DES/3DES/RC4/MD5/SHA1/ECB and any custom crypto. VERIFY with testssl.sh (transport) and code review (no weak primitives).

items.title: Secure Key Management
items.description: Verify that encryption keys are managed and stored securely.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find keys/secrets embedded in code, config, images, or client bundles:
   gitleaks detect --source . ; trufflehog git file://.
   grep -riE 'BEGIN (RSA|EC|OPENSSH|PRIVATE) KEY|-----BEGIN|aws_secret|private_key' .
2) Check storage: keys in plaintext env/config files or the repo (weak) vs a KMS/HSM/Vault (good).
3) Look for lifecycle gaps: one static key never rotated, the same key across dev/stage/prod, keys in backups/logs, and no revocation path.
4) Check access: who/what can read the key (file perms, IAM policy) - is it least-privilege?
items.how_to_fix: Store keys in a dedicated KMS/HSM or secrets manager (AWS KMS, HashiCorp Vault, GCP KMS); never in source/images/client code. Separate keys per environment, enforce least-privilege access, enable automated rotation, and audit usage. Purge and rotate any leaked keys. VERIFY with gitleaks/trufflehog (clean) and that runtime pulls keys from the vault.

items.title: Certificate Management
items.description: Check for the proper management and rotation of SSL/TLS certificates.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inspect the certificate and chain:
   echo | openssl s_client -connect target.tld:443 -servername target.tld 2>/dev/null | openssl x509 -noout -issuer -subject -dates -ext subjectAltName
2) Flag: expired or near-expiry certs, self-signed / untrusted issuer, hostname/SAN mismatch, weak signature (SHA1), <2048-bit key, or a wildcard shared too broadly.
3) Check ALL hostnames/subdomains and non-443 services (mail, APIs) - expiry often bites forgotten endpoints.
4) Confirm renewal is automated (ACME/Let's Encrypt) and monitored; check Certificate Transparency logs (crt.sh) for unexpected certs.
items.how_to_fix: Use CA-signed certs with 2048-bit+ keys and SHA-256 signatures, matching SANs, and AUTOMATE renewal (ACME) with expiry monitoring/alerting across all endpoints. Protect private keys (KMS/HSM, 600 perms), enable OCSP stapling, and monitor CT logs + set CAA records. VERIFY with openssl s_client (valid, not near expiry).

items.title: TLS Configuration
items.description: Ensure that TLS is properly configured to prevent vulnerabilities like POODLE and BEAST.
items.how_to_find:
1) Full TLS audit:
   testssl.sh https://target.tld   (or SSL Labs, sslscan target.tld:443)
2) Check for known protocol/cipher attacks:
   - POODLE (SSLv3), BEAST (TLS1.0+CBC), CRIME/BREACH (compression), Sweet32 (3DES), FREAK/Logjam (EXPORT/weak DH), Heartbleed (OpenSSL CVE-2014-0160), ROBOT (RSA).
   testssl.sh flags these directly.
